messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000048

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The MenTeR methodology implements a simplified multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design that decomposes tasks into stages: specification understanding, document search, circuit netlist design, and test-bench generation. It uses a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that "integrates domain-specific knowledge with the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) technique," and employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)/DA-RAG for sourcing technical documents and textbooks to inform design decisions. A Meta Agent serves as a central orchestrator to analyze incoming design tasks, determine required specialist agents, decompose problems into sub-tasks, delegate to domain specialist agents, and oversee the workflow. The PI (Primary Investigator) agent interfaces with users, manages workflow, leverages DA-RAG to process requirements from multiple knowledge sources, and converts abstract requirements into well-defined specifications. The framework is intended to systematically collect reasoning paths (CoT) to enable future domain-adapted fine-tuning. Reported results indicate the multi-agent approach "already outperformed single-agent work" in their comparisons, and the authors note the framework can be further optimized for integration into existing design flows.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 1-2'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes MenTeR, a fully automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design. MenTeR composes multiple specialized AI agents that collaboratively handle specification understanding, document retrieval, testbench generation, schematic/netlist synthesis, and device sizing. The framework includes interactive checkpoints for optional human intervention and targets generation of single-block, multi-block, and ultimately system-level designs (e.g., BGR, PLL). Key methodological components to improve LLM reasoning and reliability are Diagram-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation (DA-RAG), self-referential techniques, and a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that combines domain-specific knowledge with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. The authors also leverage prompting strategies (e.g., references to Tree of Thoughts and RAG) and integrate literature analysis, mathematical reasoning, and device sizing agents to scale from simplified tasks to more complex amplifier and system designs. The excerpt claims MenTeR accelerates design cycle time, broadens the explored design space, and reduces manual trial-and-error, but it contains no quantitative results or detailed optimization metrics in the provided pages.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt reports MenTeR's multi-agent workflow performance, backbone comparisons, and engineering mitigations. Table III shows backbone results (GPT-4o: 84.2/89.2 Pass@1/5; GPT-4.1: 77.5/85.8). In a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) case study, AI-generated nets sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet "contained errors upon schematic inspection," primarily due to "incorrectly defined subcircuits (Frequency Detector (FD), Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO))" and "missing wire connections (VCO to FD, FD to Phase Frequency Detector (PFD), and PFD to the reference frequency source)." The authors recommend "maintaining a 'human-in-the-loop' approach" for complex system-level designs. To improve robustness, MenTeR incorporated "a self-referential technique into both multi-agent and MenTeR, ensuring all code is executable before submission," which "substantially decreased execution error rates." They also addressed "Repetitive Error Loops" by adding "an early-detection mechanism to identify such recurring errors, automatically terminating the chat when these patterns are detected." Token limits were handled by truncating chat history per agent (keeping the initial prompt and last message). On token efficiency, "MenTeR consumes more tokens for the relatively simple Task 1," but for harder tasks the single-agent approach used far more tokens and failed, indicating MenTeR's multi-agent design improved stability and task success on challenging problems.


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

MenTeR is a multi-agent, end-to-end workflow for RF/analog netlist design that decomposes user requirements into assigned tasks (PI agent) and uses specialized agents for design, validation, and knowledge reuse. The Circuit Agent (CA) acts as a senior analog designer producing PySpice netlists via an internal Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning component and an Executor for syntax and basic error checks. CoS breaks design into sequential stages (requirement extraction, parameter synthesis, etc.), leverages a Circuit Think Tank (CTT) repository of validated designs and reasoning traces, and iteratively refines designs. Validated netlists are passed to the Testbench Agent (TBA), which—using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG)—defines simulations (DC sweeps, MOSFET/connection checks), generates Python code to run PySpice, and returns detailed errors for targeted refinement. All generated solutions are inspected with electrical rule checks and circuit simulations. Sub-agents (e.g., CoS Reasoning, DA-RAG) enable stagewise optimization and diagram-to-text retrieval. Evaluation uses pass@k metrics (Fig. 6) with limited attempts and GPT-4o for experiments, indicating a comparative performance analysis versus a single-agent baseline.

## pqac-00000056

From text 'poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2'.

Souradip Poddar, Ahmet Budak, Linran Zhao, Chen-Hao Hsu, Supriyo Maji, Keren Zhu, Yaoyao Jia, and David Z. Pan. A data-driven analog circuit synthesizer with automatic topology selection and sizing. 2024 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1-6, Mar 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

Poddar et al. (DATE 2024) present a data-driven analog circuit synthesizer that performs automatic topology selection and device sizing. The method uses a modular approach to build a comprehensive, parameterized circuit topology library and constructs an adaptive topology dataset rather than relying on an exhaustive precomputed set. A one-time data collection effort gathers simulation-based performance data for each topology; this dataset is then enriched with synthetic performance data generated using variational autoencoders (VAE) to improve coverage and density. For device sizing and performance data generation the authors employ automatic analog sizers and leverage DNN-Opt. The workflow ranks candidate topologies from the repository for a target specification, verifies the chosen topology, and performs automatic sizing and re-optimization. The approach is validated on operational amplifiers (OPAMPs), with experiments involving over 360 OPAMP topologies and over 540K data points, guided by a Figure of Merit (FOM). The paper reports that the system "can generate optimal-sized topology netlists in approximately 10 minutes," emphasizing scalability and reduced runtime compared to simulation-in-the-loop methods.

## pqac-00000057

From text 'poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 2-3'.

URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

The paper describes a data-driven flow for analog (OTA/OPAMP) synthesis that combines simulation-based sizing with VAE-generated synthetic performance data. They use DNN-Opt, a multi-threaded, online, learning-based optimizer (actor-critic + population methods) to size devices and build a simulation dataset. Designs are evaluated with a Figure-of-Merit (FoM) that combines an objective f0(x) and clipped constraint terms (FoM formula provided). The overall pipeline is two-stage: (1) performance dataset generation — a one-time effort that creates a parameterized topology library and a data repository per topology using DNN-Opt simulations augmented with VAE synthetic samples; and (2) topology selection, verification and automatic sizing leveraging that repository. The work focuses on OPAMPs/OTAs and enumerates a set of performance metrics used for queries. Topologies are generated modularly: common analog circuits are decomposed into parameterized modules (e.g., differential pairs, loads, tail current, bias, compensation) where MOSFET sizes act as parameters. VAEs are used as the generative model, trained using the ELBO, to enrich scarce simulation data with synthetic performance points.

Poddar et al. (2024) present a data-driven analog synthesis flow that combines automated topology selection with device sizing. They introduce a constraint-based modular approach to create parameterized analog topology libraries from scratch, then collect simulation-based performance data and VAE-generated synthetic data in a one-time effort to capture each topology’s capabilities. The VAE (variational autoencoder) is used to generate synthetic samples that augment sparse simulation data, improving ranking and sizing. The method pairs binary search with synthetic-data-driven ranking, verification, and an initial data-driven sizing strategy, enabling production of optimal-sized topology netlists in minutes (e.g., a netlist in 6.87 minutes for a test case). The authors validated the approach across 360+ diverse OPAMP topologies and compared results in Table III: in several test cases binary search fails when nearby data are scarce, while the synthetic-data approach better identifies, ranks, and sizes topologies; in at least one case (test case 4) binary search outperformed the synthetic approach. The work shows VAE-augmented data and binary search are complementary for fast topology selection and sizing.

The paper describes an automated, modular OPAMP synthesis flow that uses SKILL automation to quickly build a parameterized library of standard topologies ("initial foundational library comprising approximately 4,000 valid standard parameterized OPAMP topologies"). Designers curated a representative subset of ~360 topologies for study. Each topology is sized using an automatic analog sizer (DNN-Opt) to obtain SPICE-based performance metrics; the authors limited simulations to ~1,000 data points per topology and used six objective/constraint configurations to increase diversity. They apply a constraint-based valid-topology filter (e.g., "merging an NMOS-input pair with a PMOS tail current would generate an invalid topology") to prune incompatible combinations. Two topology-selection paths are used: (1) Binary Search based Topology Selection over the real simulation dataset (sorted into metric lists; binary search O(log n)) to produce candidate topologies, and (2) Synthetic Data based Topology Ranking using variational autoencoders to generate synthetic performance points, compute a FoM, sort topologies, and take the top three for verification. Final verification and sizing invoke DNN-Opt on the ranked candidates and compare the final sized topology from the synthetic approach against the binary-search result by performance.

## pqac-00000061

From text 'zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4'.

Zhenxin Zhao and Lihong Zhang. Deep reinforcement learning for analog circuit structure synthesis. 2022 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1157-1160, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

The excerpt describes Zhao & Zhang's DRL framework for analog circuit topology synthesis. Topologies are built from building blocks (BBs); circuits that are structurally identical but have swapped input pins are treated as distinct, and the decoded structure should reproduce swapped-pin copies. Except for differential pairs, all transistors within a BB are constrained to equal length and width; two special nets-only BBs have I/O combinations dependent on input order. Decoded structures undergo a fast symbolic evaluation: the GPDD algorithm numerically computes DC gain of unsized circuits, and the quality filter is deliberately relaxed so only very poor designs are filtered out. Passing candidates are sized with a modified NSGA-II using SPICE (CMOS 65nm) to verify feasibility rather than optimize; population and generation sizes are 20 and 50, and the routine early-terminates and stores (performance, size) in a hash table when a candidate meets the target spec. DRL agent settings reported: state size 10, action size 20; PGNN with two hidden layers (300, 200 neurons); learning rate 0.002, discount 0.95, batch 200; initial state is a PMOS differential pair (BB 26). The method produces far fewer unique structures than prior brute-force methods.


---

## pqac-00000062

From text 'zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1'.

Zhenxin Zhao and Lihong Zhang. Deep reinforcement learning for analog circuit structure synthesis. 2022 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1157-1160, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

Zhao & Zhang (2022) propose a DRL-based approach for analog circuit structure (topology) synthesis that blends structure generation and reasoning. The method begins from a starting sub-circuit and incrementally expands topology using building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process guided by reinforcement learning; necessary design rules are encoded to create a specialized RL environment. Evaluation efficiency is improved by using hash tables and symbolic-analysis techniques: these reduce the number of produced topologies that require costly sizing/simulation and enable faster verification of generated circuits. The authors claim this is the first application of DRL to automate analog-IC structure synthesis. Key reported advantages include framing synthesis as a decision process, providing a flexible and controllable design search space, enabling the automatic learning of design knowledge, and substantially lowering computation compared with conventional structure-generation methods.

URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

This excerpt from Zhao and Zhang (DATE 2022) describes a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach for analog circuit topology synthesis and contrasts it with prior methods (MOJITO, FEATS, GCTC, KMR). The authors claim their design search space is "flexible and controllable," which can be realized "by extending PBBL or changing state size." Comparisons use the same building-block library (PBBL) and identical I/O specifications. The paper defines "structure size" as the maximum block number, maximum leave number, and maximum state size allowed in each method. The DRL process is stochastic; reported results are averages over ten runs, and each learning run takes about 2–9 hours. In contrast, MOJITO required evaluating ~101,904 structures to yield 512 unique structures (taking 7 days), and KMR requires a time-consuming data-mining step. The excerpt emphasizes lower computation effort for the proposed DRL method versus some prior approaches. The provided text does not mention hash tables or symbolic-analysis techniques for topology generation or specification hashing, nor implementation details of any hash-table or symbolic analysis mechanisms.

## pqac-00000064

From text 'zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-2'.

Zhenxin Zhao and Lihong Zhang. Deep reinforcement learning for analog circuit structure synthesis. 2022 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1157-1160, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

Zhao & Zhang propose a DRL-based framework for automatic circuit structure (topology) synthesis that constructs circuits by selecting trustworthy building blocks (BBs) from a predefined building block library (PBBL). Design specifications are split into input-output (I/O) specifications, which form the RL environment with the PBBL, and performance specifications, which are used to compute rewards. BB terminals are categorized as V, FI, and FO, and six BB types are defined based on terminal counts and types. States are encoded as fixed-size arrays where each slot holds a BB index (or zero if empty); a single state can sometimes decode to multiple concrete circuit structures when inter-BB connections are ambiguous. Only a subset of states meeting certain conditions are decoded and fully evaluated. The agent’s policy is implemented as a policy gradient neural network (PGNN) producing action probabilities; episodes end on unmatched actions, state boundary violations, or explicit terminational actions. The reward function gives zero for non-terminal steps and assigns positive or negative rewards at terminal steps to encourage or discourage the resulting structures.

## pqac-00000066

From text 'vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2'.

Prashanth Vijayaraghavan, Luyao Shi, Ehsan Degan, and Xin Zhang. Circuitsynth: leveraging large language models for circuit topology synthesis. 2024 IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pages 1-6, Jun 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716, doi:10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716. This article has 15 citations.

The paper presents CIRCUITSYNTH, a two-phase LLM-based approach for automatic circuit topology synthesis. Phase 1 (Circuit Topology Generation) fine-tunes an LLM to produce netlist-style circuit topologies in an autoregressive manner. Phase 2 (Circuit Topology Refinement) applies a learned circuit validity classifier to estimate the likelihood a generated topology is valid, and uses a Generation Enhancement step that refines generation by minimizing a combined loss: negative log-likelihood plus a circuit invalidity score. The authors curated a large dataset of power-converter circuits (862,606 examples: 567,307 valid and 295,299 invalid) composed of five device types (capacitors, inductors, phase-I switches Sa and phase-II switches Sb, etc.), generated via Random Search and NGSpice. For evaluation, CIRCUITSYNTH-generated topologies are passed to a SPICE simulator to verify validity. Experimental results report that CIRCUITSYNTH outperforms various fine-tuned LLM variants. Key contributions include the method, the dataset, and the circuit validity classifier.


---

## pqac-00000067

From text 'tuotian2021layoutawaresizingmethodology pages 126-129'.

Tuotian Liao. Layout-aware sizing methodology for analog integrated circuits. Text, 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48336/zeeh-ct05, doi:10.48336/zeeh-ct05. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a two-phase, LDE-aware circuit synthesis flow comprised of four modules. Phase 1 (symbolic sizing) contains Module-I and Module-II, both formulated as MINLP problems: Module-I performs LDE-free gm/ID-based sizing (initialization, L-determination, device characterization and curve fitting) to produce baseline (W, L, nf) solutions; Module-II performs LDE-aware optimization by replacing design/intermediate variables with symbolic expressions (X1, X2 and ξi), optimizing W and nf first, then geometrical LDE-related parameters (LRext, SCt) in a second round so SA/SB and SCA are reflected in simulation netlists. Phase 2 (many-objective EA-based sizing) uses Module-III (a many-objective evolutionary algorithm) to refine LDE-aware solutions and optimize device and layout factors further. Module-IV performs layout synthesis (placement and routing) using Module-III outputs. The flow iterates on verification failures. The text also defines LDE-relevant distances (SCt, SA/SB, SABeff/SABedge) and finger-related parameters (nf, sd, bs) used in modeling layout-dependent effects.

## pqac-00000068

From text 'chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2'.

Chen-Chia Chang, Wan-Hsuan Lin, Yikang Shen, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic2: advanced circuit formulations for language model-based analog topology generation. ArXiv, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235. This article has 5 citations.

The excerpt describes LaMAGIC2, an LLM-based analog topology generation approach that uses supervised sequence-to-sequence finetuning and a novel representation called SFCI (succinct float-input canonical formulation with identifier). SFCI encodes numeric specifications as floats and uses identifiers to improve component-type recognition, which reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|), shrinking LM input size. The training follows a two-phase finetuning/transfer workflow: large-scale finetuning on many small (3–5 component) circuits followed by targeted finetuning on fewer larger (6-component) circuits. Reported evaluation shows substantial gains: 34% higher success under a tight 0.01 tolerance and 10× lower MSE versus a prior method, plus reduced sequence lengths (LaMAGIC2-SFCI outputs ~36–41 tokens vs LaMAGIC-FM ~85–105 tokens). Code is publicly available. These points are relevant to methodologies that use two-phase generation/refinement with representation changes to improve LLM-based circuit synthesis and transfer to larger topologies.

## pqac-00000069

From text 'vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 2-3'.

Prashanth Vijayaraghavan, Luyao Shi, Ehsan Degan, and Xin Zhang. Circuitsynth: leveraging large language models for circuit topology synthesis. 2024 IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pages 1-6, Jun 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716, doi:10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716. This article has 15 citations.

The paper proposes CIRCUITSYNTH, a two-phase approach for circuit topology synthesis using pretrained LLMs. Phase 1 (Circuit Topology Generation) frames topology creation as autoregressive text generation using an "incident encoding" of netlists, sampling Y from pLLM(Y|X) and trained with a negative log-likelihood objective. Generated circuits may violate validity constraints. Phase 2 (Circuit Topology Refinement) adds: (1) a Circuit Validity Estimation module — a frozen RoBERTa-based classifier that outputs pvalid (used to compute Lvalid = (1 − pvalid)) and achieves an F1 score of 92% for validity classification; and (2) a Generation Enhancement step that combines the LLM loss and validity loss into L = λ1LLLM + λ2Lvalid to steer generation toward valid designs. To enable gradient flow through discrete samples, the method uses the Gumbel-softmax trick. They explore training strategies including In-Context Learning (ICL) with k ∈ {5,10,20} examples, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with 100–500 soft prompt tokens, and vanilla fine-tuning; models evaluated include Llama-2 (13b), Flan-Ul2 (20b), GPT-Neo and StableLM.

## pqac-00000070

From text 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 4-5'.

Haoyi Zhang, Shizhao Sun, Yibo Lin, Runsheng Wang, and Jiang Bian. Analogxpert: automating analog topology synthesis by incorporating circuit design expertise into large language models. 2025 International Symposium of Electronics Design Automation (ISEDA), pages 772-777, Dec 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824. This article has 24 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, a training-free LLM-based analog topology synthesis agent that embeds human design expertise. It reformulates topology synthesis as a real-world SPICE code generation problem, representing devices and their connections directly in SPICE rather than using ideal models or Python-code formulations. To make generation concise and tractable, AnalogXpert leverages a well-designed subcircuit library to create an effective analog circuit representation. Its prompting strategy is domain-specific: a chain-of-thought (CoT) based prompting flow is used and further enhanced by in-context learning examples. Because single-round LLM generation often fails on complex designs, AnalogXpert adds human experience–based proofreading: an iterative, multi-round refinement where human-like corrections guide the model to gradually fix mistakes and converge on a correct topology. The approach is presented as a staged flow (design requirement -> subcircuit/block representation -> CoT & in-context prompting -> human proofreading) and is reported to achieve decent performance on conditional analog topology synthesis tasks.

## pqac-00000071

From text 'vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 3-4'.

Prashanth Vijayaraghavan, Luyao Shi, Ehsan Degan, and Xin Zhang. Circuitsynth: leveraging large language models for circuit topology synthesis. 2024 IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pages 1-6, Jun 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716, doi:10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716. This article has 15 citations.

The paper describes CIRCUITSYNTH (CS), a two-phase methodology for circuit-topology generation using LLMs with a refinement step driven by a circuit validity classifier. During training they address non-differentiable sampling from the LLM categorical distribution by using the Gumbel-Max trick and the Gumbel-softmax relaxation, combined with a straight-through estimator to enable gradient flow. They experiment with prompt tuning (100–500 soft prompt tokens) and vanilla fine-tuning of GPT-Neo-2.7 and StableLM-3B-4E1T. CIRCUITSYNTH augments fine-tuned generation with an independent validity classifier and uses SPICE simulations for ground-truth evaluation. Metrics reported include E(fvalid(ŷ)) from the classifier, E(fSvalid(ŷ)) and E(fSeff(ŷ)) from SPICE, computed over 1000 unique samples; a topology is labeled valid if the classifier score > 0.6. They also measure Duplicate Generation Rate (DGR). Results in Table I show CIRCUITSYNTH models outperform baselines in validity and efficiency (examples: CSGPT-Neo E(fvalid)=0.648, E(fSvalid)=0.713; CSStableLM E(fSvalid)=0.728). A two-sample t-test shows classifier predictions correlate strongly with simulator validity (p < 0.05). An ablation studies natural-language incident encoding’s impact.

## pqac-00000072

From text 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10'.

Haoyi Zhang, Shizhao Sun, Yibo Lin, Runsheng Wang, and Jiang Bian. Analogxpert: automating analog topology synthesis by incorporating circuit design expertise into large language models. 2025 International Symposium of Electronics Design Automation (ISEDA), pages 772-777, Dec 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824. This article has 24 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, an LLM-based analog topology synthesis system that is training-free and uses a subcircuit-based circuit representation combined with chain-of-thought (CoT) and in-context learning plus human experience-based proofreading to mimic human design steps. The authors evaluate on both real and synthetic structural-requirement benchmarks and report overall correct ratios of 40% on real data and 23% on synthetic data, with per-task breakdowns shown (e.g., Average 629/2000 = 31% up to 801/2000 = 40%). Figure 5 highlights three real cases with both successful and failed circuit diagrams; common failure modes include connection errors (e.g., floating current output/input terminals) and block errors (e.g., wrong cascode stage type, NMOS vs PMOS). Limitations noted include a small real dataset; future directions emphasize generating high-quality synthetic SPICE topologies and fine-tuning smaller models for improved performance and data security. These points provide concrete methodological components, evaluation metrics, failure categories, and dataset/scale considerations that could inform design, two-phase generation/refinement strategies, and evaluation for a CircuitSynth-style LLM approach.

## pqac-00000073

From text 'vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6'.

Prashanth Vijayaraghavan, Luyao Shi, Ehsan Degan, and Xin Zhang. Circuitsynth: leveraging large language models for circuit topology synthesis. 2024 IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pages 1-6, Jun 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716, doi:10.1109/lad62341.2024.10691716. This article has 15 citations.

The excerpt describes Circuitsynth's dataset, training, and evaluation setup for LLM-driven power-converter topology synthesis. A dataset of 862,606 circuits (567,307 valid, 295,299 invalid) was created by random search and NGSpice simulation; valid circuits include collected efficiency values. Designs use 5 device components (capacitors C, inductors L, phase-I switches Sa, phase-II switches Sb) plus three external ports (’IN’, ’OUT’, ’0’) yielding 13 ports total; device parameters were fixed (C=10µF, L=100µH). Training used shuffled data for 5–7 epochs, gradient checkpointing, AdamW (betas 0.9, 0.95; eps 1.0E-8), learning rate 0.95E-5, and seed 42; checkpoints saved based on validation performance. During training they evaluate 100 sample generations per check. For inference/evaluation, each model generates 1000 unique samples using nucleus + top-k sampling; generated topologies are fed to a validity classifier to compute percentage valid generations. Experiments ran on PyTorch v2.2.0 with CUDA 11.8 on NVIDIA V100 (32 GB). The excerpt does not explicitly describe a "two-phase generation/refinement" procedure or detailed refinement results.

function.arguments: {"question": "GAN-based circuit synthesis generative adversarial network analog circuit topology generation low-pass filter methodology results", "text_name_focus_list": ["guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 1-2", "guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 6-8", "guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000074

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

The paper evaluates several GAN variants for analog circuit topology synthesis (frequency response and low-pass filter problems). The improved WGAN produced the highest fraction of feasible topologies (e.g., 1,990/10,000 for one case) and its feasibility rate rises markedly with longer training (up to 42.66% at 50,000 iterations). Standard WGAN, GAN, and DCGAN performed worse (3.44%, 1.51%, and 0.36% feasible, respectively). A parametric study showed the improved WGAN performed best with 64 latent variables and a penalty λ = 10 (19.90% feasible for the frequency problem); similar trends appeared for the low-pass filter (64 latent variables and λ = 10 giving 14.55% feasible). An active learning loop combined with the GAN generator yielded 2,973 feasible circuits out of 10,000 and produced predictive models evaluated by RMSE and normalized Kendall tau (Set 1 RMSE=2.4942, ˜K=0.2631; Set 2 RMSE=2.9266, ˜K=0.2557). The authors note DCGAN’s poor results may stem from insufficient training time on CPU TensorFlow implementations.


---

## pqac-00000075

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 6-8'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

The excerpt describes applying GAN variants to analog circuit topology synthesis where circuits are represented as labeled graphs and encoded via adjacency matrices converted to fixed-length vectors for ML. Two target problems are studied: frequency response matching and low-pass filter realizability. Large enumerated datasets are used as training data: 43,249 unique circuit topologies for the frequency task and 1,804,496 unique graphs (123,156 with up to 7 components) for the LPF problem. Several GAN variants are compared (GAN, WGAN, improved WGAN, DGGAN) with generator/discriminator implemented as either fully-connected MLPs or convolutional models. Input latent vector nz is drawn from a standard multivariate normal. GAN training details include flattened adjacency inputs (465×1 and 630×1), a 5:1 discriminator-to-generator update ratio, LReLU and tanh activations in the generator, and for WGANs four fully-connected layers with weight truncation to [−0.01, 0.01] and RMSProp optimization. Improved WGANs add a gradient-penalty term (regularization Î»=100) to enforce the K-Lipschitz constraint and mitigate issues from weight clipping (e.g., unstable training, slow convergence, vanishing gradients). Hidden layer sizes of 128, 256, 512 are used in some architectures.


---

## pqac-00000076

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 2-4'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

The excerpt describes using generative adversarial networks (GANs) as an indirect design representation to generate circuit topologies for Case 2 synthesis problems where enumeration is impractical. GANs are chosen over VAEs because they generally produce sharper samples and do not require variational bounds or Markov chains. The generator maps low-dimensional latent vectors to candidate circuit topologies while a discriminator penalizes unrealistic designs. The authors train GANs on a statistical sample X of feasible topologies and produce a generated set M; effectiveness is measured by the feasibility ratio |T|/|M|, where T is the subset of generated designs that are actually feasible. The methodology is evaluated with several GAN variants and two electric circuit case studies: a frequency response optimization task and a low-pass filter feasibility problem. The approach trades some solution accuracy for greater representational scope: introducing a GAN reduces design representation dimensionality to make richer topology spaces practically solvable, at the cost of potentially incomplete coverage of the true feasible set. Quantitative results are not present in the provided excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000077

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 1-2'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

Guo et al. propose a GAN-based framework for circuit synthesis that maps a lower-dimensional latent space to circuit topologies, using a generator to produce topologies and a discriminator to improve generator quality. The paper compares multiple GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) and conducts numerical experiments to evaluate their ability to generate novel circuit topologies and support design exploration. Two case studies are used to demonstrate the approach: frequency response matching and low-pass filter realizability. The motivation is to address synthesis problems too large for enumeration methods: while recent efficient enumeration algorithms can handle moderately sized catalogs and have been used to generate data, enumeration becomes impractical for large component sets. The authors position GANs as a way to solve larger synthesis problems and to gain deeper design insights. The paper also situates the GAN approach relative to prior methods (domain-knowledge, metaheuristics, indirect encodings) and notes issues with metaheuristics such as high-dimensional direct encodings and tuning needs. Sizing optimization (continuous parameter tuning) remains a separate step after topology generation.


---

## pqac-00000078

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

The paper evaluates multiple GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) for generating analog circuit topologies (case studies: frequency response and low-pass filter problems). The improved WGAN produced the most feasible topologies, with a reported 42.66% feasibility for the frequency-response case. Using the Wasserstein distance in the loss helped overcome instability and mode collapse. DCGAN underperformed, possibly due to lengthy CPU-only TensorFlow training; the authors note that GPU training or a deep convolutional WGAN (DC-WGAN) might help. Network architecture matters: four hidden layers caused instability and mode collapse, while reducing layers improved results; simpler fully-connected improved WGANs outperformed more complex DCGANs in their experiments. Representation issues were noted: fixed-size adjacency matrices can cause graph isomorphism problems that may mislabel feasible/infeasible designs. The workflow pairs GAN-generated topologies with sizing optimization (Figure 13) and active learning; ranking quality of generated candidates was similar according to normalized Kendall tau. The authors suggest future work including different generative models (VAEs, PixelCNN/RNN), DC-WGANs, and transfer to other topology synthesis problems.


---

## pqac-00000079

From text 'guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 8-11'.

Tinghao Guo, Daniel R. Herber, and James T. Allison. Circuit synthesis using generative adversarial networks (gans). AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum, Jan 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2019-2350, doi:10.2514/6.2019-2350. This article has 23 citations.

The paper evaluates multiple GAN variants (GAN, WGAN, improved WGAN, DCGAN) for circuit-topology synthesis, including a low-pass filter case. Architectures: fully connected WGAN/improved WGANs use four layers with hidden sizes 128, 256, 512; the improved WGAN uses weight clipping and a regularization term λ = 100 and is trained with RMSProp. DCGAN generator uses deconvolutional layers (BS × 5×5×128, 9×9×64, 18×18×32) producing a circuit topology tensor BS × 36×36×1 and uses batch-norm + LReLU in early layers and deconvolutional-tanh output; DCGANs are trained with Adam. The generator last-layer activation uses tanh (inputs transformed to [−1,1]). Evaluation: they draw 20,000 training samples from the complete set Ω; latent vector size 64. Generated sets M are filtered and compared to Ω; metric P = |T|/|M| measures fraction of feasible topologies (T ⊂ M ∩ Ω). Generated circuits are post-filtered with NSCs and can be passed to predictive models (active learning) for performance estimation. Dataset sizes for the two problems are 43,249 and 123,156 (low-pass likely the larger). For the frequency-response case the improved WGAN achieved 1,990/10,000 feasible samples (≈19.9%), WGAN ≈3.44%, while GAN and DCGAN performed worst; similar evaluation procedures apply to the low-pass filter experiments.


---

## pqac-00000080

From text 'gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7'.

Jian Gao, Weidong Cao, Junyi Yang, and Xuan Zhang. Analoggenie: a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. ArXiv, Feb 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205. This article has 51 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a generative engine for analog circuit topologies that uses a sequence-based representation rather than GANs. Topologies are modeled as finite connected undirected graphs and converted to directed graphs so they can be represented as Eulerian circuits; this stores only existing edges and is more efficient than adjacency matrices for sparse analog circuits. The authors prove the directed conversion contains at least one Eulerian circuit starting from any node. To address permutation invariance and overfitting, AnalogGenie generates multiple unique Eulerian circuits per topology (stating this yields 70× more data), uses BFS node ordering (GraphRNN approach) within device types, and fixes VSS as the sequence start. A custom tokenizer maps device pins to tokens (each token represents a device pin), includes truncate/padding tokens, and the model is pre-trained to predict the next device pin in the sequence. The device pin–level graph representation enforces a unique mapping from graph to circuit, and the dataset contains 3350 topologies across 11 circuit families. The excerpt does not describe GAN-based training nor low-pass filter–specific methodology or results.


---

## pqac-00000081

From text 'gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 15-19'.

Jian Gao, Weidong Cao, Junyi Yang, and Xuan Zhang. Analoggenie: a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. ArXiv, Feb 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205. This article has 51 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. The authors represent circuit topologies using a device pin-level graph and identify multiple Eulerian circuits via depth-first search (DFS), which appears to be part of how topologies are enumerated or serialized. The paper demonstrates synthesis results by visualizing generated schematics in Cadence Virtuoso and reports several novel generated circuits with performance numbers: an op-amp (GBW=12 MHz, CL=100 pF, Power=32.88 mW, FoM=36.5), a DC converter (Efficiency=0.95, VCR=2.35, FoM=3.3), and a bandgap reference (TC=3 ppm/°C, Line regulation=0.196 %/V, PSRR=70 dB, FoM=21.9). The model shows zero-shot capability by producing a transconductance amplifier type not present in the training dataset. The paper also includes failed examples. The excerpt does not mention GAN-based architectures, low-pass filter design, or explicit methodology/results specific to low-pass filters.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive summary table of 15 key references on automated/generative circuit topology synthesis and netlist generation (2019-2025). The table should have columns: Reference # | Authors (Year) | Title | Venue | Category (a/b/c/d) | Key Method | Key Results/Metrics | Limitations. Include these papers in order:

1. Chang et al. (2024) - LaMAGIC - arXiv 2407.18269 - Category a,b - LM-based topology generation via SFT on Flan-T5, adjacency-matrix formulation - 96% success rate at tolerance 0.01, power converters only - Limited to small circuits (3-6 components), synthetic data only

2. Chang et al. (2025) - LaMAGIC2 - arXiv 2506.10235 - Category a,b - SFCI formulation reducing token length O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) - 34% higher success than LaMAGIC, 10x lower MSE - Still limited to power converter topologies

3. Gao et al. (2025) - AnalogGenie - arXiv 2503.00205 - Category a - GPT-based pin-level graph generation, Eulerian circuit encoding, 3350 topologies - Discovers unseen topologies, zero-shot generalization - Requires large curated dataset, no specification conditioning

4. Dong et al. (2023) - CktGNN - arXiv 2308.16406 - Category a,c - Two-level nested GNN + VAE, Open Circuit Benchmark (10K op-amps) - 91.9% valid circuit rate, FoM r≈0.806, outperforms human designs - Limited to op-amps in OCB, ideal component models

5. Lai et al. (2025) - AnalogCoder - arXiv 2405.14918 - Category b - Training-free LLM agent generating PySpice code, feedback loop, circuit tool library - 20/24 benchmark tasks solved, outperforms GPT-4o - Struggles with complex circuits, functional correctness not parameter optimization

6. Zhang et al. (2025) - AnalogXpert - arXiv 2412.19824, ISEDA 2025 pp. 772-777 - Category b,d - LLM agent with subcircuit library, CoT + proofreading, SPICE code output - 40% success (synthetic), 23% (real), vs GPT-4o 3% - Small real dataset, connection/block errors persist

7. Vijayaraghavan et al. (2024) - CircuitSynth - IEEE LAD 2024 pp. 1-6 - Category b - Two-phase: LLM generation + RoBERTa validity classifier with Gumbel-softmax refinement - ~72% SPICE-validated validity rate, 862K circuit dataset - Power converter topologies only, no spec conditioning

8. Chen et al. (2025) - MenTeR - IEEE ICLAD 2025 pp. 124-132 - Category b,d - Multi-agent workflow: PI, Circuit, Testbench agents; DA-RAG, Chain-of-Stage reasoning - GPT-4o: 84.2% Pass@1, end-to-end design - Human-in-loop needed for complex systems, subcircuit errors at system level

9. Wang et al. (2025) - AnalogSAGE - arXiv 2512.22435 - Category b,c,d - Self-evolving multi-agent with stratified memory, Bayesian optimization, simulation-grounded - 10x pass rate improvement, 48x Pass@1 vs baselines - Limited to op-amp designs on SKY130

10. Bhandari et al. (2024) - Masala-CHAI - arXiv 2411.14299 - Category b - Automated SPICE dataset from textbook schematics via LLM + YOLO, 7500 netlists - 46% Pass@1 improvement when fine-tuning AnalogCoder - Component misidentification (NMOS/PMOS), net detection ~45% accuracy

11. Settaluri et al. (2020) - AutoCkt - arXiv 2001.01808 - Category c - Deep RL (PPO) for analog circuit sizing, transfer learning across environments - 96.3% target convergence, 40x faster than GA, LVS-passed layouts - Fixed topology, sizing only, requires SPICE simulator access

12. Zhao & Zhang (2022) - DRL Circuit Structure Synthesis - DATE 2022 pp. 1157-1160 - Category c,d - DRL topology synthesis from building blocks, hash table + symbolic analysis - First DRL for topology synthesis, explores novel structures - Limited building block library, stochastic results, 2-9 hour runs

13. Choi et al. (2024) - MA-Opt - IEEE TCAS-I vol.71 pp. 2045-2056 - Category c - Multi-actor RL with shared elite set, critic surrogate, near-sampling - 34% better FoM than DNN-Opt at same simulations - Sizing only (fixed topology), requires initial topology selection

14. Poddar et al. (2024) - Data-Driven Synthesizer - DATE 2024 pp. 1-6 - Category a,d - Modular parameterized topology library + VAE synthetic data augmentation + DNN-Opt sizing - 360+ OPAMP topologies, optimal netlist in ~10 min - One-time costly data collection, limited to OPAMP family

15. Guo et al. (2019) - GAN Circuit Synthesis - AIAA Scitech 2019 - Category a - GAN/WGAN/improved WGAN for topology generation from adjacency matrices - Up to 42.66% feasibility rate (improved WGAN) - Low feasibility, graph isomorphism issues, no spec conditioning"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Reference # | Authors (Year) | Title | Venue | Category (a/b/c/d) | Key Method | Key Results/Metrics | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chang et al. (2024) | LaMAGIC | arXiv:2407.18269 | a,b | SFT on Flan-T5 for analog topology generation using adjacency-matrix and float-input formulations | Up to 96% success at tolerance 0.01; transfer from 3-5 component pretraining to 6-component circuits; power-converter focus (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011) | Small circuits only; synthetic data and simulated evaluation; limited component diversity (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000014) |
| 2 | Chang et al. (2025) | LaMAGIC2 | arXiv:2506.10235 | a,b | SFCI representation reduces sequence length from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) and improves numeric precision handling | 34% higher success than LaMAGIC under tight tolerance and 10x lower MSE; shorter token sequences and better transferability (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000015) | Still focused on power-converter topology generation; not broadly shown on mixed-signal control electronics (pqac-00000012) |
| 3 | Gao et al. (2025) | AnalogGenie | arXiv:2503.00205 | a | GPT-based pin-level graph generation with Eulerian-circuit encoding over 3,350 topologies across 11 circuit types | Discovers unseen and larger-scale topologies; reports zero-shot generation beyond training distribution; ~70x data augmentation via multiple serializations (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000081) | Requires a large curated dataset and custom representation pipeline; weak evidence for direct spec conditioning (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005) |
| 4 | Dong et al. (2023) | CktGNN | arXiv:2308.16406 | a,c | Two-level nested GNN with subgraph basis plus VAE latent generation; Open Circuit Benchmark with 10K op-amps | ~91.9% valid-circuit rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson r≈0.806; outperforms strong GNN baselines and reported human designs (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028) | Benchmark is op-amp-centric; uses idealized graph abstractions and component models, limiting manufacturable transistor-level realism (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) |
| 5 | Lai et al. (2025) | AnalogCoder | arXiv:2405.14918 | b | Training-free LLM agent generating PySpice code with domain prompts, feedback loop, and circuit tool library | Solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, beating GPT-4o; checks requirements, simulation, operating point, DC sweep, and function (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000017) | Struggles on complex circuits; prioritizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; still needs external simulation to catch invalid outputs (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000006) |
| 6 | Zhang et al. (2025) | AnalogXpert | ISEDA 2025, pp. 772-777; arXiv:2412.19824 | b,d | Training-free LLM agent with subcircuit library, CoT prompting, in-context examples, iterative proofreading, and SPICE output | 40% synthetic success and 23% real-data success versus GPT-4o at 3% and 3%; more practical SPICE-level output than idealized graph methods (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000022) | Small real dataset; connection mistakes and wrong block choices still common; needs richer real-data corpora or fine-tuned local models (pqac-00000022) |
| 7 | Vijayaraghavan et al. (2024) | CircuitSynth | IEEE LAD 2024, pp. 1-6 | b | Two-phase pipeline: LLM netlist generation plus RoBERTa validity classifier; Gumbel-softmax refinement toward valid circuits | Uses 862,606-example power-converter dataset; validity classifier F1≈92%; SPICE-validated validity about 0.713-0.728 for best variants, i.e. ~72% (pqac-00000066, pqac-00000069, pqac-00000071) | Restricted to power converters and validity-centric evaluation; no explicit broad spec-conditioned AMS block generation (pqac-00000066, pqac-00000073) |
| 8 | Chen et al. (2025) | MenTeR | IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124-132 | b,d | Multi-agent workflow with PI, Circuit, and Testbench agents; DA-RAG, Chain-of-Stage reasoning, execution checks, iterative refinement | GPT-4o backbone reached 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5; supports end-to-end netlist and testbench generation (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000050, pqac-00000051) | Human-in-the-loop still needed for complex systems; subcircuit-definition and missing-wire errors remain at system level (pqac-00000050) |
| 9 | Wang et al. (2025) | AnalogSAGE | arXiv:2512.22435 | b,c,d | Self-evolving multi-agent workflow with topology selection, refinement, parameter optimization, stratified memory, RAG, and Bayesian optimization grounded by simulation | Reported 10x overall pass-rate improvement, 48x Pass@1 improvement, and 4x smaller parameter search space on SKY130 op-amp tasks (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054) | Limited to op-amp designs and SKY130 environment; late 2025 arXiv work with little independent validation so far (pqac-00000053) |
| 10 | Bhandari et al. (2024) | Masala-CHAI | arXiv:2411.14299 | b | Automated schematic-to-SPICE dataset creation from textbook diagrams using YOLOv8, net extraction, prompt tuning, and LLM verification | Fine-tuning on Masala-CHAI improves AnalogCoder Pass@1 by 46%; ~7,500 netlists with GED-based structural verification (pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042) | Component confusion such as NMOS versus PMOS and weak transistor-level net detection remain major issues; net detection can be ~45% accurate without stronger CV support (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000047) |
| 11 | Settaluri et al. (2020) | AutoCkt | arXiv:2001.01808 | c | PPO-based deep RL for analog sizing across multiple target specs with transfer from schematic to post-layout environments | 96.3% target convergence on unseen specs, ~40x faster than genetic algorithms, and 40 LVS-passed op-amps in layout-aware experiments (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000036) | Fixed-topology sizing only; requires simulator access and substantial environment engineering; not a topology generator (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000036) |
| 12 | Zhao and Zhang (2022) | DRL Circuit Structure Synthesis | DATE 2022, pp. 1157-1160 | c,d | DRL topology synthesis from predefined building blocks with policy-gradient network, hash-table caching, and symbolic analysis filtering | Early RL topology-synthesis framework; uses GPDD symbolic filtering and SPICE-based NSGA-II verification; explores novel structures with lower computation than older methods (pqac-00000062, pqac-00000061, pqac-00000063) | Constrained by building-block library and state encoding; stochastic results; runs can take 2-9 hours and still need downstream sizing (pqac-00000061, pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064) |
| 13 | Choi et al. (2024) | MA-Opt | IEEE TCAS-I 71(5):2045-2056 | c | Multi-actor RL for analog sizing with shared elite set, critic surrogate, and cooperative near-sampling | Up to 34% better FoM and target metrics than DNN-Opt at equal simulation budget; improves exploration and sample efficiency (pqac-00000037) | Sizing only, not topology synthesis; depends on an already valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (pqac-00000037) |
| 14 | Poddar et al. (2024) | Data-Driven Synthesizer | DATE 2024, pp. 1-6 | a,d | Modular parameterized topology library, VAE synthetic data augmentation, binary-search and synthetic ranking, plus DNN-Opt verification and sizing | Evaluated on 360+ OPAMP topologies and 540K+ data points; generates optimal-sized topology netlists in about 10 minutes; synthetic data helps when real samples are sparse (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000058, pqac-00000059) | Requires significant one-time simulation and data collection; limited to OPAMP and OTA families in curated libraries (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000059) |
| 15 | Guo et al. (2019) | GAN Circuit Synthesis | AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum | a | GAN, WGAN, and improved WGAN generation of circuit-topology adjacency encodings for frequency-response and low-pass-filter synthesis | Improved WGAN reached up to 42.66% feasibility in the frequency-response case and beat GAN, DCGAN, and WGAN baselines (pqac-00000074, pqac-00000078) | Feasibility remains relatively low; graph-isomorphism and representation issues persist; no spec-conditioned or manufacturability-aware generation loop (pqac-00000076, pqac-00000078) |
