    title = "AMSbench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating MLLM Capabilities in AMS Circuits",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.24138",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.24138",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.24138"
}


Abstract:
Analog/Mixed-Signal (AMS) circuits play a critical role in the integrated circuit (IC) industry. However, automating Analog/Mixed-Signal (AMS) circuit design has remained a longstanding challenge due to its difficulty and complexity. Recent advances in Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer promising potential for supporting AMS circuit analysis and design. However, current research typically evaluates MLLMs on isolated tasks within the domain, lacking a comprehensive benchmark that systematically assesses model capabilities across diverse AMS-related challenges. To address this gap, we introduce AMSbench, a benchmark suite designed to evaluate MLLM performance across critical tasks including circuit schematic perception, circuit analysis, and circuit design. AMSbench comprises approximately 8000 test questions spanning multiple difficulty levels and assesses eight prominent models, encompassing both open-source and proprietary solutions such as Qwen 2.5-VL and Gemini 2.5 Pro. Our evaluation highlights significant limitations in current MLLMs, particularly in complex multi-modal reasoning and sophisticated circuit design tasks. These results underscore the necessity of advancing MLLMs' understanding and effective application of circuit-specific knowledge, thereby narrowing the existing performance gap relative to human expertise and moving toward fully automated AMS circuit design workflows. Our data is released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/wwhhyy/AMSBench

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4'.

Haoyi Zhang, Shizhao Sun, Yibo Lin, Runsheng Wang, and Jiang Bian. Analogxpert: automating analog topology synthesis by incorporating circuit design expertise into large language models. 2025 International Symposium of Electronics Design Automation (ISEDA), pages 772-777, Dec 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2412.19824. This article has 24 citations.

The excerpt describes prior automated topology synthesis approaches and the AnalogXpert system. AnalogXpert formulates topology synthesis as SPICE code generation and uses an extensible subcircuit library to reduce design space. It decomposes tasks into block selection and block connection (CoT-based) and applies a proofreading/iterative-refinement strategy; with several rounds of self-refinement LLM agents can avoid some basic mistakes. AnalogXpert is evaluated on a new benchmark containing 30 real tasks and 2k synthetic tasks, achieving 40% success on synthetic and 23% on real data, compared to GPT-4o's 3%/3%. ## pqac-00000008

From text 'chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10'.

Chen-Chia Chang, Yikang Shen, Shaoze Fan, Jing Li, Shun Zhang, Ningyuan Cao, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic: language-model-based topology generation for analog integrated circuits. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269. This article has 70 citations.

LaMAGIC is an LM-based topology generation system that can directly produce optimized analog circuit designs from a given specification in a single pass. The authors focus on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and develop multiple input/output formulations designed to be canonical and compatible with autoregressive language models. They find that novel circuit formulations outperform a naive formulation, reporting a success rate up to 0.96 under a tolerance of 0.01. In particular, an adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float inputs to the language model yields better effectiveness for understanding and generating more complex circuits; experiments include scalability tests on six-component circuits. The paper also observes that formulation choices affect overfitting: CF and CFDC show more severe overfitting than FM and PM, with FM and PM validated as more robust via transferability experiments that achieve higher success rates in complex 6-component circuits. The authors plan to extend LaMAGIC to a wider range of analog components.

The excerpt describes LaMAGIC, a language-model-based system for analog circuit topology generation that uses supervised finetuning (SFT) to adapt an LM to analog design. LaMAGIC is presented as able to produce an optimized circuit topology from custom specifications (e.g., conversion ratio and efficiency) in a single pass or "one iteration," achieving a high empirical success rate (reported up to 96%; figure notes "Success rate = 96%"), with an explicit claim of "LaMAGIC achieves a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01." The authors emphasize formulation choices for representing circuits to fit the autoregressive LM paradigm, framing circuits as graphs, and report that an adjacency matrix–based circuit formulation improves effectiveness and scalability for handling intricate/complex circuit designs. The approach is positioned as an alternative to many-iteration search methods (e.g., RL-based search), aiming to reduce simulation iterations by directly generating topologies aligned to given circuit specifications.

## pqac-00000010

From text 'chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 8-9'.

Chen-Chia Chang, Yikang Shen, Shaoze Fan, Jing Li, Shun Zhang, Ningyuan Cao, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic: language-model-based topology generation for analog integrated circuits. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269. This article has 70 citations.

The LaMAGIC work compares three edge-generation formulations for topology generation: CF, PM, and FM. Models are fine-tuned from LMs pretrained on 3–5-component circuits using datasets of 500, 1000, and 2000 6-component samples and evaluated on a 9k 6-component test set. PM and FM use numerical inputs and a matrix formulation, which the authors attribute to their stronger performance and transferability. FM shows superior adaptability, particularly at larger tolerances and with more training data; PM also outperforms CF. Data augmentation via random vertex order permutation for edge generation substantially improves success rates, especially for FM. Table 3 reports MSEs for voltage conversion ratio and efficiency: PM-non-augment (0.010, 0.005), PM-augment (0.007, 0.002), FM-non-augment (0.040, 0.013), FM-augment (0.006, 0.013). Table 4 shows training vs. testing losses (CF 0.05/0.07, CFDC 0.05/0.07, FM 0.07/0.09, PM 0.03/0.04), indicating more overfitting in CF/CFDC than FM/PM. Pretraining on simpler circuits is critical: training only on 6-component circuits produced invalid designs. The LM can generate previously unseen high-performance power converter topologies meeting challenging specs (VCR ~0.4–0.6 or >1.2 and efficiency >0.9).

The paper uses an adjacency-matrix-based formulation for circuit topology generation, applying T5-styled masked language modeling where, for edge generation, "two masking tokens are used to conceal the duty cycle selection and the connections," and for topology generation "the scope of masking extends further to include vertex information" so the model predicts full circuit structure. A Float-input Adjacency-matrix-based Formulation feeds numerical specifications (duty cycle options, voltage conversion ratio, efficiency) as floats into the transformer by a "shared linear layer to encode these numbers" and integrates them with token embeddings. The pretrained model is "Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022) as the pretrained model" (encoder-decoder, 248M) and models are SFT-trained: "We perform training for 120 epochs" with "learning rate of 3 × 10−4", "batch size of 128", and "L2 regularization strength of 10−5". They use data augmentation via "random vertex order permutations." Evaluation metrics include "success rate of the generated circuits within varied tolerances and the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the input specifications and the simulated performance of the generated circuits." Table 1 reports MSEs for voltage conversion ratio and efficiency across formulations (NF, CF, CFDC, PM, FM), showing substantially lower MSEs for canonical/float-matrix variants (e.g., CFDC/FM) versus NF, and the best topology MSEs reported for FM ("FM      0.006       0.013       0.002        0.001").

The excerpt describes LaMAGIC2, which replaces a prior adjacency-matrix / sequence-to-sequence LM formulation for analog topology generation with a succinct float-input canonical formulation with identifier (SFCI). SFCI reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) while adding component-type tokens and improving numeric precision sensitivity, addressing long input sequences and tight-spec tolerance issues. Table 1 shows large reductions in average LM output token length (LaMAGIC2-SFCI: 36.40 / 41.41 vs LaMAGIC-FM: 84.79 / 105.00 for 3–5 and 6-component circuits). Empirically, LaMAGIC2 attains much higher success under strict tolerances: it reports 34% higher success at tolerance 0.01 and 10× lower MSE than a prior method, and a combined claim of 122% and 34% higher success versus an RL search baseline and the original LaMAGIC respectively. The paper contrasts SFCI with an adjacency-matrix circuit formulation and notes that prior LM approaches used sequence-to-sequence supervised finetuning, which suffered low success rates on strict tolerances. Overall, LaMAGIC2 targets improved specification satisfaction and scalability for analog circuit topology generation.

LaMAGIC adapts large language models to analog circuit topology generation via supervised fine-tuning, targeting power-converter designs. Experimentally, LaMAGIC achieves a reported 0.96 success rate under a strict tolerance of 0.01. ## pqac-00000014

From text 'chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5'.

Chen-Chia Chang, Yikang Shen, Shaoze Fan, Jing Li, Shun Zhang, Ningyuan Cao, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic: language-model-based topology generation for analog integrated circuits. ArXiv, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2407.18269. This article has 70 citations.

The excerpt describes LaMAGIC’s adjacency-matrix-based formulation for representing analog circuit topologies to language models. The dataset includes 76k sampled 6-component circuits with 9k held out for evaluation. The adjacency-matrix formulation is split into a prefix and a circuit description: the prefix encodes high-level specifications (duty cycle options, voltage conversion ratio, efficiency), while the circuit description encodes the duty-cycle selection, a vertex order, and per-vertex connection entries. Connections are represented with discrete tokens (<no edge>, <edge 1>, <edge 2>, <both edges>), and ordering constraints (e.g., <edge 1> always precedes <edge 2>) enforce a canonical, unambiguous sequence. Earlier few-shot GPT-4 experiments (100 examples) failed to meet performance needs, motivating more structured representations and supervised finetuning. The excerpt explains the format and design choices (canonicalization, one-hot/selection encodings, explicit tokens) intended to improve LM training and loss alignment, but it does not report quantitative success rates or accuracy metrics for the adjacency-matrix formulation in this section.

## pqac-00000015

From text 'chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5'.

Chen-Chia Chang, Wan-Hsuan Lin, Yikang Shen, Yiran Chen, and Xin Zhang. Lamagic2: advanced circuit formulations for language model-based analog topology generation. ArXiv, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2506.10235. This article has 5 citations.

The paper frames analog topology generation as: given vertices V and circuit specifications (voltage conversion ratio r and efficiency η), generate connections E and select a duty cycle s from discrete options so the simulated circuit meets r and η. LaMAGIC had poor transferability to larger circuits, motivating LaMAGIC2 and alternative sequence formulations. The Pure-text adjacency-matrix formulation (PM) encodes hypergraph connections as an adjacency matrix indexed by the input vertex order; this removes the need for per-node identifiers (e.g., <Sa0>, <Sa1>) and improves recognition of same-type components. However, tokenization of numeric values remains problematic because numeric floats are split into subword tokens, diluting numeric semantics. The Float-input adjacency-matrix formulation (FM) addresses numeric tokenization by encoding numeric inputs via a shared linear layer (float-input embedding), improving the model’s ability to learn relationships between input specifications and circuit structure and showing stronger transferability (noted particularly for six-component circuits). FM and tabular adjacency formulations also localize errors to specific matrix entries, aiding graph-difference detection during training. A key drawback of adjacency-matrix (float) formulations is O(|V|^2) sequence length, increasing attention complexity and harming transformer effectiveness on larger graphs.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 7-8'.

Yao Lai, Sungyoung Lee, Guojin Chen, Souradip Poddar, Mengkang Hu, David Z. Pan, and Ping Luo. Analogcoder: analog circuit design via training-free code generation. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918. This article has 142 citations.

The paper evaluates a training-free LLM-based agent (AnalogCoder) for analog circuit design using a feedback-driven design flow and a circuit tool library. Evaluation uses Pass@k as the main metric (Pass@k = 1 − (n−c choose k) / (n choose k)), with n = 30 trials for open-source models and GPT-3.5, and n = 15 for fine-tuned GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and GPT-4o. A benchmark of 24 analog design tasks was created (Tasks 1–15 basic, 16–24 composite), including Task 18: Op-amp integrator. Multiple LLMs were compared (CodeLlama, WizardCoder, Llama3, DeepSeek-V2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o); open-source models ran on 4 A100 GPUs. The paper notes that all LLMs except GPT-4o were enhanced via prompt engineering, design flow feedback, and the circuit tool library; GPT-4o was evaluated without the tool. The authors state that AnalogCoder (GPT-4o + circuit tool library) achieved uniformly high success rates in the reported Table 3 results (table entries show repeated "100.0     100.0" values), indicating near-perfect Pass@1 and Pass@5 on the presented tasks.


---

## pqac-00000017

From text 'lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28'.

Yao Lai, Sungyoung Lee, Guojin Chen, Souradip Poddar, Mengkang Hu, David Z. Pan, and Ping Luo. Analogcoder: analog circuit design via training-free code generation. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918. This article has 142 citations.

The excerpt states that AnalogCoder uses a training-free, agent-based framework to improve LLM capabilities for analog circuit design. It employs a feedback-enhanced flow (Table 8) with multiple checks: requirement checks (presence of I/O nodes and basic connectivity), simulation and operating-point checks (e.g., "Check that no errors occur during the simulation, e.g., floating nodes." and "Check each MOSFET is active: Vgs>Vth; Vds> Vgs-Vth."), DC sweep checks (verify output varies over input range and find Vin producing output near Vdd/2), and functional requirement checks. Tasks are grouped for fine-tuning evaluation (Table 9 lists Task IDs by group). Benchmark tables show other models often have low pass rates (Table 10 averages are mostly small). In direct comparison (Table 11), AnalogCoder attains higher overall performance than baseline GPT-4 in these tests: reported averages are "Avg 62.2 77.7 66.1 75.9" (GPT-4 vs AnalogCoder) and the "# Solved 19 19 20 20" line indicates AnalogCoder solved one more task set (20) than GPT-4 (19). Many individual task Pass@1/Pass@5 entries in Table 11 show 100% for several tasks for both systems.

## pqac-00000018

From text 'lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 9-11'.

Yao Lai, Sungyoung Lee, Guojin Chen, Souradip Poddar, Mengkang Hu, David Z. Pan, and Ping Luo. Analogcoder: analog circuit design via training-free code generation. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2405.14918. This article has 142 citations.

AnalogCoder is presented as a training-free LLM agent that reframes analog circuit design as Python code generation. The system pairs crafted prompts, a feedback-enhanced design flow, and a circuit tool library to raise design success rates and ships with an open-source analog design benchmark. Evaluation used repeated trials (50 trials per task) with up to five attempts per trial (GPT-3.5 was used for experiments and a fine-tuned GPT-3.5 baseline was tested with two epochs). Results show most successful designs are produced within three attempts, leading the authors to set the default attempts to three; composite circuits are constrained to two attempts due to complexity and cost. They report that success probability drops significantly after the third attempt and that even small discrepancies can render generated circuits non-functional. Limitations noted include difficulty designing highly complex analog circuits and practical constraints of compute cost and resources.

The authors provide code and the benchmark on GitHub. AnalogCoder is presented as a training-free LLM-based agent that generates Python code (via PySpice/SPICE integration) to design analog circuits. Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000024

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

CktGNN is a two-level, directed-message-passing GNN that represents circuits as DAGs of non-overlapping ordered subgraphs. Inner GNNs learn contextualized representations for each subgraph in a known basis B; under a stated injectivity condition an injective graph transformation f can be constructed, ensuring consistent mapping from circuits to transformed DAGs. For benchmark construction, the authors convert analog Op-Amp circuits into acyclic graphs by abstracting complex modules into low-level functional sub-structures and using these sub-structures as graph edges while connection points become vertices. Feedforward and feedback are unified into a single direction with a polarity flag (e.g., gm+ / gm−). The Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) integrates a conversion-generation-simulation loop: generated graph topologies are turned into netlists and evaluated by circuit simulators to extract DC gain, bandwidth, phase margin, and a computed FoM. The dataset includes 10,000 Op-Amps; node types are capacitors, resistors, and single-stage Op-Amps with specified parameter ranges (resistors 105–107 Ohm, capacitors 10−14–10−12 F, gm 10−4–10−2 S). CktGNN is compared against multiple baselines and shows superior predictive and topology reconstruction performance.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 1-2'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

The paper introduces CktGNN, a circuit graph neural network that jointly addresses topology generation and device sizing via encoder-dependent optimization subroutines. Architecturally, CktGNN uses a two-level (nested) GNN: a predefined subgraph basis models each circuit as a DAG whose nodes correspond to basis subgraphs. Inner GNNs independently learn representations for each subgraph (allowing multiple message-passing iterations for expressiveness and parallelism), while an outer directed GNN performs message passing over these learned node embeddings to encode the global circuit computation and performance. This representation enables continuous optimization of both topology and device parameters in a representation-based optimization framework. To support benchmarking and reproducible evaluation, the authors release the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open-sourced dataset of 10K distinct operational amplifiers with extracted circuit specifications and circuit generation/evaluation tools. Experiments on OCB report that CktGNN outperforms recent GNN baselines and human expert manual designs under their representation-based optimization approach.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 2-4'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

The excerpt describes CktGNN, a two-level graph neural network for circuit representation and automated analog circuit design. CktGNN models circuits using a pre-designed subgraph basis: inner GNNs independently encode each subgraph (allowing stacked message-passing for expressiveness and parallelism) and produce node embeddings; an outer GNN then performs directed message passing over these embeddings to model circuit computation and overall graph representation. The design reduces topology search space and targets circuit topology generation as well as device parameter optimization. To support reproducible evaluation, the authors introduce the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open graph dataset of 10K operational amplifiers with topologies modeled as graphs and performance metrics extracted from simulators; they plan to open-source generation and evaluation code and upload OCB to OGB. The paper positions CktGNN against prior methods that focus only on device sizing given fixed topologies and notes the scarcity and limitations of topology-synthesis approaches (e.g., genetic algorithms). Experimental claims state CktGNN outperforms GNN baselines and produces competitive op-amp designs versus human experts.

## pqac-00000027

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

CktGNN is a DAG-aware circuit graph neural network used both as a predictive model and as a latent-space encoder in a VAE generator for electronic circuit design. It can be trained in supervised mode or inside a VAE: two FC layers predict the posterior mean/variance (ELBO) and the decoder reuses a D-VAE-style generation to produce transformed DAGs from latent vectors. The decoder constructs circuits subgraph-by-subgraph from a known subgraph basis B, using MLPs to sample subgraph types, predict node features, and predict inter-subgraph connections; the outer GNN uses GRU-based directed message passing and maps latent z to initial hidden state h0 via an MLP. Training follows common VAE settings (α KL = 0.005, batch size 64, 200 epochs, initial lr 1e-4 with scheduler). For evaluation/generation the authors sample 1000 latent points and decode each 10 times; validity criteria require a single input and output, DAG topology, and no R/C nodes on the main path. The OCB benchmark was updated (Ckt-Bench101: 10000 successfully simulated circuits) and a larger Ckt-Bench301 was provided for Bayesian optimization tests. Results show CktGNN achieves strong predictive performance (e.g., FoM Pearson r ≈ 0.806), high valid-circuit rate (~91.9%) and novelty (~96.3%), and better Bayesian-optimization FoM and lower regret than DAGNN and D-VAE in reported experiments.

## pqac-00000028

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 9-11'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

The paper introduces CktGNN, a two-level GNN encoder using a pre-designed subgraph basis to represent circuit DAGs, and integrates it into a VAE framework for analog circuit design automation. CktGNN is evaluated on the authors' Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open-source dataset and toolset for circuit topology generation and device parameter optimization; the authors provide generation code, simulator files, experiment code, and appendices detailing proofs, comparisons, and instructions. Empirically, CktGNN-based decoders produce a significantly higher proportion of valid circuits than other decoders and the CktGNN-based VAE is reported to find circuits with the highest figure-of-merit (FoM), reducing simulation needs and improving time- and cost-efficiency. The work compares CktGNN to general two-level GNNs and discusses expressivity in appendices, positioning the method and OCB as generalizable, scalable, and compatible with other analog circuit types.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 16-18'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

The paper's CktGNN uses a two-level GNN architecture: an inner GNN that embeds extracted subgraphs (subgraph basis B) and an outer directed message-passing GNN operating on a transformed graph produced by a graphlizer f. When the subgraph basis and order satisfy Theorem 3.2, f can be constructed to injectively map input DAGs to transformed graphs, and if both inner GNNs and outer directed message passing are injective then CktGNN injectively maps circuits to vector embeddings. Expressive power is therefore independent of domain-specific node ordering, though ordering can change the topology search-space complexity; empirically for the op-amp circuits studied reversing C and R order did not change f(G) in 10,000 cases. Corollary B.1 states that with a proper subgraph basis the two-level framework can be strictly more powerful than standard message-passing GNNs and as powerful as nested GNNs for certain graph classes. Experimental setup notes: baseline message-passing models (GCN/GIN) used 3 convolution layers with mean pooling; NGNN uses rooted subgraph height h=3 and GIN l=3; Transformer models used 5 encoder layers and 6 attention heads; DAGNN uses two-layer directed convolutions. Experiments ran on Tesla P100 and GTX 1080 Ti, with 5 independent trials; evaluation included Bayesian optimization for circuit search (Figure 8 shows exemplary optimal circuits).


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 4-6'.

Zehao Dong, Weidong Cao, Muhan Zhang, Dacheng Tao, Yixin Chen, and Xuan Zhang. Cktgnn: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation. ArXiv, Aug 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.16406. This article has 114 citations.

The excerpt describes CktGNN, a two-level GNN for circuits that decomposes input circuits into non-overlapping subgraphs from a pre-defined ordered subgraph basis. Each input circuit G is transformed by a graphlizer f into a DAG G' whose nodes represent subgraphs; the transform aims to be injective under certain conditions. For each node v in G, if it belongs to multiple basis subgraphs the highest-ordered one is selected; connected nodes that select the same subgraph are merged and use the shared subgraph representation. Inner GNNs operate on each selected subgraph treated as an undirected graph, applying multiple undirected message-passing layers and graph pooling to produce a subgraph embedding hv'. The outer GNN runs directed message passing on the transformed DAG G', using a gated summation aggregation and a GRU-based update; the outer node inputs concatenate one-hot subgraph type encodings with the inner subgraph embeddings. The authors choose a VAE-based framework for circuit/DAG encoding (motivated by NAS methods) and note improved expressivity over 1-WL by encoding local subgraphs rather than rooted subtrees.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7'.

Jian Gao, Weidong Cao, Junyi Yang, and Xuan Zhang. Analoggenie: a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. ArXiv, Feb 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205. This article has 51 citations.

The paper represents analog circuit topologies at the device-pin level as finite connected undirected graphs G = (V, E), then converts each undirected edge into two directed arcs and encodes the resulting directed graph as an Eulerian circuit (a trail visiting every edge exactly once). This sequential Eulerian-circuit representation stores only existing edges, making it more space-efficient than adjacency matrices for sparse analog graphs; the authors prove a directed conversion always admits an Eulerian circuit. To reduce inductive bias and overfitting, they generate multiple unique Eulerian circuits per topology (yielding ~70× more data) and enforce graph-level permutation invariance by applying a BFS node ordering per device type and fixing “VSS” as the start node. They customize a tokenizer mapping tokens to device pins, add a “Truncate” token and padding for variable device counts, and pre-train the model to predict the next device pin in complete topology sequences. The dataset used contains 3350 distinct topologies across 11 circuit types. These design choices target scalable, unique sequential encodings for topology generation and downstream fine-tuning.

function.arguments: {"question": "AutoCkt deep reinforcement learning analog circuit sizing design specifications environment multi-objective results", "text_name_focus_list": ["settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2", "settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000032

From text 'settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2'.

Keertana Settaluri, Ameer Haj-Ali, Qijing Huang, Kourosh Hakhamaneshi, and Borivoje Nikolic. Autockt: deep reinforcement learning of analog circuit designs. Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808. This article has 298 citations.

AutoCkt is a machine-learning optimization framework that applies deep reinforcement learning to analog circuit sizing. The method is trained over a sparse sub-sample of the design space to learn tradeoffs and reduce convergence time during deployment, and it is explicitly formulated for N parameters and M target design specifications (x ∈ Z^N, y ∈ R^M). Training uses trajectory-based RL with policy-gradient updates to maximize expected accumulated reward. Compared to prior methods, AutoCkt generalizes across design goals via sparse sampling and transfer learning, avoiding per-spec retraining. In schematic simulations it converged to meet target specifications on at least 96.3% of tested goals and ran on average 40× faster than a traditional genetic algorithm. When including layout parasitics using the Berkeley Analog Generator, AutoCkt produced 40 LVS-passed operational amplifiers in 68 hours, about 9.6× faster than the state-of-the-art. The authors emphasize that the framework "intuitively understands the design space" and can identify unreachable targets when it fails to converge.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 2-3'.

Keertana Settaluri, Ameer Haj-Ali, Qijing Huang, Kourosh Hakhamaneshi, and Borivoje Nikolic. Autockt: deep reinforcement learning of analog circuit designs. Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808. This article has 298 citations.

The excerpt describes AutoCkt, a reinforcement-learning-based framework for analog circuit sizing. The system has two main components: an RL agent and a simulation environment. The agent’s neural network observes simulated performance o and a target specification o* and decides per-parameter increments/decrements/retains, with H simulation steps allowed to reach o*. Training samples 50 target specifications and generates trajectories; training ends when the mean reward reaches zero (meaning targets are consistently satisfied). The reward is a dense cumulative function that increases as observed performance approaches targets. Implementation details: a three-layer neural network with 50 neurons per layer trained using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) via OpenAI Gym and the Ray framework. AutoCkt interfaces with simulators (predictive technology models, Spectre, and BAG/Cadence with parasitics). In experiments on a transimpedance amplifier, AutoCkt achieved 25.1× speedup in sample efficiency versus a vanilla genetic algorithm and generalized to 97.4% of the design space; deployment tested 500 randomized targets. The excerpt also lists example action and specification ranges for evaluated circuits (e.g., TIA settling time, cutoff, input-referred noise).


---

## pqac-00000034

From text 'settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5'.

Keertana Settaluri, Ameer Haj-Ali, Qijing Huang, Kourosh Hakhamaneshi, and Borivoje Nikolic. Autockt: deep reinforcement learning of analog circuit designs. Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808. This article has 298 citations.

AutoCkt formulates analog sizing as a multi-objective RL environment with per-component action spaces and multi-parameter design specifications (examples: gain, unity-gain bandwidth, phase margin, bias current). For the transimpedance amplifier example the speciﬁcations considered were gain ([200,400] V/V), UGBW ([1.0e6,2.5e7] Hz), phase margin and bias current ([0.1,10] mA); the combined action space size can be enormous (e.g., 10^14 or 10^11 combinations), so random search is infeasible. Agents are allowed limited trajectory lengths (e.g., 30 simulation steps for the TIA). AutoCkt achieves strong multi-objective results and generalization: for a two-stage op amp it reaches 963/1000 unseen targets (generalizing ~20× beyond training specs) with an average of 27 simulations to converge (∼40× faster than a genetic algorithm). For a harder negative-gm two-stage op amp it converged to all 500 test targets with only 10 simulations on average. Training uses fast schematic sims (∼25 ms each), enabling tractable training (wall-clock example 1.3 hours on 8 cores), and transfer learning lets agents trained on schematic simulations handle slower post-layout/PEX environments despite much larger sim times (2.4s vs 91s).


---

## pqac-00000035

From text 'settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 6-6'.

Keertana Settaluri, Ameer Haj-Ali, Qijing Huang, Kourosh Hakhamaneshi, and Borivoje Nikolic. Autockt: deep reinforcement learning of analog circuit designs. Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808. This article has 298 citations.

The excerpt reports empirical results for AutoCkt, a deep reinforcement learning system for analog circuit sizing. It emphasizes that a vanilla genetic algorithm is "too sample inefﬁcient," while AutoCkt achieves a sample efficiency 9.56× greater than the prior state-of-the-art (a combined ML and genetic algorithm baseline). The method was evaluated on 40 randomly generated target design specifications and was able to obtain 40 LVS-passed designs, indicating robustness across the test set. Runtime is practical: on a single-core CPU the algorithm completes in 1.7 hours. The text also notes that relationships hold when considering layout parasitics despite differences between schematic and PEX simulations, suggesting the evaluation considered realistic parasitic effects. The authors provide an open-source implementation at the linked GitHub repository. Overall, the excerpt highlights improved sample efficiency, full success on a 40-spec benchmark, reasonable single-core runtimes, and awareness of layout parasitics in evaluations.


---

## pqac-00000036

From text 'settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6'.

Keertana Settaluri, Ameer Haj-Ali, Qijing Huang, Kourosh Hakhamaneshi, and Borivoje Nikolic. Autockt: deep reinforcement learning of analog circuit designs. Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2001.01808. This article has 298 citations.

AutoCkt is a deep RL framework for analog circuit sizing that handles multi-objective design specifications and both schematic and post-layout (PEX) environments. The authors use BAG to generate layouts and include PVT variations by taking the worst-performing metric as the specification. They demonstrate transfer learning by training an agent in Spectre (schematic) and running it on a TSMC 16nm FF generator in BAG for PEX, with no additional training when the environment changes. AutoCkt achieves large sample-efficiency gains versus genetic algorithms (reported ~40× on average and a 40.6× faster convergence in one case), with schematic-only agents converging in about 10 simulation steps and transferred PEX runs taking around 23 steps. Trajectories show rapid convergence (example: 11 time steps to meet a target), and multi-objective results indicate successful attainment of targets (figure notes: no unreached objectives). Including layout parasitics increases convergence time but the agent learns tradeoffs between specs; using ranges for constraints (e.g., phase margin) improved transfer by encouraging exploration. Overall, AutoCkt demonstrates robust, sample-efficient multi-objective analog sizing across schematic and layout-extracted environments.


---

## pqac-00000037

From text 'choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2'.

Youngchang Choi, Sejin Park, Minjeong Choi, Kyongsu Lee, and Seokhyeong Kang. Ma-opt: reinforcement learning-based analog circuit optimization using multi-actors. IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers, 71:2045-2056, May 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcsi.2024.3356582, doi:10.1109/tcsi.2024.3356582. This article has 26 citations.

This excerpt describes MA-Opt, an RL-inspired framework for analog circuit sizing that uses multiple actors running in parallel to predict diverse circuit designs and perform multiprocessing SPICE simulations. To make multi-actor learning effective, MA-Opt shares an "elite solution set" that influences the network loss and accelerates training, and it introduces a tuning technique to refine previously best designs. It also employs a near-sampling method that densely samples designs adjacent to the current best and uses a critic network that mimics the SPICE simulator as a surrogate to predict sampled results—reducing reliance on expensive simulator calls. The authors position MA-Opt relative to DDPG and DNN-Opt: DDPG-like methods often require thousands of SPICE simulations, while DNN-Opt uses a single agent. Experimentally, MA-Opt achieves up to 24% better metrics than DNN-Opt within the same number of simulations and better Figure of Merit (FoM) at the same runtime. The framework targets satisfying performance constraints in continuous-state analog sizing problems by improving exploration and surrogate-assisted local search.

KSN Suryanarayana. Analog circuit design and optimization using reinforcement learning. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes key RL components and how they apply to analog circuit sizing. It explains the replay buffer storing transitions (si, ai, ri, s′i) and prioritised experience replay (PER), where transitions are sampled with probability proportional to their TD-error to focus learning on high-error samples, and importance-sampling weights (wi with hyperparameter β) correct the introduced bias. The text outlines Q-learning and Double Q-learning update rules and the soft target-network update Q′weights = τ.Qweights + (1−τ).Q′weights, and notes that DDPG uses actor (µ/µ′) and critic (Q/Q′) networks with target counterparts. In the circuits context, circuits are treated as complex, nonlinear systems with emergent, sensitive behavior, making optimization difficult. Problem formulation is given: for a D-dimensional design vector x ∈ Ω (resistors, capacitors, transistor sizings), circuit performance f(x) — possibly a combined figure of merit — is evaluated via costly circuit simulation, and the goal is to find x that maximizes f(x). This highlights that RL/actor-critic methods with PER and target networks are suitable for expensive, black-box, possibly multi-objective circuit optimization but the excerpt does not present AutoCkt-specific architecture or empirical multi-objective results.

From text 'zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 4-5'.

Danial Noori Zadeh and Mohamed B. Elamien. Generative ai for analog integrated circuit design: methodologies and applications. IEEE Access, 13:58043-58059, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/access.2025.3553743, doi:10.1109/access.2025.3553743. This article has 20 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt frames analog circuit sizing as a context-dependent, often multi-objective constrained optimization problem where a Figure of Merit (FoM) is formed via a weighted preference vector and clipping to avoid single-constraint dominance. Practical automation must handle topology choices (topology-aware design) and device sizing, motivating bi-level formulations where an upper-level picks topology x and a lower-level optimizes device variables y (y*(x) = arg min G(x,y)). Layout parasitics and PVT (process, voltage, temperature) variations are critical: parasitics necessitate layout-level or post-layout-aware evaluation, and PVT corner testing identifies worst-case performance. Approaches discussed include constrained satisfaction/cost formulations and multi-fidelity strategies — e.g., multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization to integrate cheaper schematic and expensive post-layout simulations — as well as AI techniques that form trust regions or freeze low-impact variables to speed convergence. The text emphasizes the need for an intelligent agent that learns from experience, predicts objective behavior, generalizes to unseen tasks, and performs guided exploration in a high-dimensional, multi-objective design space.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000040

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

The Masala-CHAI paper presents a fully automated framework that uses large language models to generate SPICE netlists from analog circuit schematic images. The authors propose a three-step workflow—labeling analog circuits, prompt tuning, and netlist verification—to enable end-to-end netlist generation. They built and open-sourced the largest curated analog SPICE dataset to date: ~7,500 captioned netlists and metadata collected from 10 textbooks. The framework combines multiple components: a schematic extractor from documents, fine-tuned component detectors for passive and active parts, deep Hough-transform priors to identify nets, extensive prompt tuning for LLMs to correct common extraction errors, and post-extraction verification. They fine-tuned several LLMs (CodeLlama, DeepSeek-34B, GPT-3.5/4) and benchmarked open-source and proprietary models. Results show that larger fine-tuning datasets improve pass@k performance, and models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI used within agentic frameworks like AnalogCoder achieved a reported ~46% improvement in Pass@1, demonstrating substantial gains in automated analog netlist generation and verification.


---

## pqac-00000041

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

Masala-CHAI is an open-sourced, automated dataset of approximately 7,500 SPICE netlists with metadata (captions, components, nodes) gathered from 10 textbooks. The framework turns schematic images and associated textual context (captions, page text) into labeled training data and follows a three-step pipeline: (i) labeling analog circuits, (ii) prompt tuning, and (iii) SPICE netlist verification. The authors finetune and benchmark multiple LLMs (CodeLLama-70B, DeepSeek-34B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4o) on this dataset and report that incorporating GPT-4o fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI into the AnalogCoder agent yields a 46% absolute Pass@1 improvement. A key challenge is accurate component recognition: GPT-4o often misidentifies or omits elements (e.g., confusing NMOS vs. PMOS). To address this, the paper proposes two solutions: (1) a dedicated CNN-based object detection model (e.g., YOLO) tailored to schematic components to improve detection and bounding, and (2) targeted prompt tuning that emphasizes structural differences (e.g., NMOS vs PMOS arrow directions) to improve LLM differentiation. The pipeline includes SPICE netlist verification to ensure correctness after extraction.


---

## pqac-00000042

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 7-9'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

The excerpt describes Masala-CHAI's automated pipeline for generating SPICE netlists from circuit schematics and how the authors evaluate and verify its outputs. Netlists are parsed into graph representations where nodes are component types and edges are interconnections. They measure structural similarity between two SPICE netlists using Graph Edit Distance (GED), normalize GED by the maximum possible edit distance (summing nodes and edges across graphs) to produce a 0–100 metric, and define similarity accordingly. Using this metric, Masala-CHAI-generated netlists matched AMSNet outputs with a 100% similarity score, validating the flow and motivating broader evaluation. A key distinction is that AMSNet requires manual effort and lacks human-readable netlist descriptions, which limits its usefulness for supervised fine-tuning of LLMs. Masala-CHAI provides an end-to-end automated flow that also generates descriptions alongside SPICE netlists, improving suitability for LLM SFT (they fine-tuned GPT-4o and show example outputs). The evaluation emphasizes label-invariant graph construction (component types) and quantitative GED-based verification for large-scale netlist generation from images.


---

## pqac-00000043

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 5-6'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

The Masala-CHAI work provides a large-scale SPICE netlist dataset used to fine-tune LLMs for automated SPICE netlist generation and benchmarking. Baseline models were fine-tuned on the MASALA-CHAI corpus: open-source models were fine-tuned using LoRA on an Nvidia A100 80GB, while proprietary GPT models were fine-tuned via their fine-tuning playgrounds. Evaluation uses Pass@k (defined in the paper) with n = 15 trials. The authors selected 20 benchmark design problems (Table II) — common amplifiers, filters, current mirrors, cascode, differential stages, diode-connected designs, logic gate, etc. Dataset characterization includes distributions of #components, #nodes, #MOSFETs, and #lines of SPICE code (Fig. 9). Results show open-source models (DeepSeek, CodeLlama) underperform proprietary GPT models, and the fine-tuned GPT-4o achieved the best result, successfully generating SPICE netlists for all evaluated design IDs. Figure 10 highlights representative success and failure cases (e.g., NMOS used improperly as a current source, diode-connected transistor gate miswired); fine-tuning resolved issues like floating nets. Further analyses are in the paper appendices.


---

## pqac-00000044

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 4-5'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

Masala-CHAI is an automated pipeline that extracts schematics and related text from PDF documents and generates SPICE netlists. The system performs component detection, net clustering, and creates annotated schematics (Fig. 6) before producing netlists (Fig. 5, Fig. 8). The authors collected ~7,500 schematic images from 10 textbooks and applied the Masala-CHAI framework to produce structurally correct netlists, marking each component with default parameters. Prompt tuning is used to steer LLM behavior; example prompts instruct an "expert analog designer" to list components, map MOSFET terminals using red net numbers, and write a SPICE netlist. Because automatic generation can produce errors (e.g., floating nets), Masala-CHAI includes a final verification step that calls an LLM on the extracted netlist to identify and correct common issues (removing floating nets). The paper highlights prompt design and shows effectiveness with examples (Figures 3, 7), emphasizing automated dataset creation and LLM-based verification for large-scale netlist generation.


---

## pqac-00000045

From text 'bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 3-4'.

Jitendra Bhandari, Vineet Bhat, Yuheng He, Siddharth Garg, Hamed Rahmani, and Ramesh Karri. Masala-chai: a large-scale spice netlist dataset for analog circuits by harnessing ai. ArXiv, Nov 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.14299. This article has 26 citations.

The Masala-CHAI pipeline converts schematic images into SPICE netlists by combining computer-vision preprocessing with LLM-based netlist generation. The dataset is built by paginating texts into PDFs and extracting schematics using a fine-tuned YOLOv8 (Darknet53 backbone with CSP bottleneck), trained on ~4,300 annotated diagrams (resized to 640x640, 1000 epochs, lr=0.01) to detect 12 component classes. Net detection uses pre-trained deep Hough transform line priors; components are removed before line detection, then line segments are clustered into nets (heuristic: cluster segments whose endpoints fall within a 40-pixel radius, tuned and manually verified). The authors observed GPT-4o failure modes: assuming intersecting nets are connected, misidentifying MOSFET terminals (drain/gate/source), and mishandling differential I/O pairs. To mitigate these, they developed automatic net annotation using deep Hough priors and incorporated annotations into prompts, which reduced ambiguities and improved GPT-4o netlist accuracy. The process includes manual tuning/verification of clustering and uses annotations to assist the LLM in mapping components to correct net connections, improving SPICE generation reliability.


---

## pqac-00000046

From text 'fang2025asurveyof pages 61-62'.

Wenji Fang, Jing Wang, Yao Lu, Shang Liu, Yuchao Wu, Yuzhe Ma, and Zhiyao Xie. A survey of circuit foundation model: foundation ai models for vlsi circuit design and eda. ArXiv, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2504.03711. This article has 40 citations.

The excerpt lists numerous recent works applying large language models to EDA tasks relevant to converting schematics to SPICE/netlists and evaluating them. It cites LLM-assisted formal verification testbench generation (From rtl to sva), netlist-focused analysis and security (Netlist whisperer), and autonomous LLM agents for EDA workflows (Chateda, Drc-coder). Several entries address code generation for hardware description languages and SPICE-like outputs, and methods to improve generation quality (Vrank: self-consistency). There are domain-specific datasets and benchmarks (Circuitnet; Metrex) and projects for specification generation/review (SpecLLM). The bibliography also references evaluation and tuning approaches such as fine-tuning from human preferences. Together these works provide precedents for: (1) training/evaluating LLMs on schematic-to-netlist tasks using Circuitnet-like datasets and Verilog/SPICE benchmarks; (2) verification via generated formal testbenches and security/side-channel analyses; (3) quality-improvement techniques like self-consistency, retrieval-augmentation, RLHF-style fine-tuning, and autonomous agent orchestration for multi-step EDA pipelines. These citations are useful context when designing Masala-CHAI dataset splits, GPT-4 evaluation metrics, and verification workflows for SPICE netlist generation from schematic images.


---

## pqac-00000047

From text 'tao2024amsnetnetlistdataset pages 1-2'.

AMSNet: Netlist Dataset for AMS Circuits This article has 38 citations.

The paper introduces AMSNet, a dataset and generation tool that automatically converts schematic diagrams into netlists to support MLLM-driven AMS circuit design. The authors outline a pipeline of schematic collection, component detection, net recognition, and netlist generation, and assembled AMSNet-1.0 from literature sources. They note a key bottleneck: lack of datasets mapping schematics to netlists for AMS circuits, which are often specified at the transistor level (unlike modular digital circuits). The authors evaluated GPT-4 on tasks derived from AMSNet and report that GPT-4 achieved about 90% accuracy on component detection but only about 45% accuracy on transistor-level net detection, indicating current LLM limitations for extracting precise transistor-level SPICE-style netlists. AMSNet and its generation tool are publicly available to support further research and improve automatic AMS netlist extraction and synthesis.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000048

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The MenTeR methodology implements a simplified multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design that decomposes tasks into stages: specification understanding, document search, circuit netlist design, and test-bench generation. It uses a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that "integrates domain-specific knowledge with the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) technique," and employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)/DA-RAG for sourcing technical documents and textbooks to inform design decisions. A Meta Agent serves as a central orchestrator to analyze incoming design tasks, determine required specialist agents, decompose problems into sub-tasks, delegate to domain specialist agents, and oversee the workflow. The PI (Primary Investigator) agent interfaces with users, manages workflow, leverages DA-RAG to process requirements from multiple knowledge sources, and converts abstract requirements into well-defined specifications. The framework is intended to systematically collect reasoning paths (CoT) to enable future domain-adapted fine-tuning. Reported results indicate the multi-agent approach "already outperformed single-agent work" in their comparisons, and the authors note the framework can be further optimized for integration into existing design flows.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 1-2'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes MenTeR, a fully automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design. MenTeR composes multiple specialized AI agents that collaboratively handle specification understanding, document retrieval, testbench generation, schematic/netlist synthesis, and device sizing. The framework includes interactive checkpoints for optional human intervention and targets generation of single-block, multi-block, and ultimately system-level designs (e.g., BGR, PLL). Key methodological components to improve LLM reasoning and reliability are Diagram-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation (DA-RAG), self-referential techniques, and a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that combines domain-specific knowledge with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. The authors also leverage prompting strategies (e.g., references to Tree of Thoughts and RAG) and integrate literature analysis, mathematical reasoning, and device sizing agents to scale from simplified tasks to more complex amplifier and system designs. The excerpt claims MenTeR accelerates design cycle time, broadens the explored design space, and reduces manual trial-and-error, but it contains no quantitative results or detailed optimization metrics in the provided pages.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt reports MenTeR's multi-agent workflow performance, backbone comparisons, and engineering mitigations. Table III shows backbone results (GPT-4o: 84.2/89.2 Pass@1/5; GPT-4.1: 77.5/85.8). In a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) case study, AI-generated nets sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet "contained errors upon schematic inspection," primarily due to "incorrectly defined subcircuits (Frequency Detector (FD), Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO))" and "missing wire connections (VCO to FD, FD to Phase Frequency Detector (PFD), and PFD to the reference frequency source)." The authors recommend "maintaining a 'human-in-the-loop' approach" for complex system-level designs. To improve robustness, MenTeR incorporated "a self-referential technique into both multi-agent and MenTeR, ensuring all code is executable before submission," which "substantially decreased execution error rates." They also addressed "Repetitive Error Loops" by adding "an early-detection mechanism to identify such recurring errors, automatically terminating the chat when these patterns are detected." Token limits were handled by truncating chat history per agent (keeping the initial prompt and last message). On token efficiency, "MenTeR consumes more tokens for the relatively simple Task 1," but for harder tasks the single-agent approach used far more tokens and failed, indicating MenTeR's multi-agent design improved stability and task success on challenging problems.


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5'.

Pin-Han Chen, Yu-Sheng Lin, W. Lee, Tin-Yu Leu, Po-Hsiang Hsu, Anjana Dissanayake, Sungjin Oh, and Chinq-Shiun Chiu. Menter: a fully-automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end rf/analog circuits netlist design. 2025 IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), pages 124-132, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.22990. This article has 7 citations.

MenTeR is a multi-agent, end-to-end workflow for RF/analog netlist design that decomposes user requirements into assigned tasks (PI agent) and uses specialized agents for design, validation, and knowledge reuse. The Circuit Agent (CA) acts as a senior analog designer producing PySpice netlists via an internal Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning component and an Executor for syntax and basic error checks. CoS breaks design into sequential stages (requirement extraction, parameter synthesis, etc.), leverages a Circuit Think Tank (CTT) repository of validated designs and reasoning traces, and iteratively refines designs. Validated netlists are passed to the Testbench Agent (TBA), which—using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG)—defines simulations (DC sweeps, MOSFET/connection checks), generates Python code to run PySpice, and returns detailed errors for targeted refinement. All generated solutions are inspected with electrical rule checks and circuit simulations. Sub-agents (e.g., CoS Reasoning, DA-RAG) enable stagewise optimization and diagram-to-text retrieval. Evaluation uses pass@k metrics (Fig. 6) with limited attempts and GPT-4o for experiments, indicating a comparative performance analysis versus a single-agent baseline.


---

## pqac-00000052

From text 'wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 2-3'.

Zining Wang, Jian Gao, Weimin Fu, Xiaolong Guo, and Xuan Zhang. Analogsage: self-evolving analog design multi-agents with stratified memory and grounded experience. ArXiv, Dec 2512. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, a self-evolving, simulation-grounded framework for translating analog circuit specifications into executable netlist designs via an iterative, memory-augmented multi-agent workflow. The pipeline is driven by three stage agents: a Topology Selection Agent that converts textual performance specifications into a netlist and initializes designs via a Candidates Module, a Topology Refinement Agent that applies constrained, localized structural edits (e.g., bias or compensation adjustments), and a Parameter Optimization Agent that finalizes numerical device values. Reasoning and feedback are mediated by four Stratified Memory Layers — including Evolution Memory and Introspective Optimization — enabling cross-task knowledge transfer, self-correction from past iterations, and compressed stage context fusion. The system emphasizes retrieval-based initialization to start from validated topologies, iterative closed-loop simulation feedback, and maintaining structural validity through local edits. The authors position this approach as addressing prior limitations (over-reliance on heuristics, idealized models, limited context retention) and claim an open-source, simulation-grounded pipeline that improves across iterations and tasks for practical analog (including RF) netlist synthesis and optimization.


---

## pqac-00000053

From text 'wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2'.

Zining Wang, Jian Gao, Weimin Fu, Xiaolong Guo, and Xuan Zhang. Analogsage: self-evolving analog design multi-agents with stratified memory and grounded experience. ArXiv, Dec 2512. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, an open-source, self-evolving multi-agent framework for analog circuit design that coordinates three sequential agent stages—topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization—within a closed-loop, simulation-grounded workflow. It introduces a four-layer stratified memory to preserve stage-relevant context and support long-horizon reasoning while discarding irrelevant content. AnalogSAGE combines LLM-based reasoning with numerical optimization for accurate device sizing and biasing, and it explicitly learns from failed iterations by generating new topologies and incorporating simulation feedback. The authors evaluated the framework on specification-driven amplifier design tasks using the SKY130 PDK with ngspice and report substantial improvements (10× overall pass rate, 48× Pass@1, and 4× reduction in parameter search space). The implementation is released to support reproducibility. The paper contrasts this approach with prior prompt/template-based or library methods that either generate structurally invalid circuits or fail to generalize, highlighting the importance of persistently grounded experience, stratified memory, and closed-loop simulator feedback for robust end-to-end analog/netlist design automation.


---

## pqac-00000054

From text 'wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 4-5'.

Zining Wang, Jian Gao, Weimin Fu, Xiaolong Guo, and Xuan Zhang. Analogsage: self-evolving analog design multi-agents with stratified memory and grounded experience. ArXiv, Dec 2512. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2512.22435. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, a multi-agent, modular pipeline for end-to-end analog circuit design that is relevant to netlist generation and parameter optimization. Key components include a Candidates Module backed by a Topology Vector Database that stores netlists paired with concise two-sentence textual summaries embedded for semantic retrieval; the embedded description serves as keys while the netlist is stored as the value. A Knowledge Module runs a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to inject domain literature into agent reasoning. Stage agents (Topology Selection Agent, Topology Refinement Agent, Parameter Optimization Agent) orchestrate topology selection, refinement, and parameter estimation. The Numerical Module applies Bayesian Optimization (BO), calls a circuit simulator, evaluates metrics, and updates a surrogate model—providing simulation-grounded feedback (noted as ground-truth numerical feedback) to guide decisions. The workflow includes candidate re-ranking based on inferred constraints and a failure-analysis step (Compression Module) to analyze shortfalls. An op-amp design walk-through and specification table (GBW, Phase Margin, CMRR, PSRR, power targets) illustrate iterative search and optimization. The description focuses on analog design broadly rather than RF-specific techniques.

Zihao Chen, Zhuang Ji, Jinyi Shen, Xiaoyue Ke, Xinyi Yang, Ming Zhou, Zhuoyao Du, Xu Yan, Zhouyang Wu, Zhenyu Xu, Jiangli Huang, Li Shang, Xuan Zeng, and Fan Yang. Analogseeker: an open-source foundation language model for analog circuit design. ArXiv, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2508.10409, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2508.10409. This article has 5 citations.

This excerpt describes an LLM-driven multi-agent design case study (AnalogSeeker) applied to a three-stage op-amp within the Atelier framework. The LLM agents are split into explicit roles: topology design, topology modification, and circuit analysis. Agents produce SPICE netlists and enumerate designable parameters and ranges. The task objective is to maximize FOM = GBW·CL / Power while meeting constraints (Gain > 85dB; GBW requirement noted as challenging; PM > 55 degrees; Power < 250 μW) with a specified load CL = 10 pF. The workflow is iterative with a back-tracking mechanism and a maximum of four iterations; Fig. 7 shows the design trajectory across iterations. In the example the topology agent initially selects an NMCF (nested Miller compensation with feedforward) topology, justified because it combines high gain and power efficiency and handles GBW/PM trade-offs for small load capacitances. The agents perform topology selection, modification, and convert designs into netlists for analysis and optimization. This provides a concrete example of an end-to-end multi-agent analog-circuit netlist-design loop, though the example focuses on op-amp (analog) rather than RF-specific circuits.

## pqac-00000056

From text 'poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2'.

Souradip Poddar, Ahmet Budak, Linran Zhao, Chen-Hao Hsu, Supriyo Maji, Keren Zhu, Yaoyao Jia, and David Z. Pan. A data-driven analog circuit synthesizer with automatic topology selection and sizing. 2024 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1-6, Mar 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

Poddar et al. (DATE 2024) present a data-driven analog circuit synthesizer that performs automatic topology selection and device sizing. The method uses a modular approach to build a comprehensive, parameterized circuit topology library and constructs an adaptive topology dataset rather than relying on an exhaustive precomputed set. A one-time data collection effort gathers simulation-based performance data for each topology; this dataset is then enriched with synthetic performance data generated using variational autoencoders (VAE) to improve coverage and density. For device sizing and performance data generation the authors employ automatic analog sizers and leverage DNN-Opt. The workflow ranks candidate topologies from the repository for a target specification, verifies the chosen topology, and performs automatic sizing and re-optimization. The approach is validated on operational amplifiers (OPAMPs), with experiments involving over 360 OPAMP topologies and over 540K data points, guided by a Figure of Merit (FOM). The paper reports that the system "can generate optimal-sized topology netlists in approximately 10 minutes," emphasizing scalability and reduced runtime compared to simulation-in-the-loop methods.

## pqac-00000057

From text 'poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 2-3'.

Souradip Poddar, Ahmet Budak, Linran Zhao, Chen-Hao Hsu, Supriyo Maji, Keren Zhu, Yaoyao Jia, and David Z. Pan. A data-driven analog circuit synthesizer with automatic topology selection and sizing. 2024 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1-6, Mar 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

The paper describes a data-driven flow for analog (OTA/OPAMP) synthesis that combines simulation-based sizing with VAE-generated synthetic performance data. They use DNN-Opt, a multi-threaded, online, learning-based optimizer (actor-critic + population methods) to size devices and build a simulation dataset. Designs are evaluated with a Figure-of-Merit (FoM) that combines an objective f0(x) and clipped constraint terms (FoM formula provided). The overall pipeline is two-stage: (1) performance dataset generation — a one-time effort that creates a parameterized topology library and a data repository per topology using DNN-Opt simulations augmented with VAE synthetic samples; and (2) topology selection, verification and automatic sizing leveraging that repository. The work focuses on OPAMPs/OTAs and enumerates a set of performance metrics used for queries. Topologies are generated modularly: common analog circuits are decomposed into parameterized modules (e.g., differential pairs, loads, tail current, bias, compensation) where MOSFET sizes act as parameters. VAEs are used as the generative model, trained using the ELBO, to enrich scarce simulation data with synthetic performance points.

A data-driven analog circuit synthesizer with automatic topology selection and sizing. 2024 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1-6, Mar 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

Poddar et al. (2024) present a data-driven analog synthesis flow that combines automated topology selection with device sizing. They introduce a constraint-based modular approach to create parameterized analog topology libraries from scratch, then collect simulation-based performance data and VAE-generated synthetic data in a one-time effort to capture each topology’s capabilities. The VAE (variational autoencoder) is used to generate synthetic samples that augment sparse simulation data, improving ranking and sizing. The method pairs binary search with synthetic-data-driven ranking, verification, and an initial data-driven sizing strategy, enabling production of optimal-sized topology netlists in minutes (e.g., a netlist in 6.87 minutes for a test case). The authors validated the approach across 360+ diverse OPAMP topologies and compared results in Table III: in several test cases binary search fails when nearby data are scarce, while the synthetic-data approach better identifies, ranks, and sizes topologies; in at least one case (test case 4) binary search outperformed the synthetic approach. The work shows VAE-augmented data and binary search are complementary for fast topology selection and sizing.

## pqac-00000059

From text 'poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4'.

A data-driven analog circuit synthesizer with automatic topology selection and sizing. 2024 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1-6, Mar 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840, doi:10.23919/date58400.2024.10546840. This article has 24 citations.

The paper describes an automated, modular OPAMP synthesis flow that uses SKILL automation to quickly build a parameterized library of standard topologies ("initial foundational library comprising approximately 4,000 valid standard parameterized OPAMP topologies"). Designers curated a representative subset of ~360 topologies for study. Each topology is sized using an automatic analog sizer (DNN-Opt) to obtain SPICE-based performance metrics; the authors limited simulations to ~1,000 data points per topology and used six objective/constraint configurations to increase diversity. They apply a constraint-based valid-topology filter (e.g., "merging an NMOS-input pair with a PMOS tail current would generate an invalid topology") to prune incompatible combinations. Two topology-selection paths are used: (1) Binary Search based Topology Selection over the real simulation dataset (sorted into metric lists; binary search O(log n)) to produce candidate topologies, and (2) Synthetic Data based Topology Ranking using variational autoencoders to generate synthetic performance points, compute a FoM, sort topologies, and take the top three for verification. Final verification and sizing invoke DNN-Opt on the ranked candidates and compare the final sized topology from the synthetic approach against the binary-search result by performance.

## pqac-00000061

From text 'zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4'.

Zhenxin Zhao and Lihong Zhang. Deep reinforcement learning for analog circuit structure synthesis. 2022 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1157-1160, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

The excerpt describes Zhao & Zhang's DRL framework for analog circuit topology synthesis. Topologies are built from building blocks (BBs); circuits that are structurally identical but have swapped input pins are treated as distinct, and the decoded structure should reproduce swapped-pin copies. Except for differential pairs, all transistors within a BB are constrained to equal length and width; two special nets-only BBs have I/O combinations dependent on input order. Decoded structures undergo a fast symbolic evaluation: the GPDD algorithm numerically computes DC gain of unsized circuits, and the quality filter is deliberately relaxed so only very poor designs are filtered out. Passing candidates are sized with a modified NSGA-II using SPICE (CMOS 65nm) to verify feasibility rather than optimize; population and generation sizes are 20 and 50, and the routine early-terminates and stores (performance, size) in a hash table when a candidate meets the target spec. DRL agent settings reported: state size 10, action size 20; PGNN with two hidden layers (300, 200 neurons); learning rate 0.002, discount 0.95, batch 200; initial state is a PMOS differential pair (BB 26). The method produces far fewer unique structures than prior brute-force methods.


---

## pqac-00000062

From text 'zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1'.

Zhenxin Zhao and Lihong Zhang. Deep reinforcement learning for analog circuit structure synthesis. 2022 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1157-1160, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699, doi:10.23919/date54114.2022.9774699. This article has 15 citations.

Zhao & Zhang (2022) propose a DRL-based approach for analog circuit structure (topology) synthesis that blends structure generation and reasoning. The method begins from a starting sub-circuit and incrementally expands topology using building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process guided by reinforcement learning; necessary design rules are encoded to create a specialized RL environment. Evaluation efficiency is improved by using hash tables and symbolic-analysis techniques: these reduce the number of produced topologies that require costly sizing/simulation and enable faster verification of generated circuits. The authors claim this is the first application of DRL to automate analog-IC structure synthesis. Key reported advantages include framing synthesis as a decision process, providing a flexible and controllable design search space, enabling the automatic learning of design knowledge, and substantially lowering computation compared with conventional structure-generation methods.


---
