(2024) | Masala-CHAI | arXiv:2411.14299 | b | Automated schematic-to-SPICE dataset creation from textbook diagrams using YOLOv8, net extraction, prompt tuning, and LLM verification | Fine-tuning on Masala-CHAI improves AnalogCoder Pass@1 by 46%; ~7,500 netlists with GED-based structural verification (pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042) | Component confusion such as NMOS versus PMOS and weak transistor-level net detection remain major issues; net detection can be ~45% accurate without stronger CV support (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000047) |
| 11 | Settaluri et al. (2020) | AutoCkt | arXiv:2001.01808 | c | PPO-based deep RL for analog sizing across multiple target specs with transfer from schematic to post-layout environments | 96.3% target convergence on unseen specs, ~40x faster than genetic algorithms, and 40 LVS-passed op-amps in layout-aware experiments (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000036) | Fixed-topology sizing only; requires simulator access and substantial environment engineering; not a topology generator (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000036) |
| 12 | Zhao and Zhang (2022) | DRL Circuit Structure Synthesis | DATE 2022, pp. 1157-1160 | c,d | DRL topology synthesis from predefined building blocks with policy-gradient network, hash-table caching, and symbolic analysis filtering | Early RL topology-synthesis framework; uses GPDD symbolic filtering and SPICE-based NSGA-II verification; explores novel structures with lower computation than older methods (pqac-00000062, pqac-00000061, pqac-00000063) | Constrained by building-block library and state encoding; stochastic results; runs can take 2-9 hours and still need downstream sizing (pqac-00000061, pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064) |
| 13 | Choi et al. (2024) | MA-Opt | IEEE TCAS-I 71(5):2045-2056 | c | Multi-actor RL for analog sizing with shared elite set, critic surrogate, and cooperative near-sampling | Up to 34% better FoM and target metrics than DNN-Opt at equal simulation budget; improves exploration and sample efficiency (pqac-00000037) | Sizing only, not topology synthesis; depends on an already valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (pqac-00000037) |
| 14 | Poddar et al. (2024) | Data-Driven Synthesizer | DATE 2024, pp. 1-6 | a,d | Modular parameterized topology library, VAE synthetic data augmentation, binary-search and synthetic ranking, plus DNN-Opt verification and sizing | Evaluated on 360+ OPAMP topologies and 540K+ data points; generates optimal-sized topology netlists in about 10 minutes; synthetic data helps when real samples are sparse (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000058, pqac-00000059) | Requires significant one-time simulation and data collection; limited to OPAMP and OTA families in curated libraries (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000059) |
| 15 | Guo et al. (2019) | GAN Circuit Synthesis | AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum | a | GAN, WGAN, and improved WGAN generation of circuit-topology adjacency encodings for frequency-response and low-pass-filter synthesis | Improved WGAN reached up to 42.66% feasibility in the frequency-response case and beat GAN, DCGAN, and WGAN baselines (pqac-00000074, pqac-00000078) | Feasibility remains relatively low; graph-isomorphism and representation issues persist; no spec-conditioned or manufacturability-aware generation loop (pqac-00000076, pqac-00000078) |


*Table: This table summarizes 15 influential 2019-2025 references on automated and generative circuit-topology synthesis, netlist generation, and analog design agents. It is useful for comparing methods, results, and practical limitations when building a code-based electronics design pipeline.*

---

## Detailed Reference Summaries

### Category (a): ML and Generative Models for Analog Circuit Topology Generation

**1. Chang, C.-C., Shen, Y., Fan, S., Li, J., Zhang, S., Cao, N., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2024). "LaMAGIC: Language-Model-based Topology Generation for Analog Integrated Circuits." arXiv:2407.18269.**

LaMAGIC pioneers the use of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a Flan-T5 (248M parameters) encoder-decoder model to generate analog circuit topologies from user-specified performance targets (e.g., voltage conversion ratio, efficiency) in a single forward pass (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000013). The system represents circuits as graphs encoded via adjacency matrices and develops multiple input/output formulations — including a float-input adjacency-matrix formulation (FM) — that align with the autoregressive nature of language models. Experimental results on power converter circuits demonstrate a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01 (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009). The model is pretrained on 3–5 component circuits and transferred to 6-component designs using a dataset of 76K sampled circuits (pqac-00000014). **Relevance to dosing device:** Demonstrates that specification-to-topology generation is feasible via LMs, a paradigm applicable to generating power converter sub-circuits (e.g., stepper driver power stages). **Limitations:** Restricted to small power converter topologies (3–6 passive/switching components); synthetic evaluation only; no direct applicability to mixed-signal blocks like load-cell amplifiers (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000014).

**2. Chang, C.-C., Lin, W.-H., Shen, Y., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2025). "LaMAGIC2: Advanced Circuit Formulations for Language Model-Based Analog Topology Generation." arXiv:2506.10235.**

LaMAGIC2 introduces the Succinct Float-Input Canonical Formulation with Identifier (SFCI), which reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|²) to O(|V|) and adds component-type identifiers for better differentiation (pqac-00000012). This yields 34% higher success rates under tight tolerance (0.01) and 10× lower MSE compared to the original LaMAGIC, with substantially shorter output sequences (~36–41 tokens vs. ~85–105) (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000015). **Relevance:** Improved scalability and precision make this formulation more practical for generating larger circuit blocks. **Limitations:** Still confined to power converter topologies; O(|V|) scaling, while improved, has not been demonstrated on complex mixed-signal circuits (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000015).

**3. Gao, J., Cao, W., Yang, J., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogGenie: A Generative Engine for Automatic Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies." arXiv:2503.00205.**

AnalogGenie is a GPT-based generative engine that represents circuits at the device-pin level using an Eulerian-circuit encoding of directed graphs, trained on a dataset of 3,350 distinct topologies across 11 circuit families (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000003). The pin-level representation captures low-level design details and scales to larger graphs better than adjacency-matrix methods. Data augmentation via multiple Eulerian serializations per topology yields ~70× more training data (pqac-00000031). AnalogGenie demonstrates zero-shot generation of circuit types not present in training (e.g., transconductance amplifiers) and produces novel topologies validated in Cadence Virtuoso, including op-amps (GBW=12 MHz), DC converters (efficiency=0.95), and bandgap references (TC=3 ppm/°C) (pqac-00000081). **Relevance:** The ability to discover novel topologies across diverse circuit families (including amplifiers and references) is directly applicable to generating load-cell front-end and voltage reference sub-circuits. **Limitations:** Requires a large curated dataset; specification conditioning is indirect (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005).

**4. Guo, T., Herber, D. R., and Allison, J. T. (2019). "Circuit Synthesis Using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)." AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum. DOI: 10.2514/6.2019-2350.**

This early work applies GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) to generate circuit topologies encoded as adjacency matrices. Using datasets of 43K–123K enumerated topologies for frequency-response matching and low-pass filter realizability, the improved WGAN achieved the highest feasibility rate of up to 42.66% after 50,000 training iterations (pqac-00000074, pqac-00000078). **Relevance:** Establishes the foundational concept of using generative models for circuit topology search, relevant to exploring filter and signal-conditioning topologies. **Limitations:** Relatively low feasibility rates; graph isomorphism problems in fixed-size adjacency representations; no specification conditioning or manufacturability awareness (pqac-00000076, pqac-00000078).

**5. Poddar, S., Budak, A., Zhao, L., Hsu, C.-H., Maji, S., Zhu, K., Jia, Y., and Pan, D. Z. (2024). "A Data-Driven Analog Circuit Synthesizer with Automatic Topology Selection and Sizing." DATE 2024, pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.23919/DATE58400.2024.10546840.**

This work presents a modular, data-driven framework that combines a parameterized OPAMP topology library (~4,000 valid topologies, ~360 curated), VAE-based synthetic data augmentation, and DNN-Opt sizing to automatically select and size topologies from performance specifications (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000059). The system generates optimal-sized netlists in approximately 10 minutes using over 540K data points (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000058). **Relevance:** The modular library approach and specification-driven selection are directly applicable to choosing amplifier topologies for load-cell signal conditioning. **Limitations:** Requires a costly one-time data-collection effort; limited to OPAMP/OTA families (pqac-00000056, pqac-00000059).

---

### Category (b): LLM-Based and Agentic Generation of Schematics and Netlists

**6. Lai, Y., Lee, S., Chen, G., Poddar, S., Hu, M., Pan, D. Z., and Luo, P. (2025). "AnalogCoder: Analog Circuit Design via Training-Free Code Generation." arXiv:2405.14918.**

AnalogCoder is the first training-free LLM agent that reframes analog circuit design as Python (PySpice) code generation (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020). It uses domain-specific prompts, a four-stage feedback loop (requirement checks, simulation/operating-point checks, DC sweep checks, functional checks), and a circuit tool library that archives successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020). AnalogCoder solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, outperforming standard GPT-4o (15 solved) and Llama-3 (11 solved) (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020). **Relevance:** The PySpice code-generation paradigm and modular sub-circuit library are directly transferable to generating driver electronics: one could encode stepper-driver half-bridges, motor H-bridges, or instrumentation amplifier blocks as library components and compose them via LLM-generated code. **Limitations:** Struggles with highly complex circuits; emphasizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; requires external SPICE simulation for validation; a later evaluation (AnalogCoder-Pro) showed only 10% pass rate on harder specification-driven benchmarks (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000021).

**7. Zhang, H., Sun, S., Lin, Y., Wang, R., and Bian, J. (2025). "AnalogXpert: Automating Analog Topology Synthesis by Incorporating Circuit Design Expertise into Large Language Models." ISEDA 2025, pp. 772–777. arXiv:2412.19824.**

AnalogXpert formulates topology synthesis as subcircuit-level SPICE code generation and incorporates circuit design expertise through a subcircuit library, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, in-context learning examples, and iterative human-experience-based proofreading (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000004). On a benchmark of 30 real and 2,000 synthetic tasks, AnalogXpert achieved 40% and 23% success rates respectively, markedly outperforming GPT-4o at 3%/3% (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000022). **Relevance:** The SPICE-level output and subcircuit decomposition align well with practical PCB-level design (e.g., decomposing a motor driver into gate driver, half-bridge, current sense subcircuits). **Limitations:** Connection errors and wrong block selections remain common failure modes; the real dataset is small (30 tasks) (pqac-00000022).

**8. Vijayaraghavan, P., Shi, L., Degan, E., and Zhang, X. (2024). "CircuitSynth: Leveraging Large Language Models for Circuit Topology Synthesis." IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.1109/LAD62341.2024.10691716.**

CircuitSynth introduces a two-phase pipeline: Phase 1 fine-tunes an LLM to generate netlist-encoded topologies autoregressively, and Phase 2 applies a RoBERTa-based validity classifier (F1 ≈ 92%) with Gumbel-softmax refinement to steer generation toward valid circuits (pqac-00000066, pqac-00000069). Trained on 862,606 power-converter examples, the best models achieved ~71–73% SPICE-validated topology validity (pqac-00000071, pqac-00000073). **Relevance:** The validity-classifier refinement concept could be adapted to filter LLM-generated netlists for driver electronics against electrical rule checks. **Limitations:** Restricted to power converters; no specification-conditioned generation (pqac-00000066, pqac-00000073).

**9. Chen, P.-H., Lin, Y.-S., Lee, W., Leu, T.-Y., Hsu, P.-H., Dissanayake, A., Oh, S., and Chiu, C.-S. (2025). "MenTeR: A Fully-Automated Multi-Agent Workflow for End-to-End RF/Analog Circuits Netlist Design." IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124–132. arXiv:2505.22990.**

MenTeR deploys a multi-agent architecture comprising a Primary Investigator (PI) agent for specification understanding, a Circuit Agent for netlist synthesis via Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning, and a Testbench Agent for simulation validation using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG) (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000051). With GPT-4o as backbone, MenTeR achieved 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5, supporting end-to-end design from specification to validated PySpice netlist and testbench (pqac-00000050). **Relevance:** The multi-agent spec-to-netlist-to-testbench pipeline is the closest paradigm to what would be needed for an automated dosing-device electronics generator — one agent interprets requirements (e.g., "4 stepper channels, 24V, load-cell excitation"), another generates netlists, and a third validates via simulation. **Limitations:** AI-generated nets can contain subcircuit definition errors and missing wires at system level, necessitating human-in-the-loop review for complex designs (pqac-00000050).

**10. Wang, Z., Gao, J., Fu, W., Guo, X., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogSAGE: Self-Evolving Analog Design Multi-Agents with Stratified Memory and Grounded Experience." arXiv:2512.22435.**

AnalogSAGE coordinates three sequential agent stages — topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization — within a closed-loop, simulation-grounded workflow augmented by four-layer stratified memory for long-horizon reasoning and cross-task knowledge transfer (pqac-00000052, pqac-00000053). Using SKY130 PDK and ngspice, AnalogSAGE reports a 10× overall pass-rate improvement and 48× Pass@1 improvement over baselines, with Bayesian optimization for parameter tuning (pqac-00000053, pqac-00000054). **Relevance:** The self-evolving, simulation-grounded approach with memory-based learning could enable iterative improvement of driver circuit designs across multiple dosing channels. **Limitations:** Currently demonstrated only on op-amp designs in SKY130; recent preprint with limited independent validation (pqac-00000053).

**11. Bhandari, J., Bhat, V., He, Y., Garg, S., Rahmani, H., and Karri, R. (2024). "Masala-CHAI: A Large-Scale SPICE Netlist Dataset for Analog Circuits by Harnessing AI." arXiv:2411.14299.**

Masala-CHAI provides an automated pipeline for extracting SPICE netlists from circuit schematic images in textbooks, using YOLOv8 for component detection and deep Hough-transform priors for net recognition, followed by LLM-based netlist generation and verification (pqac-00000040, pqac-00000045). The open-sourced dataset of ~7,500 netlists from 10 textbooks enables LLM fine-tuning; models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI improved AnalogCoder's Pass@1 by 46% (pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041). Structural correctness is verified via Graph Edit Distance (GED), achieving 100% similarity with AMSNet reference netlists (pqac-00000042). **Relevance:** The dataset and pipeline could be extended to extract and catalog common control electronics circuits (H-bridges, stepper drivers, instrumentation amplifiers) from open-source hardware documentation, creating training data for a dosing-device-specific generator. **Limitations:** Component confusion (NMOS vs. PMOS) and weak transistor-level net detection (~45% accuracy without CV support) remain significant challenges (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000047).

---

### Category (c): GNN and RL Approaches to Circuit Synthesis and Device Sizing

**12. Dong, Z., Cao, W., Zhang, M., Tao, D., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2023). "CktGNN: Circuit Graph Neural Network for Electronic Design Automation." arXiv:2308.16406.**

CktGNN uses a two-level (nested) GNN architecture where inner GNNs encode subgraph-basis components and an outer directed GNN performs message passing over the transformed circuit DAG (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000030). Integrated into a VAE framework, CktGNN jointly addresses topology generation and device sizing. The authors release the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) with 10K distinct operational amplifiers. In experiments, CktGNN achieves ~91.9% valid-circuit generation rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson correlation r ≈ 0.806, and outperforms human expert designs in Bayesian-optimization-guided search (pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028). **Relevance:** The GNN+VAE approach to joint topology-and-sizing optimization is applicable to amplifier blocks in a dosing device (e.g., load-cell instrumentation amplifiers, analog front-ends). The OCB benchmark and open-source code support reproducible research. **Limitations:** Benchmark is op-amp-centric with idealized component models; not directly demonstrated on mixed-signal driver circuits (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026).

**13. Settaluri, K., Haj-Ali, A., Huang, Q., Hakhamaneshi, K., and Nikolic, B. (2020). "AutoCkt: Deep Reinforcement Learning of Analog Circuit Designs." arXiv:2001.01808.**

AutoCkt formulates analog circuit sizing as a multi-objective RL problem using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), where the agent observes simulated performance and target specifications and iteratively adjusts device parameters (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033). Trained on only 50 target specifications, AutoCkt generalizes to 96.3% of 1,000 unseen targets with an average of 27 simulations to converge — approximately 40× faster than genetic algorithms (pqac-00000034). Transfer learning from schematic to post-layout (PEX) environments produced 40 LVS-passed operational amplifier layouts in TSMC 16nm (pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036). **Relevance:** Once a topology is selected for a dosing-device sub-circuit (e.g., a transimpedance amplifier for load-cell readout), AutoCkt-style RL sizing can rapidly optimize component values to meet gain, bandwidth, and noise specifications. **Limitations:** Fixed-topology sizing only; requires access to a SPICE simulator and environment engineering; not a topology generator (pqac-00000032).

**14. Zhao, Z. and Zhang, L. (2022). "Deep Reinforcement Learning for Analog Circuit Structure Synthesis." DATE 2022, pp. 1157–1160. DOI: 10.23919/DATE54114.2022.9774699.**

This work is among the first to apply deep RL directly to analog circuit topology synthesis (rather than just sizing). A policy-gradient neural network (PGNN) incrementally constructs topologies by selecting building blocks from a predefined library, with hash tables and GPDD symbolic analysis used to efficiently filter and evaluate candidates before SPICE-based NSGA-II verification (pqac-00000062, pqac-00000061). The method requires substantially less computation than brute-force enumeration approaches like MOJITO (pqac-00000063). **Relevance:** The building-block-based topology construction paradigm could be adapted for driver electronics by defining blocks such as half-bridges, gate drivers, current mirrors, and feedback networks. **Limitations:** Constrained by the predefined building-block library; stochastic results requiring multiple runs (2–9 hours each); downstream sizing still needed (pqac-00000061, pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064).

**15. Choi, Y., Park, S., Choi, M., Lee, K., and Kang, S. (2024). "MA-Opt: Reinforcement Learning-Based Analog Circuit Optimization Using Multi-Actors." IEEE TCAS-I, 71(5):2045–2056. DOI: 10.1109/TCSI.2024.3356582.**

MA-Opt uses multiple parallel RL actors with a shared elite-solution memory and a cooperative near-sampling strategy to optimize analog circuit device parameters. A critic network serves as a surrogate simulator to reduce expensive SPICE calls (pqac-00000037). MA-Opt achieves up to 34% better Figure-of-Merit than DNN-Opt at the same simulation budget and consistently satisfies all given performance constraints (pqac-00000037). **Relevance:** Multi-actor RL sizing is applicable to optimizing component values in motor driver and signal-conditioning circuits where multiple performance targets (efficiency, thermal, bandwidth) must be simultaneously satisfied. **Limitations:** Sizing-only; requires a valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (pqac-00000037).

---

## Cross-Cutting Analysis: Capabilities, Limitations, and Relevance to a Dosing-Device Electronics Pipeline

### Capabilities
The surveyed methods collectively demonstrate that: (1) LM-based approaches can achieve high success rates (up to 96%) for specification-conditioned topology generation of well-defined circuit families (pqac-00000009); (2) training-free LLM agents like AnalogCoder and MenTeR can generate functional PySpice/SPICE netlists with multi-stage feedback verification, solving 20+/24 benchmark tasks (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000050); (3) GNN+VAE methods enable joint topology-sizing optimization with >91% validity rates (pqac-00000027); and (4) RL-based sizing converges to meet specifications on 96%+ of unseen targets with 40× speedup over genetic algorithms (pqac-00000034).

### Hallucination and Validity
A recurring challenge across LLM-based methods is the generation of structurally invalid or electrically incorrect circuits. AnalogCoder requires multi-stage simulation-based feedback to catch floating nodes and incorrect operating points (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000006). MenTeR found that AI-generated netlists sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet contained errors upon schematic inspection, including incorrectly defined subcircuits and missing wire connections (pqac-00000050). AnalogXpert reports persistent connection errors and wrong block selections even with iterative proofreading (pqac-00000022). These findings underscore the necessity of SPICE-level simulation verification in any generative pipeline.

### Manufacturability
Most methods operate at the schematic level and do not address layout parasitics, PCB-level constraints, or component availability (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000006). AutoCkt is a notable exception, demonstrating transfer to post-layout (PEX) environments with LVS-passed designs (pqac-00000036). For a dosing device using discrete, off-the-shelf components (DRV8825 stepper drivers, HX711 load-cell ADCs, etc.), the gap between schematic-level generation and a manufacturable BOM/PCB is substantial — generated topologies would need to be mapped to specific component footprints, validated against datasheets, and checked for thermal and EMC compliance.

### Relevance to Dosing-Device Control Electronics
The control electronics for a multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end) represent a system-level design challenge that spans digital control logic and multiple analog/mixed-signal sub-circuits. The most directly applicable paradigms are: (1) **Multi-agent agentic workflows** (MenTeR, AnalogSAGE) that decompose a system specification into sub-blocks and generate, validate, and iterate on each block's netlist (pqac-00000048, pqac-00000053); (2) **Code-generation agents** (AnalogCoder) that produce executable PySpice code from natural-language descriptions, enabling composition of modular sub-circuits such as H-bridges and instrumentation amplifiers (pqac-00000019); (3) **Specification-driven topology selectors** (Poddar et al.) that rank candidate topologies from a parameterized library against target specifications and automatically size them (pqac-00000056); and (4) **RL-based optimizers** (AutoCkt, MA-Opt) for downstream component-value tuning once topologies are selected (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000037). A practical pipeline would likely combine these approaches: an LLM agent interprets system requirements and selects/generates topology candidates, a GNN or RL module optimizes device parameters, and simulation-in-the-loop verification catches hallucinated or invalid designs before output.

### Key Gaps
No surveyed work has demonstrated end-to-end generation of complete, manufacturable PCB-level control electronics from specifications. Current methods are largely validated on IC-level analog circuits (op-amps, power converters, bandgap references) rather than board-level mixed-signal systems with discrete components. Bridging this gap — from transistor-level IC topology to board-level system design with real component libraries — remains an open challenge for the field (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000001).

contexts.context: The excerpt outlines capabilities and limitations of learning- and graph-based automation relevant to automated circuit topology generation. GNNs are effective because circuits map naturally to non‑Euclidean graphs and can be combined with VAEs to generate synthetic training data. RL frameworks (L2DC, RoSE, CRONus, AutoCkt, fRL-AD) can learn constraint satisfaction, PVT-aware sizing, and multi-objective tradeoffs using simulator-derived observations (Hspice, Cadence Spectre). Hybrid methods and surrogate neural simulators accelerate exploration and have been industrially validated (TSMC 5nm/6nm, 16-nm FinFET) and report concrete benefits (e.g., saving "over 16 days" of computation; "outperforms human designers" on area/time). Evaluation commonly uses SPICE-level simulation, PVT-corner verification, layout-aware validation, and technology-node demonstrations. Identified limitations include schematic-only verification that may not generalize to unseen topologies, reliance on surrogate or small-signal models that can fail for strongly nonlinear components, and data/sampling challenges. The use of VAEs and surrogate models implies a risk of generating unrealistic or inaccurate designs if not rigorously validated across PVT and layout parasitics.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, a training-free LLM-based analog topology synthesis tool that uses a subcircuit-based representation, chain-of-thought and in‑context learning, plus human experience–based proofreading to mimic human design steps. Evaluation used both real and synthetic benchmarks focused on structural requirements; reported correct ratios are 40% on real data and 23% on synthetic data. Figure 5 shows examples of successful and failed generated circuit diagrams. Observed failure modes include connection errors (e.g., floating current outputs, inputs tied to incorrect terminals), block-type errors (e.g., wrong cascode stage type, NMOS vs PMOS mistakes), and invalid stage connections. Limitations noted include a small real dataset; the paper suggests using AnalogXpert to produce high-quality synthetic SPICE-format topologies to fine-tune smaller local models, which could improve performance and protect proprietary design data. Human proofreading is used to mitigate errors, implying a nontrivial risk of structural hallucinations in the generated topologies.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: In this work, we propose AnalogXpert, a powerful analog topology synthesis tool based on training-free LLMs.

AnalogXpert leverages the subcircuit-based circuit representation, CoT&in-context learning, and human experience-based proofreading to imitate the human design process and im-prove the design accuracy.
The experimental results (40% and 23% correct ratio in real and synthetic data) demonstrate the effectiveness of the AnalogXpert.
Figure 5: Three real cases with successful circuit diagrams and failed circuit diagrams.
Connection Error: Floating current output(I/O)、current input(I/I) terminal
Block Error: Wrong Cascode stage type, NMOS not PMOS
Connection Error: The second stage input (V) connected to a voltage only terminal
Limitation and Future Directions. Although this work has constructed both a real dataset and a synthetic dataset, the size of the real dataset is still small.

AnalogXpert can be used to generate sufficient synthetic data of the analog circuit topologies in the SPICE code format. With high-quality synthetic data, some small models can be fine-tuned to achieve a competitive performance.
Having fine-tuned mini-models that can be run locally ensures the security of design data, which is important for commercial circuit design companies.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes prior automated topology synthesis approaches and the AnalogXpert system. AnalogXpert formulates topology synthesis as SPICE code generation and uses an extensible subcircuit library to reduce design space. It decomposes tasks into block selection and block connection (CoT-based) and applies a proofreading/iterative-refinement strategy; with several rounds of self-refinement LLM agents can avoid some basic mistakes. AnalogXpert is evaluated on a new benchmark containing 30 real tasks and 2k synthetic tasks, achieving 40% success on synthetic and 23% on real data, compared to GPT-4o's 3%/3%. Prior methods: RLATS uses RL with a subcircuit library but faces transfer difficulty across circuit types due to design diversity; CKTGNN trains on a 10k dataset but relies on an ideal model that cannot represent the full legalized design space or be directly converted to final topologies; LaMAGIC uses a 120k synthetic dataset but focuses on simpler RF tasks with limited device types; AnalogCoder used prompt-based LLMs on a few real cases. The excerpt notes limited real-data evaluation in prior works (≤5) and the complexity of analog circuits (0–50 basic devices), motivating AnalogXpert's formulation and benchmark.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC, a language-model-based topology generation approach that uses supervised finetuning (SFT) to adapt a pretrained LM to analog circuit design. LaMAGIC is presented as capable of producing an optimized circuit topology in a single pass ("one iteration") rather than many simulator-heavy search iterations, addressing a shortcoming of prior search-based/RL methods. The work reports a high empirical success rate—"up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01"—and evaluates scalability and adaptability by testing on more complex circuits. A key design choice is an adjacency matrix–based circuit formulation intended to produce canonical graph-like representations aligned with the autoregressive nature of LMs, which the authors claim improves handling of intricate designs. The excerpt notes prior methods are time-consuming due to many simulation iterations. The excerpt does not discuss other systems (AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, AnalogXpert), detailed failure modes, or explicit analyses of hallucination risk; hallucination potential and cross-system comparisons are not addressed in the provided text.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie and contrasts it with prior systems (AnalogCoder, CktGNN, LaMAGIC). The authors built a large dataset (>3000 distinct topologies, augmented >70×) to train generative models. AnalogGenie is a GPT-based engine that generates circuits by predicting the next device pin, using a sequence-style, pin-level graph representation: each topology is an undirected graph with device pins as nodes, sequentialized into an Eulerian trail. This representation is claimed to capture low-level design details and scale to large graphs. By contrast, AnalogCoder uses high-level text representations that "use multiple tokens to describe a single connection", which the authors say makes generation "prone to errors" (implying higher hallucination/error risk). CktGNN and LaMAGIC use fixed-node graph representations and one-shot adjacency-matrix prediction, which "ignore[] the critical low-level details" and "suffer[] from low scalability," causing ambiguous or unscalable outputs. The paper reports that AnalogGenie produces "far more, large-scale, valid, unseen, and high-performance topologies" than prior graph- and foundation-model methods, but the excerpt provides no numerical success rates or detailed evaluation metrics. AnalogXpert is not mentioned in the excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: we build an extensive dataset that consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies
apply data augmentation techniques to expand these circuit topologies by over 70×.
AnalogCoder (Lai et al., 2024) relies on high-level text representations that use multiple tokens to describe a single connection between devices, making the generation prone to errors.
CktGNN (Dong et al., 2023) and LaMAGIC (Chang et al., 2024) use graph-based representations with a fixed number of nodes, where each node represents a circuit device or subgraph.
This ignores the critical low-level details essential in analog circuit design, leading to ambiguous and unscalable circuit generation.
We propose a scalable sequence-style data structure that captures fundamental analog circuit design details while efficiently describing large circuit graphs.
we represent each circuit topology as an undirected graph where each node is a device pin
We then sequentialize it into an Eulerian circuit–a trail that visits every edge exactly once and starts and ends at the same node.
AnalogGenie, built on a GPT model to generate diverse analog circuits by predicting the next device pin to connect in the circuit;
sequence-based, pin-level graph representation that efficiently and expressively captures large analog circuit topologies;

Experiment results show that AnalogGenie is capable of automatically generating far more, large-scale, valid, unseen, and high-performance topologies compared to existing graph generation and foundation model work.
they rely on one-shot generation by predicting the adjacency matrix directly and pre-define the number of nodes for generation, thereby suffering from low scalability (Zhu et al., 2022).

contexts.context: The excerpt summarizes recent automated topology-generation and analog-design systems and their approaches. LaMAGIC fine-tunes LLMs for analog topology generation (notably power converters) and uses structured input–output representations to improve synthesis accuracy. Analog-Coder is a training-free, Python-based generation approach relying on prompt engineering and feedback loops. AnalogGenie is a generative-AI framework trained on a large corpus (over 3,000 analog circuits). AnalogXpert reduces design space via a subcircuit library and applies CoT prompting plus iterative proofreading to boost accuracy. ADO-LLM pairs Bayesian Optimization (Gaussian Process models) with LLMs for guided design-space exploration. LADAC provides an LLM-driven decision agent with a knowledge library and interactive tools for transistor sizing and simulation. Methods used across these works include fine-tuning, prompt engineering, in-context learning, RL/Bayesian optimization, CoT/ToT reasoning, dataset-driven generation, and iterative feedback. The excerpt notes that analog design “requires tuning and optimization based on human expertise, making LLM-assisted automation more challenging.” It does not report quantitative success rates. For hallucination mitigation, multi-agent decomposition and specialized agents, as well as iterative proofreading, are mentioned as tactics to reduce hallucinations and improve reasoning accuracy. Evaluation is implied via simulation, transistor sizing, topology synthesis, and design-space exploration, but explicit metrics or success-rate numbers are not provided in the excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes several ML approaches relevant to automated circuit topology generation. GNNs and VAEs are used for topology exploration and generation (e.g., a VAE built 360 op-amp topologies; CktGNN encodes circuits with a two-level GNN and provides the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) for evaluation). LLM-based agents such as AnalogCoder are training-free, open-source tools that generate PySpice code and can rapidly produce schematic-level designs; AnalogCoder achieved 20/24 successful generations but struggles with high-complexity blocks and lacks topology-aware and post-layout parasitic management. Methods addressing parasitics include ParaGraph, BagNet (a DNN discriminator to reduce simulations), and GNN+BO predictors. Evaluation approaches cited include open benchmarks (OCB, ACOB references), experimental comparisons to strong LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o), and simulation-based verification loops. Limitations highlighted: many ML models verify only at schematic level and may not generalize to unseen topologies; LLMs struggle with precise mathematical calculations and thus often require generated code and external simulation to verify designs, implying a nontrivial risk of hallucinated or incorrect designs without verification. The excerpt does not contain details on LaMAGIC, AnalogGenie, or AnalogXpert specifically.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: GNNs have demonstrated significant potential in EDA by processing non-Euclidean data, which naturally represents circuit netlists as graphs.
Poddar et al. achieve joint topology optimization using a VAE to build an adaptive dataset of 360 op-amp topologies, as detailed in [22].

CktGNN [106] encodes circuit graphs using a two-level GNN framework (nested GNN). CktGNN represents circuits as combinations of subgraphs in a known subgraph basis. It additionally introduces Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) as an open-sourced dataset to assess and reproduce the results.

AnalogCoder: Lai et al. present a training-free, open-source LLM agent that automates analog circuit design through PySpice code generation, achieving rapid initial design with prompt engineering and a feedback-enhanced workflow [28].

AnalogCoder automates schematic-level tasks, yet the absence of topology-aware design and post-layout parasitic management limits its capability to accurately predict or mitigate parasitic or PVT variations.

Experimental results show that AnalogCoder successfully generates 20 out of 24 targeted circuits, surpassing the performance of models like GPT-4o, yet it fails in high-complexity designs such as op-amp integrators and voltage-controlled oscillators.
Ren et al. propose a custom message passing function for their GNN, named ParaGraph, to predict layout-dependent parasitics [60].
Liu et al. avoid layout generation by predicting parasitics using GNNs and BO [63].
BagNet integrates this discriminator into the optimization loop, learning from past designs to improve sample efficiency.

ADO-LLM addresses complex circuit sizing by integrating BO with LLM in-context learning, making it well-suited for layout and parasitic-aware circuit sizing [30]. The model can infer design principles from a few example circuit layouts and their associated performance metrics.

Practitioners should be aware of certain limitations when applying LLMs to this field: LLMs struggle with precise mathematical calculations, often requiring instructions to generate code that can verify or test their o

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that modern automated analogue circuit topology and layout systems often combine LLM reasoning with iterative verification and domain adaptation. AnalogCoder is a training-free, iterative agent using four feedback stages (requirements, simulation & operating point, DC sweep, function) and solves 20/24 benchmark tasks, outperforming baseline LLMs. GLayout uses fine-tuned LLMs plus RAG for analogue layout from natural-language descriptions; its 22B model completes 70% of tasks but only passes DRC/LVS verification in 44% of cases, and authors note limited training data (<50 circuits). Domain adaptation techniques (DAPT, domain-adaptive tokenization) and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improve domain performance, though DAPT can reduce open-domain benchmark scores. Artisan (op-amp design) shows domain-specific training and structured representations (NetlistTuple) boost success versus optimisation baselines. Evaluation methods reported include task-specific benchmarks, DRC/LVS checks, functional simulation stages, and comparisons to baselines (BOBO, RLBO, other LLMs). A recurring limitation is verification and correctness (necessitating testbenches, sequential equivalence checking); this implies risk of incorrect or ‘hallucinated’ designs unless mitigated by iterative feedback, retrieval, fine-tuning, and formal/functional verification steps.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: AnalogCoder, a training-free AI agent that, through an iterative, feedback-enhanced process, designs analogue circuits [19].
The provided feedback is divided into four steps: requirements, simulation & operating point, Direct Current (DC) sweep, and function.
The benchmark consists of 24 different analogue circuit designs divided into three difficulty levels: easy, medium, and hard.
AnalogCoder solved 20 out of 24 tasks on this benchmark, standard GPT-4o solved 15, and LLaMA 3-70B solved 11.
Verification remains a challenge, with traditional methods such as self-verifying test benches and sequential equivalence checking providing a baseline for correctness evaluation.
GLayout framework, a new LLM method for analogue layout generation from human language description [4].
They fine-tuned the LLM and used RAG to retrieve and apply relevant design knowledge dynamically.
The 22 billion model successfully completes 70% of the tasks and achieves Design Rule Checking (DRC) and layout versus schematic verification in 44% of the cases
DAPT improved the internal design and general circuit design knowledge, but there was a downgrade in the performance on open-domain academic benchmarks.
One of Artisan’s key findings is that domain-specific training is important for success in specialized areas like circuit design.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert’s approach and positions it relative to prior topology synthesis systems. AnalogXpert outputs real SPICE-code topologies and reduces design space using a subcircuit library; it decomposes synthesis into block selection and block connection (using CoT and in-context learning) and adds a proofreading step to iteratively correct designs. It uses a benchmark composed of 30 real cases and 2k synthetic cases and reports success rates of 40% (synthetic) and 23% (real), versus GPT-4o's 3% on both. The paper criticizes earlier work for taking vague requirements and producing ideal models rather than device-level SPICE, and notes prior formulations as graph generation or Python code generation. The table lists representative methods: CKTGNN (ideal model, synthetic, GNN), LaMAGIC (ideal model, synthetic, SFT-based LLM), AnalogCoder (no reduction, real, prompt-based LLM), and AnalogXpert (subcircuit library, real+synthetic, prompt-based LLM). The excerpt does not provide success rates for the other systems nor directly discuss hallucination risk, though it argues SPICE representation aligns with LLM pretraining and practical design workflows.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: AnalogXpert achieves 40% and 23% success rates on the synthetic dataset and real dataset respectively, which is markedly better than those of GPT-4o (3% on both the synthetic dataset and the real dataset).
we represent analog topology as SPICE code and introduce a subcircuit library to reduce the design space, in the same manner as experienced designers.
we decompose the problem into two sub-task (i.e., block selection and block connection) through the use of CoT and in-context learning techniques, to mimic the practical design process.

we introduce a proofreading strategy that allows LLMs to incrementally correct the errors in the initial design, akin to human designers who iteratively check and adjust the initial topology design to ensure accuracy.
Specifically, existing work uses vague design requirements as input and out- puts an ideal model, but detailed structural requirements and device-level models are more practical.
Another issue is that previous studies either treat the topology synthesis purely as graph generation (Chang et al.) or python code generation (Lai et al., a).
Finally, we construct a high-quality benchmark containing both real data (30) and synthetic data (2k).
CKTGNN Dong et al.                    Ideal Model                   synthetic          Graph Neural Network
LaMAGIC Chang et al.                  Ideal Model                   synthetic             SFT-based LLM
AnalogCoder Lai et al. (a)              None                           real              Prompt-based LLM
AnalogXpert                        Subcircuit Library              real+synthetic          Prompt-based LLM
The intuition behind is that the SPICE code is widely used in practical design scenarios and exists on the internet which is very likely to have been seen in the pre-training of LLMs.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes several automated topology-generation systems and their strengths/limitations. CktGNN frames topology design as a graph generation task and uses a graph VAE to generate topologies for operational amplifiers. LaMAGIC is a fine-tuned masked language model that generates circuits with a fixed number of graph nodes and "shows a high success rate" for small power converters (fewer than four devices). AnalogCoder leverages domain-specific prompt engineering on large LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) to output PySpice code (convertible to SPICE netlists) and can produce conventional analog circuits typically up to about ten devices. AnalogGenie is presented as a more scalable engine: it aims to broaden circuit variety, increase device counts per design, and discover unseen topologies; the authors also built an extensive dataset of over 3000 distinct topologies across many analog functionalities to enable this. The excerpt explicitly notes prior methods are "limited to a smaller scale of generation". The paper claims strong experimental performance for AnalogGenie but the excerpt does not provide quantitative success rates, detailed evaluation metrics, or discussion of hallucination/incorrect outputs, nor specifics of evaluation methodology beyond general experimental claims.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: formulates the topology design as a graph generation task
uses a graph variational autoencoder (VAE) to generate various circuit topologies for a specific type of analog ICs, i.e., operational-amplifiers (Op-Amps).
LaMAGIC (Chang et al., 2024), a fine-tuned masked language model (MLM), has been proposed to generate analog circuits with a fixed number of graph nodes.
It shows a high success rate in designing a specific type of analog ICs, i.e., power converters (with fewer than 4 devices).
AnalogCoder (Lai et al., 2024), another LLM-based work, uses domain-specific prompt engineering to generate analog circuits from well-established LLM models (e.g., GPT-4).

Instead of directly generating circuit topologies, it generates PySpice codes that can be converted to a SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) netlist–a textual high-level description of device connections used for circuit simulation.
AnalogCoder can generate a range of conventional analog circuits that often have a limited number of devices on the order of ten.
AnalogGenie, a Generative engine (model) for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies.
It can significantly broaden the variety of analog ICs, increase the number of devices within a single design, and discover unseen circuit topologies.

A major obstacle to advancing generative models for scalable analog circuit design automation is the lack of a comprehensive dataset of analog circuit topologies. We bridge this gap by building a extensive dataset that consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies with diverse functionalities
limited to a smaller scale of generation (e.g., generating a single type of analog ICs, small-size analog ICs, or conventional analog ICs)

Experimental results show the remarkable generation performance of AnalogGenie in broadening the variety of analog ICs, increasing the number of devices within a single design, and discovering unseen circuit topologies far beyond any prior arts.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes using GANs for circuit topology synthesis: a GAN maps a lower-dimensional latent space to circuit topologies, with a generator producing topologies and a discriminator improving generator quality. The authors demonstrate GANs on case studies (frequency response matching and low-pass filter realizability) and frame sizing optimization as the follow-up step to compare candidate topologies. The paper contrasts three broad solution classes: enumeration (can guarantee optimal topologies but scales poorly), metaheuristic methods (e.g., EAs—require many tuned parameters and can depend on good initialization), and generative/indirect encodings (reduce dimensionality and can support larger problems). Efficient enumeration via perfect-matching graph theory and brute-force search can extend enumerative reach. The excerpt highlights the motivation to leverage AI to solve larger synthesis problems and gain deeper design insights. The excerpt does not discuss specific systems named in the question (LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, AnalogXpert) nor does it mention evaluation metrics like success rates or hallucination behavior for those systems.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, limitations, success rates, evaluation methods, and potential for hallucination in automated circuit topology generation systems like LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, CktGNN, AnalogGenie, and AnalogXpert?

snippets: A GAN is a generative unsupervised learning model where a lower-dimensional latent space is predeﬁned as an abstract representation of the circuit topology space.
Circuit topologies can be produced using the generator, and a discriminator helps to improve generator quality.
Two circuit synthesis case studies are presented here: frequency response matching and low-pass ﬁlter realizability.
enumeration-based solution methods do have limitations on the size of systems that can be considered, these methods can guarantee identiﬁcation of optimal system topologies
metaheuristic methods ... they do have limitations. Often the algorithms require tuning of many parameters

Direct encodings (design representations or genotype) are used in the application of EAs to circuit synthesis problems. Direct encodings result in high-dimensional design spaces that are diﬃcult to navigate for large systems
indirect encodings with targeted reduced-dimension representations better mimic biological evolutionary processes, and have proven to be useful for supporting the design of larger-scale systems.
Herber and Allison developed an eﬃcient enumeration strategy for general synthesis problems based on perfect matching graph theory and a brute-force search algorithm.

This work ... is an eﬀort to leverage recent advancements in artiﬁcial intelligence to 1) create the ability to solve larger synthesis problems than previously possible, and 2) gain deeper design insights into engineering synthesis problems.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC, a language-model-based topology generator for analog integrated circuits. LaMAGIC adapts a pre-trained language model to analog circuit topology generation primarily via supervised finetuning (SFT). The authors emphasize careful design of input/output formulations to yield canonical circuit representations that align with the autoregressive nature of LMs. They evaluate multiple formulations and find an adjacency matrix–based circuit representation particularly effective for handling more complex circuit graphs. The system is presented as a single-pass generator (figure text: "One iteration!") capable of producing optimized circuit designs from custom specifications, and experimental results claim a high success rate ("achieves a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tol- erance of 0.01"). The paper contrasts LaMAGIC with prior RL-based search methods that require many simulator iterations, highlighting LaMAGIC’s efficiency and scalability. The work is by Chen-Chia Chang et al., posted on arXiv (2024). The excerpt does not contain methodological, architectural, dataset, or quantitative details for LaMAGIC2, CktGNN, AutoCkt, AnalogCoder, Masala-CHAI, MenTeR, or the Poddar2024 synthesizer.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogCoder: a training-free LLM agent for automatic analog circuit design that reframes design tasks as Python code generation to reduce LLM complexity. AnalogCoder leverages crafted prompts, a feedback-enhanced design flow, and a circuit tool library, and the authors publish an open-source analog design benchmark. Experimental notes: fine-tuning GPT-3.5 (two epochs) was tested and "Fine-tuned GPT-3.5 generally performs better on design tasks," but the base model still struggles to design additional circuits when data is limited. Attempt policy experiments used 50 trials per task with up to five attempts and found "success probability significantly drops after the third attempt," so the default is three attempts and composite circuits were limited to two attempts; "most designs are completed within three attempts." The paper also cites limitations and societal impacts: current LLMs cannot design highly complex analog circuits and are constrained by cost and compute. The excerpt contains no technical details (architectures, datasets, quantitative metrics) for LaMAGIC, LaMAGIC2, CktGNN, AutoCkt, Masala-CHAI, MenTeR, or the Poddar2024 synthesizer, so those items are not covered here.

snippets: CktGNN (Dong et al., 2023), formulates the topology design as a graph generation task, as circuit topologies of analog ICs can be naturally represented as graph structures.
It uses a graph variational autoencoder (VAE) to generate various circuit topologies for a specific type of analog ICs, i.e., operational-amplifiers (Op-Amps).
LaMAGIC (Chang et al., 2024), a fine-tuned masked language model (MLM), has been proposed to generate analog circuits with a fixed number of graph nodes.
It shows a high success rate in designing a specific type of analog ICs, i.e., power converters (with fewer than 4 devices).
AnalogCoder (Lai et al., 2024), another LLM-based work, uses domain-specific prompt engineering to generate analog circuits from well-established LLM models (e.g., GPT-4).

Instead of directly generating circuit topologies, it generates PySpice codes that can be converted to a SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) netlist–a textual high-level description of device connections used for circuit simulation.
AnalogCoder can generate a range of conventional analog circuits that often have a limited number of devices on the order of ten.
building a foundational comprehensive dataset of analog circuit topology

building a extensive dataset that consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies with diverse functionalities (e.g., Op-Amps, Low Dropout Regulator (LDO), Bandgap reference, Comparator, Phase-Locked Loop (PLL), Low Noise Amplifier (LNA), Power Amplifiers (PA), Mixer, Voltage-Controlled Os-cillator (VCO), etc)

In contrast to previous methods (Dong et al., 2023; Chang et al., 2024; Lai et al., 2024) that are limited to a smaller scale of generation (e.g., generating a single type of analog ICs, small-size analog ICs, or conventional analog ICs), AnalogGenie addresses the problem of scalable and general design.
These methods have demonstrated the potential of applying generative AI to analog IC design. Yet, a vast untapped frontier remains.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC as a language-model-based (LM-based) topology generation approach for analog circuit design that generates optimized circuit designs from custom specifications in a single pass. The authors focus on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and carefully design input/output formulations to produce canonical representations compatible with autoregressive LMs and to represent analog circuits as graphs. They propose an adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float inputs to the LM, which the authors report is more effective for understanding complex circuits and scalable to six-component circuits. Experimental results indicate their novel formulations outperform a naive formulation, achieving a success rate up to 0.96 under a tolerance of 0.01. The paper also compares different formulation variants, noting more severe overfitting in CF and CFDC than in FM and PM, with FM and PM showing better robustness and transferability (higher success rates) on complex 6-component circuits. The excerpt does not include architecture details, datasets, or specific results for LaMAGIC2, CktGNN, AutoCkt, AnalogCoder, Masala-CHAI, MenTeR, or the Poddar2024 synthesizer.

snippets: In this paper, we propose LaMAGIC, an LM-based topology generation model for analog circuit design that can directly generate an optimized circuit design given the custom specification in a single pass.
Our approach focuses on SFT

We systematically develop and analyze various input and output formulations to ensure canonical representations and the compatibility with the autoregressive nature of LMs, addressing the specific challenges of representing analog circuits as graphs.
Experimental results show that our novel circuit formulations can clearly outperform a naive formulation and achieve a success rate of up to 0.96 under a tolerance of 0.01.
adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float input to LM can have better effectiveness on complex circuit understanding
We observe more severe overfitting in CF and CFDC than FM and PM.

The robustness of FM and PM is further validated by their performance in transferability experiments detailed in Section 5.3, where these formulations achieve higher success rates in complex 6-component circuits.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Masala-CHAI: a fully automated framework that uses LLMs and custom-trained deep networks to extract SPICE netlists from analog schematic images. The pipeline components include a schematic extractor from documents, fine-tuned component detectors for passive and active parts, a deep Hough transform to identify nets, extensive prompt tuning for LLMs to correct common extraction errors, and post-extraction verification. Masala-CHAI produced an open dataset of roughly 7,500 captioned SPICE netlists and metadata drawn from 10 textbooks — reported as the largest curated open-source analog SPICE dataset to date. The authors fine-tuned multiple LLMs (CodeLlama, DeepSeek-34B, GPT-3.5/4) on this corpus; fine-tuning yields substantial gains in netlist generation tasks (e.g., >40% Pass@1 on the largest dataset, and up to a 46% Pass@1 improvement when used within LLM-agentic frameworks like AnalogCoder). The excerpt does not provide methodological or architectural details for LaMAGIC, LaMAGIC2, CktGNN, AutoCkt, MenTeR, or the Poddar2024 synthesizer, so those systems are not covered here.

snippets: Masala-CHAI is a fully automated framework leveraging large language models (LLMs) to generate Simulation Programs with Integrated Circuit Emphasis (SPICE) netlists.
We propose a three-step workflow to overcome existing limitations: labeling analog circuits, prompt tuning, and netlist verification.

various techniques, including  (i) Schematic extractor from documents, (ii) fine-tuned circuit component detectors to extract passive and active components,  (iii) deep hough transform to identify nets, (iv) extensive prompt tuning for LLMs       to  fix common  errors  in  netlist  extraction, and  (v)  post-extraction verification.
We curated the largest open-source analog circuit dataset, comprising 7,500 examples using Masala-CHAI
fine-tuned various LLMs, including CodeLlama, DeepSeek-34B, and GPT-(3.5,4).
Models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI when used in LLM-agentic frameworks such as AnalogCoder achieve a notable 46% improvement in Pass@1 scores.

largest dataset of 7500 captioned SPICE netlists extracted from 10 textbooks provides more than 40% Pass@1 accuracy even on challenging tasks like telescoping cascode amplifier and bandgap reference circuit generation.

contexts.context: LaMAGIC is an LM-based topology generation system that can directly produce optimized analog circuit designs from a given specification in a single pass. The authors focus on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and develop multiple input/output formulations designed to be canonical and compatible with autoregressive language models. They find that novel circuit formulations outperform a naive formulation, reporting a success rate up to 0.96 under a tolerance of 0.01. In particular, an adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float inputs to the language model yields better effectiveness for understanding and generating more complex circuits; experiments include scalability tests on six-component circuits. The paper also observes that formulation choices affect overfitting: CF and CFDC show more severe overfitting than FM and PM, with FM and PM validated as more robust via transferability experiments that achieve higher success rates in complex 6-component circuits. The authors plan to extend LaMAGIC to a wider range of analog components.

snippets: LaMAGIC, an LM-based topology generation model for analog circuit design that can directly generate an optimized circuit design given the custom specification in a single pass.
Our approach focuses on SFT and demonstrates the effectiveness of LMs in generating complex circuit topologies and deciding circuit parameters.
We systematically develop and analyze various input and output formulations to ensure canonical representations and the compatibility with the autoregressive nature of LMs,
Experimental results show that our novel circuit formulations can clearly outperform a naive formulation and achieve a success rate of up to 0.96 under a tolerance of 0.01.
we examine the scalability and adaptability of our LMs on more complex six-component circuits.
our proposed adjacency-matrix-based circuit formulation with float input to LM can have better effectiveness on complex circuit understanding,
We observe more severe overfitting in CF and CFDC than FM and PM.

The robustness of FM and PM is further validated by their performance in transferability experiments detailed in Section 5.3, where these formulations achieve higher success rates in complex 6-component circuits.

contexts.context: The LaMAGIC work compares three edge-generation formulations for topology generation: CF, PM, and FM. Models are fine-tuned from LMs pretrained on 3–5-component circuits using datasets of 500, 1000, and 2000 6-component samples and evaluated on a 9k 6-component test set. PM and FM use numerical inputs and a matrix formulation, which the authors attribute to their stronger performance and transferability. FM shows superior adaptability, particularly at larger tolerances and with more training data; PM also outperforms CF. Data augmentation via random vertex order permutation for edge generation substantially improves success rates, especially for FM. Table 3 reports MSEs for voltage conversion ratio and efficiency: PM-non-augment (0.010, 0.005), PM-augment (0.007, 0.002), FM-non-augment (0.040, 0.013), FM-augment (0.006, 0.013). Table 4 shows training vs. testing losses (CF 0.05/0.07, CFDC 0.05/0.07, FM 0.07/0.09, PM 0.03/0.04), indicating more overfitting in CF/CFDC than FM/PM. Pretraining on simpler circuits is critical: training only on 6-component circuits produced invalid designs. The LM can generate previously unseen high-performance power converter topologies meeting challenging specs (VCR ~0.4–0.6 or >1.2 and efficiency >0.9).

contexts.context: LaMAGIC adapts large language models to analog circuit topology generation via supervised fine-tuning, targeting power-converter designs. The authors emphasize framing circuit generation as graph generation: components are nodes and connections are edges, and they design input/output formulations to make graph-based topologies compatible with autoregressive LM training. They model generation as a sequence-to-sequence process that sequentially selects components or connections using preceding subgraph context, enabling one-shot generation of custom designs from specifications. The paper explicitly studies how to represent circuit specifications (floating-point numbers versus characters) and proposes canonical/LM-friendly output formats including float inputs to improve LM processing. Experimentally, LaMAGIC achieves a reported 0.96 success rate under a strict tolerance of 0.01. The excerpt does not explicitly describe an adjacency-matrix output format; instead it highlights sequential connection selection and various I/O formulations to capture graph structure for autoregressive LMs.

snippets: We propose and investigate various innova- tive input and output formulation for circuit generation to synchronize with the operational dynamics of LMs and ef- fectively capture graph-based circuit representations using the autoregressive loss function of LMs.
With the sequence-to-sequence modeling in LM, we can abstract the circuit generation into a process of sequential component or connection selection by utilizing the preceding subgraph information.
explore the effects of representing circuit specifications with different data types i.e., floating-point numbers versus characters.
This enables the efficient one-shot generation of custom circuit designs from user-defined specifications.
Experimental results show that our model achieves superior 0.96 success rate under a strict tolerance of 0.01.
Analog circuits can be effectively represented as graphs, where components are nodes and connections are edges, encapsulating both the structural and functional aspects of the design.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC, a language-model-based system for analog circuit topology generation that uses supervised finetuning (SFT) to adapt an LM to analog design. LaMAGIC is presented as able to produce an optimized circuit topology from custom specifications (e.g., conversion ratio and efficiency) in a single pass or "one iteration," achieving a high empirical success rate (reported up to 96%; figure notes "Success rate = 96%"), with an explicit claim of "LaMAGIC achieves a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01." The authors emphasize formulation choices for representing circuits to fit the autoregressive LM paradigm, framing circuits as graphs, and report that an adjacency matrix–based circuit formulation improves effectiveness and scalability for handling intricate/complex circuit designs. The approach is positioned as an alternative to many-iteration search methods (e.g., RL-based search), aiming to reduce simulation iterations by directly generating topologies aligned to given circuit specifications.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC’s adjacency-matrix-based formulation for representing analog circuit topologies to language models. The dataset includes 76k sampled 6-component circuits with 9k held out for evaluation. The adjacency-matrix formulation is split into a prefix and a circuit description: the prefix encodes high-level specifications (duty cycle options, voltage conversion ratio, efficiency), while the circuit description encodes the duty-cycle selection, a vertex order, and per-vertex connection entries. Connections are represented with discrete tokens (<no edge>, <edge 1>, <edge 2>, <both edges>), and ordering constraints (e.g., <edge 1> always precedes <edge 2>) enforce a canonical, unambiguous sequence. Earlier few-shot GPT-4 experiments (100 examples) failed to meet performance needs, motivating more structured representations and supervised finetuning. The excerpt explains the format and design choices (canonicalization, one-hot/selection encodings, explicit tokens) intended to improve LM training and loss alignment, but it does not report quantitative success rates or accuracy metrics for the adjacency-matrix formulation in this section.

Our experiment with GPT-4, involving few-shot prompt engineering with 100 samples in the analog circuit design domain, revealed that the model failed to produce circuits meeting our specific performance requirements.
The formulation consists of two main parts: the prefix and the circuit description.
Prefix: Duty cycle options 0.1 0.3 0.5 0.7 0.9 <sep> voltage conversion ratio 0.18887 <sep> efficiency 0.95544 <sep>
Duty cycle: <unselect> <unselect> <select> <unselect> <unselect> <sep> Vertex order: VIN VOUT GND Sa
Distinct tokens <no edge>, <edge 1>, <edge 2>, and <both edges> represent the presence or absence of connections between vertices.
Note that <edge 1> always precedes <edge 2> in each vertex’s connection representation.
Thus, this formulation ensures a canonical form for each circuit.

contexts.context: The paper uses an adjacency-matrix-based formulation for circuit topology generation, applying T5-styled masked language modeling where, for edge generation, "two masking tokens are used to conceal the duty cycle selection and the connections," and for topology generation "the scope of masking extends further to include vertex information" so the model predicts full circuit structure. A Float-input Adjacency-matrix-based Formulation feeds numerical specifications (duty cycle options, voltage conversion ratio, efficiency) as floats into the transformer by a "shared linear layer to encode these numbers" and integrates them with token embeddings. The pretrained model is "Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022) as the pretrained model" (encoder-decoder, 248M) and models are SFT-trained: "We perform training for 120 epochs" with "learning rate of 3 × 10−4", "batch size of 128", and "L2 regularization strength of 10−5". They use data augmentation via "random vertex order permutations." Evaluation metrics include "success rate of the generated circuits within varied tolerances and the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the input specifications and the simulated performance of the generated circuits." Table 1 reports MSEs for voltage conversion ratio and efficiency across formulations (NF, CF, CFDC, PM, FM), showing substantially lower MSEs for canonical/float-matrix variants (e.g., CFDC/FM) versus NF, and the best topology MSEs reported for FM ("FM      0.006       0.013       0.002        0.001").

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC's formulation and dataset for language-model-based analog power-converter topology generation. Circuits are represented as hypergraphs (vertices V for devices and terminals, hyperedges E for connections); components include C, L, phase-I switches Sa, phase-II switches Sb, while terminals are VIN, VOUT, and GND. The model's objectives are to generate connections (Edge Generation) or full topologies (Topology Generation) and select duty cycles to meet a target voltage conversion ratio r and efficiency η. Duty cycles are restricted to the set {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9}. Training data are generated by randomly sampling 3-, 4-, and 5-component topologies, creating five circuits per topology with different duty cycles, simulating each with NGSPICE to obtain conversion ratio and efficiency, and pruning invalid topologies. Dataset sizes reported are 1k, 14k, and 117k for 3/4/5-component circuits, with ~120k training and 12k evaluation samples; they also sample 76k six-component circuits for transfer tests. The LM approach uses autoregressive training (next-token prediction with negative log-probability loss). The authors note potential precision issues when encoding numerical circuit specifications in textual form and hint at a bespoke circuit formulation (Section 4.2) to address this.

snippets: An example of the power converter and its hypergraph representation is shown in Figure 2.
The objective of our model is to design circuit topolo- gies and select appropriate duty cycles to achieve specified voltage conversion ratio and power conversion efficiency.
we consider a range of duty cycle options: {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9}.

Problem 1 (Edge Generation). Given vertices V , a target voltage conversion ratio r, and an efficiency η, the task is to generate the connections E and determine the duty cycle s ∈{0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} to construct a circuit that satisfies both r and η.

Problem 2 (Topology Generation). In the scenario where the device requirements are not predefined, the challenge expands to generating both vertices V and their corresponding connections E, along with deciding the duty cycle s.
The circuit topology G is conceptualized as a hypergraph consisting of vertices V and hyperedges E.
The vertices V include various analog devices and three external terminal ports.
The device (or called component in later context), including capacitors C, inductors L, phase-I switches Sa, and phase-II switches Sb, is connected to other devices or ports via two outgoing edges.
The terminal ports are the voltage input port VIN , the voltage output port VOUT, and the ground GND, each with a single outgoing edge.

we generate five circuits using different duty cycles {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} according to our design space.
Then, we simulate each circuit with NGSPICE (Nenzi P, 2011) to compute the corresponding voltage conversion ratio and efficiency.
Next, we pruned out the invalid topologies reported by the simulator.

In total, we randomly split around 120k data points for training and 12k for evaluation.
In total, we sample 76k different 6-component circuits and allocate 9k data points for evaluation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC2, which replaces a prior adjacency-matrix / sequence-to-sequence LM formulation for analog topology generation with a succinct float-input canonical formulation with identifier (SFCI). SFCI reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) while adding component-type tokens and improving numeric precision sensitivity, addressing long input sequences and tight-spec tolerance issues. Table 1 shows large reductions in average LM output token length (LaMAGIC2-SFCI: 36.40 / 41.41 vs LaMAGIC-FM: 84.79 / 105.00 for 3–5 and 6-component circuits). Empirically, LaMAGIC2 attains much higher success under strict tolerances: it reports 34% higher success at tolerance 0.01 and 10× lower MSE than a prior method, and a combined claim of 122% and 34% higher success versus an RL search baseline and the original LaMAGIC respectively. The paper contrasts SFCI with an adjacency-matrix circuit formulation and notes that prior LM approaches used sequence-to-sequence supervised finetuning, which suffered low success rates on strict tolerances. Overall, LaMAGIC2 targets improved specification satisfaction and scalability for analog circuit topology generation.

contexts.context: The paper frames analog topology generation as: given vertices V and circuit specifications (voltage conversion ratio r and efficiency η), generate connections E and select a duty cycle s from discrete options so the simulated circuit meets r and η. LaMAGIC had poor transferability to larger circuits, motivating LaMAGIC2 and alternative sequence formulations. The Pure-text adjacency-matrix formulation (PM) encodes hypergraph connections as an adjacency matrix indexed by the input vertex order; this removes the need for per-node identifiers (e.g., <Sa0>, <Sa1>) and improves recognition of same-type components. However, tokenization of numeric values remains problematic because numeric floats are split into subword tokens, diluting numeric semantics. The Float-input adjacency-matrix formulation (FM) addresses numeric tokenization by encoding numeric inputs via a shared linear layer (float-input embedding), improving the model’s ability to learn relationships between input specifications and circuit structure and showing stronger transferability (noted particularly for six-component circuits). FM and tabular adjacency formulations also localize errors to specific matrix entries, aiding graph-difference detection during training. A key drawback of adjacency-matrix (float) formulations is O(|V|^2) sequence length, increasing attention complexity and harming transformer effectiveness on larger graphs.

PM represents circuit connections as an adjacency matrix for hypergraph, where rows and columns are indexed based on the vertex order given in the input.
For example, instead of using <Sa0> and <Sa1>, PM can simply use <Sa> to represent multiple nodes of the same type.

When numeric values (e.g., 0.95544) are fed into the tokenizer, they are split into multiple subword tokens (e.g., “0”, “.”, “9”, “5”, ...), diluting their semantic meaning and making it harder for the model to learn numeric relationships.
a missing or incorrect edge affects only the corresponding matrix entry, making it easier for the model to identify and correct isolated errors.
