Disadvantage: (1) Long token length O(|V |2): Longer sequences increase attention complexity, which leads to ineffectiveness in trans- former models.

Since the task formulation of AnalogXpert is very different from previous works, we construct a new benchmark including both real data (30) and synthetic data (2k).
AnalogXpert achieves 40% and 23% success rates in the synthetic dataset and real dataset respectively, which is much better than the GPT-4o (3% in the synthetic dataset, 3% in the real dataset).

contexts.context: The paper evaluates a training-free LLM-based agent (AnalogCoder) for analog circuit design using a feedback-driven design flow and a circuit tool library. Evaluation uses Pass@k as the main metric (Pass@k = 1 − (n−c choose k) / (n choose k)), with n = 30 trials for open-source models and GPT-3.5, and n = 15 for fine-tuned GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and GPT-4o. A benchmark of 24 analog design tasks was created (Tasks 1–15 basic, 16–24 composite), including Task 18: Op-amp integrator. Multiple LLMs were compared (CodeLlama, WizardCoder, Llama3, DeepSeek-V2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o); open-source models ran on 4 A100 GPUs. The paper notes that all LLMs except GPT-4o were enhanced via prompt engineering, design flow feedback, and the circuit tool library; GPT-4o was evaluated without the tool. The authors state that AnalogCoder (GPT-4o + circuit tool library) achieved uniformly high success rates in the reported Table 3 results (table entries show repeated "100.0     100.0" values), indicating near-perfect Pass@1 and Pass@5 on the presented tasks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, a training-free LLM-based analog design agent that frames analog topology synthesis as a real-world SPICE code generation problem rather than an ideal-model or Python-code formulation. It leverages a subcircuit library to produce concise circuit representations and combines chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting with in-context learning to guide generation. Because single-round generation struggles on complex tasks, the system uses human experience–based proofreading as a feedback loop across several rounds to let the LLM gradually correct mistakes and converge on correct topologies. The paper claims AnalogXpert achieves "decent performance" on conditional analog topology synthesis tasks, but the excerpt does not report numerical benchmark success rates or direct comparisons to other agents (e.g., AnalogCoder) or Python-code generation pipelines.

contexts.context: The excerpt states that AnalogCoder uses a training-free, agent-based framework to improve LLM capabilities for analog circuit design. It employs a feedback-enhanced flow (Table 8) with multiple checks: requirement checks (presence of I/O nodes and basic connectivity), simulation and operating-point checks (e.g., "Check that no errors occur during the simulation, e.g., floating nodes." and "Check each MOSFET is active: Vgs>Vth; Vds> Vgs-Vth."), DC sweep checks (verify output varies over input range and find Vin producing output near Vdd/2), and functional requirement checks. Tasks are grouped for fine-tuning evaluation (Table 9 lists Task IDs by group). Benchmark tables show other models often have low pass rates (Table 10 averages are mostly small). In direct comparison (Table 11), AnalogCoder attains higher overall performance than baseline GPT-4 in these tests: reported averages are "Avg 62.2 77.7 66.1 75.9" (GPT-4 vs AnalogCoder) and the "# Solved 19 19 20 20" line indicates AnalogCoder solved one more task set (20) than GPT-4 (19). Many individual task Pass@1/Pass@5 entries in Table 11 show 100% for several tasks for both systems.

contexts.context: AnalogCoder is presented as a training-free LLM agent that reframes analog circuit design as Python code generation. The system pairs crafted prompts, a feedback-enhanced design flow, and a circuit tool library to raise design success rates and ships with an open-source analog design benchmark. Evaluation used repeated trials (50 trials per task) with up to five attempts per trial (GPT-3.5 was used for experiments and a fine-tuned GPT-3.5 baseline was tested with two epochs). Results show most successful designs are produced within three attempts, leading the authors to set the default attempts to three; composite circuits are constrained to two attempts due to complexity and cost. They report that success probability drops significantly after the third attempt and that even small discrepancies can render generated circuits non-functional. Limitations noted include difficulty designing highly complex analog circuits and practical constraints of compute cost and resources.

snippets: This paper proposes AnalogCoder, a training-free LLM agent for automatic analog circuit design.
It innovatively transforms the analog circuit design tasks into the generation of Python code,
At the same time, it is equipped with crafted prompts, a feedback-enhanced design flow, and a circuit tool library, effectively enhancing the success rate of the designs.
Also, we provide an open-source analog design benchmark for future research.
Fine-tuning was conducted using the API of GPT-3.5 with two epochs.
Fine-tuned GPT-3.5 generally performs better on design tasks, as fine-tuning helps standardize design outputs through correct examples and reduces common syntax and design errors.
50 trials with a maximum of 5 attempts per trial using GPT-3.5.
most designs are completed within three attempts.
we have set the default number of attempts to three.
The number of design attempts for composite circuits has been limited to two because they involve more complex and challenging fixes and have high generation costs.
the findings indicate that success probability significantly drops after the third attempt.
even the slightest discrepancy can render a circuit non-functional.
Currently, LLMs lack the capability to design highly complex analog circuits, but future advancements may address this.

contexts.context: AnalogCoder is presented as the first training-free LLM agent that designs analog circuits by generating Python code. It leverages a feedback-enhanced flow and domain-specific prompts to enable automated, self-correcting circuit design and claims a high success rate. To simplify building composite designs, AnalogCoder includes a circuit tool library that archives successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits. Extensive experiments on a benchmark covering a wide range of analog tasks indicate AnalogCoder outperforms other LLM-based methods; specifically, it "has successfully designed 20 circuits, 5 more than standard GPT-4o." The authors provide code and the benchmark on GitHub. The paper motivates this approach by noting analog circuit design is substantially more challenging than digital design—due to complexity, lower abstraction, and scarce SPICE corpora—so generating Python (rather than SPICE/Verilog) leverages LLM strengths without further training. Overall, the excerpt highlights the training-free agent, the feedback loop and domain prompts, the modular sub-circuit library, benchmark results, and claimed improvement over GPT-4o.

contexts.context: AnalogCoder is presented as a training-free LLM-based agent that generates Python code (via PySpice/SPICE integration) to design analog circuits. The system emphasizes functional correctness rather than extensive parameter optimization and augments LLM performance with domain-specific prompt engineering, a feedback-enhanced design flow, and a dedicated circuit tool library. It is designed to operate fully automatically without human involvement and includes automatic error correction (LLM-based auto-fix). The authors introduce a new benchmark specifically for LLM analog-circuit design comprising 24 unique circuits, detailed task descriptions, sample designs, and test-benches. In experiments, AnalogCoder autonomously solved 20 of the 24 circuit challenges (outperforming standard GPT-4o at 15 solved and Llama-3 at 11 solved). The paper positions AnalogCoder as the first LLM agent targeted at analog integrated-circuit design and provides open benchmark resources to evaluate and compare LLM-based analog design methods.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports benchmark results for AnalogCoder-Pro within a comparative analog-design study. AnalogCoder-Pro provides limited feedback but does not include grounded numerical optimization under the PDK, which constrains its ability to reliably produce valid designs. As a result, AnalogCoder-Pro only intermittently finds correct designs after many trials: it achieves a 10% pass rate overall and a 2% Pass@1 (first-try success) on the evaluated tasks. For experimental fairness, the study set the maximum number of reasoning iterations in the evaluated system to three, matching the iteration limit used in AnalogCoder-Pro. The paper contrasts these low success metrics with other methods (e.g., a 0% pass rate for a purely zero-shot baseline and much higher rates for more integrated approaches), highlighting that lacking grounded numerical optimization and iterative, simulation-driven correction substantially reduces a training-free LLM-agent’s reliability in analog circuit design code-generation and feedback-loop workflows.

contexts.context: CktGNN is a two-level, directed-message-passing GNN that represents circuits as DAGs of non-overlapping ordered subgraphs. Inner GNNs learn contextualized representations for each subgraph in a known basis B; under a stated injectivity condition an injective graph transformation f can be constructed, ensuring consistent mapping from circuits to transformed DAGs. For benchmark construction, the authors convert analog Op-Amp circuits into acyclic graphs by abstracting complex modules into low-level functional sub-structures and using these sub-structures as graph edges while connection points become vertices. Feedforward and feedback are unified into a single direction with a polarity flag (e.g., gm+ / gm−). The Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) integrates a conversion-generation-simulation loop: generated graph topologies are turned into netlists and evaluated by circuit simulators to extract DC gain, bandwidth, phase margin, and a computed FoM. The dataset includes 10,000 Op-Amps; node types are capacitors, resistors, and single-stage Op-Amps with specified parameter ranges (resistors 105–107 Ohm, capacitors 10−14–10−12 F, gm 10−4–10−2 S). CktGNN is compared against multiple baselines and shows superior predictive and topology reconstruction performance.

Nodes in a circuit (graph) are sampled from C (capacitor), R (resistor), and single-stage Op-Amps with different polarities (positive or negative) and directions (feedforward or feedback).

Node features are then determined based on the node type: resistor R has a specification from 105 to 107 Ohm, capacitor C has a specification from 10−14 to 10−12 F, and the single-stage Op-Amp has a specification (transconductance, gm) from 10−4 to 10−2 S.

For each circuit, the circuit simulator of OCB performs careful simulations to get the graph properties (circuit specifications): DC gain (Gain), bandwidth (BW), and phase margin (PM), which characterize the circuit performance from different perspectives.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CktGNN, a two-level GNN for circuits that decomposes input circuits into non-overlapping subgraphs from a pre-defined ordered subgraph basis. Each input circuit G is transformed by a graphlizer f into a DAG G' whose nodes represent subgraphs; the transform aims to be injective under certain conditions. For each node v in G, if it belongs to multiple basis subgraphs the highest-ordered one is selected; connected nodes that select the same subgraph are merged and use the shared subgraph representation. Inner GNNs operate on each selected subgraph treated as an undirected graph, applying multiple undirected message-passing layers and graph pooling to produce a subgraph embedding hv'. The outer GNN runs directed message passing on the transformed DAG G', using a gated summation aggregation and a GRU-based update; the outer node inputs concatenate one-hot subgraph type encodings with the inner subgraph embeddings. The authors choose a VAE-based framework for circuit/DAG encoding (motivated by NAS methods) and note improved expressivity over 1-WL by encoding local subgraphs rather than rooted subtrees.

contexts.context: The paper's CktGNN uses a two-level GNN architecture: an inner GNN that embeds extracted subgraphs (subgraph basis B) and an outer directed message-passing GNN operating on a transformed graph produced by a graphlizer f. When the subgraph basis and order satisfy Theorem 3.2, f can be constructed to injectively map input DAGs to transformed graphs, and if both inner GNNs and outer directed message passing are injective then CktGNN injectively maps circuits to vector embeddings. Expressive power is therefore independent of domain-specific node ordering, though ordering can change the topology search-space complexity; empirically for the op-amp circuits studied reversing C and R order did not change f(G) in 10,000 cases. Corollary B.1 states that with a proper subgraph basis the two-level framework can be strictly more powerful than standard message-passing GNNs and as powerful as nested GNNs for certain graph classes. Experimental setup notes: baseline message-passing models (GCN/GIN) used 3 convolution layers with mean pooling; NGNN uses rooted subgraph height h=3 and GIN l=3; Transformer models used 5 encoder layers and 6 attention heads; DAGNN uses two-layer directed convolutions. Experiments ran on Tesla P100 and GTX 1080 Ti, with 5 independent trials; evaluation included Bayesian optimization for circuit search (Figure 8 shows exemplary optimal circuits).

contexts.context: The excerpt references CktGNN (Dong et al., 2023) as an example of prior graph-based analog topology generators. CktGNN and similar methods use graph representations with a fixed number of nodes (each node corresponding to a device or subgraph) and perform one-shot generation by predicting an adjacency matrix. The paper highlights limitations of this approach: it pre-defines node counts, is low in scalability, and ignores low-level (pin-level) details that are critical in analog design, leading to ambiguous and unscalable circuit generation. As a contrast, the excerpt describes AnalogGenie's approach: representing topologies as undirected graphs at the pin level and sequentializing them into Eulerian circuits so generation proceeds by predicting the next pin/edge, which is claimed to be more scalable and expressive. The excerpt does not provide CktGNN's detailed architecture, implementation specifics, Open Circuit Benchmark results, device sizing methodology, or quantitative evaluation metrics and results; those items are not present here and would require consulting the original CktGNN paper or benchmark reports.

contexts.context: The paper introduces CktGNN, a circuit graph neural network that jointly addresses topology generation and device sizing via encoder-dependent optimization subroutines. Architecturally, CktGNN uses a two-level (nested) GNN: a predefined subgraph basis models each circuit as a DAG whose nodes correspond to basis subgraphs. Inner GNNs independently learn representations for each subgraph (allowing multiple message-passing iterations for expressiveness and parallelism), while an outer directed GNN performs message passing over these learned node embeddings to encode the global circuit computation and performance. This representation enables continuous optimization of both topology and device parameters in a representation-based optimization framework. To support benchmarking and reproducible evaluation, the authors release the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open-sourced dataset of 10K distinct operational amplifiers with extracted circuit specifications and circuit generation/evaluation tools. Experiments on OCB report that CktGNN outperforms recent GNN baselines and human expert manual designs under their representation-based optimization approach.

snippets: CktGNN: circuit graph neural network for electronic design automation.
automates the circuit topology generation and device sizing based on the encoder-dependent optimization subroutines.
CktGNN encodes circuit graphs using a two-level GNN framework (of nested GNN) where circuits are represented as combinations of subgraphs in a known subgraph basis.
a subgraph basis is formulated in advance, and each circuit is modeled as a DAG G where each node represents a subgraph in the basis.

CktGNN uses two-level GNNs to encode a circuit: the inner GNNs independently learn the representation of each subgraph as node embedding, and the outer GNN further performs directed message passing with learned node embeddings to learn a representation for the entire graph.

The inner GNNs enable CktGNN to stack multiple message passing iterations to increase the expressiveness and parallelizability, while the outer directed message passing operation empowers CktGNN to encode computation of circuits (i.e. circuit performance).
Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open-sourced dataset that contains 10K distinct operational amplifiers with carefully-extracted circuit specifications.

OCB is also equipped with communicative circuit generation and evaluation capabilities such that it can help to generalize CktGNN to design various analog circuits by producing corresponding datasets.
Experiments on OCB show the extraordinary advantages of CktGNN through representation-based optimization frameworks over other recent powerful GNN baselines and human experts’ manual designs.

contexts.context: The paper represents analog circuit topologies at the device-pin level as finite connected undirected graphs G = (V, E), then converts each undirected edge into two directed arcs and encodes the resulting directed graph as an Eulerian circuit (a trail visiting every edge exactly once). This sequential Eulerian-circuit representation stores only existing edges, making it more space-efficient than adjacency matrices for sparse analog graphs; the authors prove a directed conversion always admits an Eulerian circuit. To reduce inductive bias and overfitting, they generate multiple unique Eulerian circuits per topology (yielding ~70× more data) and enforce graph-level permutation invariance by applying a BFS node ordering per device type and fixing “VSS” as the start node. They customize a tokenizer mapping tokens to device pins, add a “Truncate” token and padding for variable device counts, and pre-train the model to predict the next device pin in complete topology sequences. The dataset used contains 3350 distinct topologies across 11 circuit types. These design choices target scalable, unique sequential encodings for topology generation and downstream fine-tuning.

AnalogGenie adopt the approach used by GraphRNN (You et al., 2018), employing a breadth-first-search (BFS) algo- rithm to assign node orders within each device type, ensuring that each circuit topology has a unique graph representation.
AnalogGenie manually defines “VSS” (the ground node universal to all analog circuits) as the starting node for all sequences.
we customize a tokenizer to encode and decode our sequence that represents the circuit topology to train AnalogGenie.
To allow AnalogGenie to generate topologies with different numbers of devices, we introduce a special “Truncate” token and use padding to ensure that all sequences have the same length.
Our circuit dataset contains 3350 distinct topologies, spanning 11 circuit types

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CktGNN, a two-level graph neural network for circuit representation and automated analog circuit design. CktGNN models circuits using a pre-designed subgraph basis: inner GNNs independently encode each subgraph (allowing stacked message-passing for expressiveness and parallelism) and produce node embeddings; an outer GNN then performs directed message passing over these embeddings to model circuit computation and overall graph representation. The design reduces topology search space and targets circuit topology generation as well as device parameter optimization. To support reproducible evaluation, the authors introduce the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open graph dataset of 10K operational amplifiers with topologies modeled as graphs and performance metrics extracted from simulators; they plan to open-source generation and evaluation code and upload OCB to OGB. The paper positions CktGNN against prior methods that focus only on device sizing given fixed topologies and notes the scarcity and limitations of topology-synthesis approaches (e.g., genetic algorithms). Experimental claims state CktGNN outperforms GNN baselines and produces competitive op-amp designs versus human experts.

snippets: CktGNN uses two-level GNNs to encode a circuit: the inner GNNs independently learn the representation of each subgraph as node embedding, and the outer GNN further performs directed message passing with learned node embeddings to learn a representation for the entire graph.

The inner GNNs enable CktGNN to stack multiple message passing iterations to increase the expressiveness and parallelizability, while the outer directed message passing operation empowers CktGNN to encode computation of circuits (i.e. circuit performance).
Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), the first open graph dataset for optimizing both analog circuit topologies and device parameters
OCB contains 10K distinct operational amplifiers (circuits) whose topologies are modeled as graphs and performance metrics are carefully extracted from circuit simulators.
we will open-source codes of the communicative circuit generation and evaluation processes to facilitate further research by producing datasets with arbitrary sizes and various analog circuits.

The key contributions in this paper are: 1) we propose a novel two-level GNN, CktGNN, to encode circuits with deep contextualized information, and show that our GNN framework with a pre-designed subgraph basis can effectively increase the expressiveness and reduce the design space of a very challenging problem–circuit topology generation; 2) we introduce the first circuit benchmark dataset OCB with open-source codes, which can serve as an indispensable tool to advance research in EDA; 3) experimental results on OCB show that CktGNN not only outperforms competitive GNN baselines but also produces high-competitive operational amplifiers compared to human experts’ designs.
Despite their great promise, all these prior arts have been limited to optimizing the device parameters with a given analog circuit topology.
There are only a few efforts (e.g., Genetic Algorithms (Das & Vemuri, 2007)) to tackle another very challenging yet more important problem, i.e, circuit topology synthesis.
Illustration of the two-level GNN framework with a pre-designed subgraph basis.
It first represents input graph g as a combination of subgraphs in the subgraph basis B and then learns representations of subgraphs with inner GNNs.

contexts.context: CktGNN is a DAG-aware circuit graph neural network used both as a predictive model and as a latent-space encoder in a VAE generator for electronic circuit design. It can be trained in supervised mode or inside a VAE: two FC layers predict the posterior mean/variance (ELBO) and the decoder reuses a D-VAE-style generation to produce transformed DAGs from latent vectors. The decoder constructs circuits subgraph-by-subgraph from a known subgraph basis B, using MLPs to sample subgraph types, predict node features, and predict inter-subgraph connections; the outer GNN uses GRU-based directed message passing and maps latent z to initial hidden state h0 via an MLP. Training follows common VAE settings (α KL = 0.005, batch size 64, 200 epochs, initial lr 1e-4 with scheduler). For evaluation/generation the authors sample 1000 latent points and decode each 10 times; validity criteria require a single input and output, DAG topology, and no R/C nodes on the main path. The OCB benchmark was updated (Ckt-Bench101: 10000 successfully simulated circuits) and a larger Ckt-Bench301 was provided for Bayesian optimization tests. Results show CktGNN achieves strong predictive performance (e.g., FoM Pearson r ≈ 0.806), high valid-circuit rate (~91.9%) and novelty (~96.3%), and better Bayesian-optimization FoM and lower regret than DAGNN and D-VAE in reported experiments.

snippets: CktGNN can be trained in a supervised fashion, or in the VAE framework.
In the VAE architecture, we take CktGNN as the DAG encoder.

Two fully connected (FC) layers take the output representation of CktGNN as inputs to predict the mean and variance of the approximated posterior distribution in the evidence lower bound (ELBO) (Kingma & Welling, 2013).
The decoder is formulated as the decoder of D-VAE to generate transformed DAGs G ′ from the latent space.
the decoder is also based on the CktGNN framework where the GRU-based directed message passing (introduced in D-VAE) is used as the outer GNN.
Given a point z in the latent vectorial space, an MLP is used to map z to h0 as the initial hidden state.
we updated the OCB benchmark datasets https://github.com/zehao-dong/ CktGNN as following: ... stored 10000 successfully simulated circuits in the dataset Ckt-Bench101.
To calculate these values, we randomly sample 1000 random points in the latent vectorial space from a (prior) standard normal distribution, and decode each point for 10 times.
A generated circuit is valid if it satisfies following three criteria:
(1) It has only one input node and one output node;
(2) The generated circuit (directed graph) is a DAG (i.e. there is no cycle);
(3) In the main path, there is no R node nor C node.
Optimization on the latent space by CktGNN yields a circuit with the largest FoM score than competitive DAG encoders (or GNN for undirected graphs).

contexts.context: The paper introduces CktGNN, a two-level GNN encoder using a pre-designed subgraph basis to represent circuit DAGs, and integrates it into a VAE framework for analog circuit design automation. CktGNN is evaluated on the authors' Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB), an open-source dataset and toolset for circuit topology generation and device parameter optimization; the authors provide generation code, simulator files, experiment code, and appendices detailing proofs, comparisons, and instructions. Empirically, CktGNN-based decoders produce a significantly higher proportion of valid circuits than other decoders and the CktGNN-based VAE is reported to find circuits with the highest figure-of-merit (FoM), reducing simulation needs and improving time- and cost-efficiency. The work compares CktGNN to general two-level GNNs and discusses expressivity in appendices, positioning the method and OCB as generalizable, scalable, and compatible with other analog circuit types.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a DRL-based approach for automated analog circuit structure synthesis. The method "behaves like a designer," learning from trials to construct circuits that meet given design specifications. To enable RL, "necessary design rules are defined and applied to set up the specialized environment of reinforcement learning." The synthesis starts from a "starting sub-circuit" and "gradually expands it with building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process." The framework integrates simulation-in-loop sizing and employs "hash table and symbolic analysis techniques" to greatly reduce the number of produced structures that must be sized, improving evaluation efficiency. The authors claim this is the first DRL work for automated analog structure synthesis and highlight features: the process is a decision-making one, the design search space is "flexible and controllable," design knowledge is automatically learned, and it requires "considerably less computation effort" than traditional generation methods. The excerpt reports experimental results demonstrating efficiency, reliability, and wide applicability. The text does not provide detailed multi-objective formulation, numeric results, or mention AutoCkt specifically.

snippets: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Analog Circuit Structure Synthesis
This paper presents a novel deep-reinforcement-learning (DRL)-based method, which integrates the merits of the structure generation and structure reasoning methods.

behaves like a designer, who learns from trials, derives design knowledge and experience, and evolves gradually to eventually figure out a way to construct circuit structures that can meet the given design specifications.
Necessary design rules are defined and applied to set up the specialized environment of reinforcement learning
starts with a starting sub-circuit and gradually expands it with building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process
simulation-in-loop sizing
hash table and symbolic analysis techniques are employed to significantly reduce the number of the produced structures to be sized during the synthesis process.
1)  The synthesis process is a decision-making process;
2)  Design search space is flexible and controllable;
3)  Design knowledge is automatically learned;
4)  Considerably less computation effort compared with the conventional circuit structure generation methods.

contexts.context: This excerpt describes MA-Opt, an RL-inspired framework for analog circuit sizing that uses multiple actors running in parallel to predict diverse circuit designs and perform multiprocessing SPICE simulations. To make multi-actor learning effective, MA-Opt shares an "elite solution set" that influences the network loss and accelerates training, and it introduces a tuning technique to refine previously best designs. It also employs a near-sampling method that densely samples designs adjacent to the current best and uses a critic network that mimics the SPICE simulator as a surrogate to predict sampled results—reducing reliance on expensive simulator calls. The authors position MA-Opt relative to DDPG and DNN-Opt: DDPG-like methods often require thousands of SPICE simulations, while DNN-Opt uses a single agent. Experimentally, MA-Opt achieves up to 24% better metrics than DNN-Opt within the same number of simulations and better Figure of Merit (FoM) at the same runtime. The framework targets satisfying performance constraints in continuous-state analog sizing problems by improving exploration and surrogate-assisted local search.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports empirical results for AutoCkt, a deep reinforcement learning system for analog circuit sizing. It emphasizes that a vanilla genetic algorithm is "too sample inefﬁcient," while AutoCkt achieves a sample efficiency 9.56× greater than the prior state-of-the-art (a combined ML and genetic algorithm baseline). The method was evaluated on 40 randomly generated target design specifications and was able to obtain 40 LVS-passed designs, indicating robustness across the test set. Runtime is practical: on a single-core CPU the algorithm completes in 1.7 hours. The text also notes that relationships hold when considering layout parasitics despite differences between schematic and PEX simulations, suggesting the evaluation considered realistic parasitic effects. The authors provide an open-source implementation at the linked GitHub repository. Overall, the excerpt highlights improved sample efficiency, full success on a 40-spec benchmark, reasonable single-core runtimes, and awareness of layout parasitics in evaluations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes key RL components and how they apply to analog circuit sizing. It explains the replay buffer storing transitions (si, ai, ri, s′i) and prioritised experience replay (PER), where transitions are sampled with probability proportional to their TD-error to focus learning on high-error samples, and importance-sampling weights (wi with hyperparameter β) correct the introduced bias. The text outlines Q-learning and Double Q-learning update rules and the soft target-network update Q′weights = τ.Qweights + (1−τ).Q′weights, and notes that DDPG uses actor (µ/µ′) and critic (Q/Q′) networks with target counterparts. In the circuits context, circuits are treated as complex, nonlinear systems with emergent, sensitive behavior, making optimization difficult. Problem formulation is given: for a D-dimensional design vector x ∈ Ω (resistors, capacitors, transistor sizings), circuit performance f(x) — possibly a combined figure of merit — is evaluated via costly circuit simulation, and the goal is to find x that maximizes f(x). This highlights that RL/actor-critic methods with PER and target networks are suitable for expensive, black-box, possibly multi-objective circuit optimization but the excerpt does not present AutoCkt-specific architecture or empirical multi-objective results.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes EEsizer, an LLM-based closed-loop agent for transistor sizing that collects annotated data during optimization and uses chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting to guide stepwise reasoning. EEsizer requires no domain-specific fine-tuning or external algorithms (e.g., BO or gm/Id) and is designed to adapt across LLMs, circuit types, specifications, and technology nodes without modifying prompts or functions. The authors applied it to six basic circuits (two amplifiers, a ring oscillator, three logic gates) and a 20-transistor opamp. At 90 nm, OpenAI o3 reached intended design targets across three test groups within 20 iterations. Robustness testing (50 variations across nine metrics) showed rail-to-rail metrics had small deviations while gain-related metrics had larger spreads; a minimum pass rate of 76% for gain was reported. Practical limits included constraining experiments to 25 iterations and five trials per configuration due to API rate and cost, and observed pathologies such as LLM oscillation between candidates. Large, reasoning-focused LLMs gave higher-quality strategies but are costly/latent. The paper cites prior works including AutoCkt (deep RL) as related literature and suggests future work like LLM-based automatic analog testbench generation.

contexts.context: AutoCkt is a deep RL framework for analog circuit sizing that handles multi-objective design specifications and both schematic and post-layout (PEX) environments. The authors use BAG to generate layouts and include PVT variations by taking the worst-performing metric as the specification. They demonstrate transfer learning by training an agent in Spectre (schematic) and running it on a TSMC 16nm FF generator in BAG for PEX, with no additional training when the environment changes. AutoCkt achieves large sample-efficiency gains versus genetic algorithms (reported ~40× on average and a 40.6× faster convergence in one case), with schematic-only agents converging in about 10 simulation steps and transferred PEX runs taking around 23 steps. Trajectories show rapid convergence (example: 11 time steps to meet a target), and multi-objective results indicate successful attainment of targets (figure notes: no unreached objectives). Including layout parasitics increases convergence time but the agent learns tradeoffs between specs; using ranges for constraints (e.g., phase margin) improved transfer by encouraging exploration. Overall, AutoCkt demonstrates robust, sample-efficient multi-objective analog sizing across schematic and layout-extracted environments.

contexts.context: AutoCkt is a machine-learning optimization framework that applies deep reinforcement learning to analog circuit sizing. The method is trained over a sparse sub-sample of the design space to learn tradeoffs and reduce convergence time during deployment, and it is explicitly formulated for N parameters and M target design specifications (x ∈ Z^N, y ∈ R^M). Training uses trajectory-based RL with policy-gradient updates to maximize expected accumulated reward. Compared to prior methods, AutoCkt generalizes across design goals via sparse sampling and transfer learning, avoiding per-spec retraining. In schematic simulations it converged to meet target specifications on at least 96.3% of tested goals and ran on average 40× faster than a traditional genetic algorithm. When including layout parasitics using the Berkeley Analog Generator, AutoCkt produced 40 LVS-passed operational amplifiers in 68 hours, about 9.6× faster than the state-of-the-art. The authors emphasize that the framework "intuitively understands the design space" and can identify unreachable targets when it fails to converge.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AutoCkt, a reinforcement-learning-based framework for analog circuit sizing. The system has two main components: an RL agent and a simulation environment. The agent’s neural network observes simulated performance o and a target specification o* and decides per-parameter increments/decrements/retains, with H simulation steps allowed to reach o*. Training samples 50 target specifications and generates trajectories; training ends when the mean reward reaches zero (meaning targets are consistently satisfied). The reward is a dense cumulative function that increases as observed performance approaches targets. Implementation details: a three-layer neural network with 50 neurons per layer trained using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) via OpenAI Gym and the Ray framework. AutoCkt interfaces with simulators (predictive technology models, Spectre, and BAG/Cadence with parasitics). In experiments on a transimpedance amplifier, AutoCkt achieved 25.1× speedup in sample efficiency versus a vanilla genetic algorithm and generalized to 97.4% of the design space; deployment tested 500 randomized targets. The excerpt also lists example action and specification ranges for evaluated circuits (e.g., TIA settling time, cutoff, input-referred noise).

contexts.context: The excerpt frames analog circuit sizing as a context-dependent, often multi-objective constrained optimization problem where a Figure of Merit (FoM) is formed via a weighted preference vector and clipping to avoid single-constraint dominance. Practical automation must handle topology choices (topology-aware design) and device sizing, motivating bi-level formulations where an upper-level picks topology x and a lower-level optimizes device variables y (y*(x) = arg min G(x,y)). Layout parasitics and PVT (process, voltage, temperature) variations are critical: parasitics necessitate layout-level or post-layout-aware evaluation, and PVT corner testing identifies worst-case performance. Approaches discussed include constrained satisfaction/cost formulations and multi-fidelity strategies — e.g., multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization to integrate cheaper schematic and expensive post-layout simulations — as well as AI techniques that form trust regions or freeze low-impact variables to speed convergence. The text emphasizes the need for an intelligent agent that learns from experience, predicts objective behavior, generalizes to unseen tasks, and performs guided exploration in a high-dimensional, multi-objective design space.

contexts.context: AutoCkt formulates analog sizing as a multi-objective RL environment with per-component action spaces and multi-parameter design specifications (examples: gain, unity-gain bandwidth, phase margin, bias current). For the transimpedance amplifier example the speciﬁcations considered were gain ([200,400] V/V), UGBW ([1.0e6,2.5e7] Hz), phase margin and bias current ([0.1,10] mA); the combined action space size can be enormous (e.g., 10^14 or 10^11 combinations), so random search is infeasible. Agents are allowed limited trajectory lengths (e.g., 30 simulation steps for the TIA). AutoCkt achieves strong multi-objective results and generalization: for a two-stage op amp it reaches 963/1000 unseen targets (generalizing ~20× beyond training specs) with an average of 27 simulations to converge (∼40× faster than a genetic algorithm). For a harder negative-gm two-stage op amp it converged to all 500 test targets with only 10 simulations on average. Training uses fast schematic sims (∼25 ms each), enabling tractable training (wall-clock example 1.3 hours on 8 cores), and transfer learning lets agents trained on schematic simulations handle slower post-layout/PEX environments despite much larger sim times (2.4s vs 91s).

contexts.context: Masala-CHAI is an automated pipeline that extracts schematics and related text from PDF documents and generates SPICE netlists. The system performs component detection, net clustering, and creates annotated schematics (Fig. 6) before producing netlists (Fig. 5, Fig. 8). The authors collected ~7,500 schematic images from 10 textbooks and applied the Masala-CHAI framework to produce structurally correct netlists, marking each component with default parameters. Prompt tuning is used to steer LLM behavior; example prompts instruct an "expert analog designer" to list components, map MOSFET terminals using red net numbers, and write a SPICE netlist. Because automatic generation can produce errors (e.g., floating nets), Masala-CHAI includes a final verification step that calls an LLM on the extracted netlist to identify and correct common issues (removing floating nets). The paper highlights prompt design and shows effectiveness with examples (Figures 3, 7), emphasizing automated dataset creation and LLM-based verification for large-scale netlist generation.

contexts.context: The Masala-CHAI paper presents a fully automated framework that uses large language models to generate SPICE netlists from analog circuit schematic images. The authors propose a three-step workflow—labeling analog circuits, prompt tuning, and netlist verification—to enable end-to-end netlist generation. They built and open-sourced the largest curated analog SPICE dataset to date: ~7,500 captioned netlists and metadata collected from 10 textbooks. The framework combines multiple components: a schematic extractor from documents, fine-tuned component detectors for passive and active parts, deep Hough-transform priors to identify nets, extensive prompt tuning for LLMs to correct common extraction errors, and post-extraction verification. They fine-tuned several LLMs (CodeLlama, DeepSeek-34B, GPT-3.5/4) and benchmarked open-source and proprietary models. Results show that larger fine-tuning datasets improve pass@k performance, and models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI used within agentic frameworks like AnalogCoder achieved a reported ~46% improvement in Pass@1, demonstrating substantial gains in automated analog netlist generation and verification.

snippets: Masala-CHAI is a fully automated framework leveraging large language models (LLMs) to generate Simulation Programs with Integrated Circuit Emphasis (SPICE) netlists.
This approach enables end-to-end SPICE netlist generation from circuit schematic images
We propose a three-step workflow to overcome existing limitations: labeling analog circuits, prompt tuning, and netlist verification.
We curated the largest open-source analog circuit dataset, comprising 7,500 examples using Masala-CHAI

We incorporate various techniques, including  (i) Schematic extractor from documents, (ii) fine-tuned circuit component detectors to extract passive and active components,  (iii) deep hough transform       priors to identify nets, (iv) extensive prompt tuning for LLMs       to  fix common  errors  in  netlist  extraction, and (v)  post-       extraction verification.
We fine-tuned various LLMs, including CodeLlama, DeepSeek-34B, and GPT-(3.5,4).
Models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI when used in LLM-agentic frameworks such as AnalogCoder achieve a notable 46% improvement in Pass@1 scores.
As shown in Figure 1, larger datasets significantly enhance performance, improving pass@k results across different numbers of textbooks used for fine-tuning.

contexts.context: The excerpt lists numerous recent works applying large language models to EDA tasks relevant to converting schematics to SPICE/netlists and evaluating them. It cites LLM-assisted formal verification testbench generation (From rtl to sva), netlist-focused analysis and security (Netlist whisperer), and autonomous LLM agents for EDA workflows (Chateda, Drc-coder). Several entries address code generation for hardware description languages and SPICE-like outputs, and methods to improve generation quality (Vrank: self-consistency). There are domain-specific datasets and benchmarks (Circuitnet; Metrex) and projects for specification generation/review (SpecLLM). The bibliography also references evaluation and tuning approaches such as fine-tuning from human preferences. Together these works provide precedents for: (1) training/evaluating LLMs on schematic-to-netlist tasks using Circuitnet-like datasets and Verilog/SPICE benchmarks; (2) verification via generated formal testbenches and security/side-channel analyses; (3) quality-improvement techniques like self-consistency, retrieval-augmentation, RLHF-style fine-tuning, and autonomous agent orchestration for multi-step EDA pipelines. These citations are useful context when designing Masala-CHAI dataset splits, GPT-4 evaluation metrics, and verification workflows for SPICE netlist generation from schematic images.

contexts.context: Masala-CHAI is an open-sourced, automated dataset of approximately 7,500 SPICE netlists with metadata (captions, components, nodes) gathered from 10 textbooks. The framework turns schematic images and associated textual context (captions, page text) into labeled training data and follows a three-step pipeline: (i) labeling analog circuits, (ii) prompt tuning, and (iii) SPICE netlist verification. The authors finetune and benchmark multiple LLMs (CodeLLama-70B, DeepSeek-34B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4o) on this dataset and report that incorporating GPT-4o fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI into the AnalogCoder agent yields a 46% absolute Pass@1 improvement. A key challenge is accurate component recognition: GPT-4o often misidentifies or omits elements (e.g., confusing NMOS vs. PMOS). To address this, the paper proposes two solutions: (1) a dedicated CNN-based object detection model (e.g., YOLO) tailored to schematic components to improve detection and bounding, and (2) targeted prompt tuning that emphasizes structural differences (e.g., NMOS vs PMOS arrow directions) to improve LLM differentiation. The pipeline includes SPICE netlist verification to ensure correctness after extraction.

snippets: Leverage Masala-CHAI to compile an open-sourced dataset        of ∼7,500 SPICE netlists with metadata (figure captions, components, and nodes) from 10 textbooks.
Finetune  and  benchmark   four   state-of-art  LLMs (CodeLLama-70B,  DeepSeek-34B,  GPT-3.5,  GPT-4o) on the Masala-CHAI dataset
Enhance the AnalogCoder [14] agentic framework by incorporating GPT-4o fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI, achieving a 46% absolute improvement in Pass@1 scores.
When generating a SPICE netlist, accurately identifying all circuit components is essential for designers and challenging for language models.
GPT-4o frequently misidentifies components, often confusing NMOS and PMOS transistors

Solution 1: Customized Object Detection Network for Analog Circuit Components- Recognizing the limitations of GPT-4o in this task, we propose a dedicated solution using a deep CNN-based object detection model, using the strong performance of models such as YOLO [24].
Solution 2: Prompt Tuning - We developed a targeted prompt enhancement strategy to refine LLM component differentia- tion, especially between NMOS and PMOS transistors.
Masala-CHAI follows a three-step process: (i) labeling analog circuits, (ii) prompt tuning, and (iii) SPICE netlist verification
Along with extracted schematics, we also extract and summarize text describing the schematic, for example, from the schematic caption or text on the page referring to the schematic.
For NMOS, the arrow on the source terminal points outwards from the transistor. For PMOS, the arrow on the source terminal points inward towards the transistor.

contexts.context: The Masala-CHAI pipeline converts schematic images into SPICE netlists by combining computer-vision preprocessing with LLM-based netlist generation. The dataset is built by paginating texts into PDFs and extracting schematics using a fine-tuned YOLOv8 (Darknet53 backbone with CSP bottleneck), trained on ~4,300 annotated diagrams (resized to 640x640, 1000 epochs, lr=0.01) to detect 12 component classes. Net detection uses pre-trained deep Hough transform line priors; components are removed before line detection, then line segments are clustered into nets (heuristic: cluster segments whose endpoints fall within a 40-pixel radius, tuned and manually verified). The authors observed GPT-4o failure modes: assuming intersecting nets are connected, misidentifying MOSFET terminals (drain/gate/source), and mishandling differential I/O pairs. To mitigate these, they developed automatic net annotation using deep Hough priors and incorporated annotations into prompts, which reduced ambiguities and improved GPT-4o netlist accuracy. The process includes manual tuning/verification of clustering and uses annotations to assist the LLM in mapping components to correct net connections, improving SPICE generation reliability.

This dataset creation framework, shown in Figure 5, starts by paginating a textbook into individual PDFs (➊), which are then passed through a YOLOv8 [24], a state-of-the-art object localization and classification model, to extract schematics from a page.

Incorporating these annotations into the prompt (see Section IV), led to improvement in GPT-4o’s performance in translating schematics to SPICE netlists.
This systematic annotation reduced ambiguities and enhanced the LLM’s ability to interpret and connect components, particularly in complex schematics correctly.

contexts.context: The paper introduces AMSNet, a dataset and generation tool that automatically converts schematic diagrams into netlists to support MLLM-driven AMS circuit design. The authors outline a pipeline of schematic collection, component detection, net recognition, and netlist generation, and assembled AMSNet-1.0 from literature sources. They note a key bottleneck: lack of datasets mapping schematics to netlists for AMS circuits, which are often specified at the transistor level (unlike modular digital circuits). The authors evaluated GPT-4 on tasks derived from AMSNet and report that GPT-4 achieved about 90% accuracy on component detection but only about 45% accuracy on transistor-level net detection, indicating current LLM limitations for extracting precise transistor-level SPICE-style netlists. AMSNet and its generation tool are publicly available to support further research and improve automatic AMS netlist extraction and synthesis.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a GPT-based generative engine for analog circuit topology generation and a large curated dataset to support it. The dataset "consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies" spanning many analog functions and is expanded via augmentation "by over 70×." Rather than high-level text netlists or fixed-node graph encodings, the authors "represent each circuit topology as an undirected graph where each node is a device pin" and "sequentialize it into an Eulerian circuit–a trail that visits every edge exactly once," enabling scalable sequential graph generation. AnalogGenie "generate[s] diverse analog circuits by predicting the next device pin to connect in the circuit," addressing limitations of prior methods that are "often limited to generating only a single type of circuit topology" or suffer from one-shot adjacency prediction. The paper notes that "A SPICE netlist is a text-based high-level description of the connection between devices" and that generating higher-level code (e.g., PySpice) can be error-prone ("PySpice code is a high-level representation, making its generation process more prone to error"). This material is relevant for systems that convert schematic images to SPICE netlists and for dataset design, representation choice (pin-level sequentialization), model architecture (LLM/GPT), and evaluation/verification concerns.

Fine-tuning LLMs on Masala-CHAI demonstrates its use in automated SPICE netlist generation from natural language prompts.
Our dataset and codebase (https: //masala-chai-llm.github.io/) are fully open-sourced and easily extensible on additional textbooks and documentation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Masala-CHAI's automated pipeline for generating SPICE netlists from circuit schematics and how the authors evaluate and verify its outputs. Netlists are parsed into graph representations where nodes are component types and edges are interconnections. They measure structural similarity between two SPICE netlists using Graph Edit Distance (GED), normalize GED by the maximum possible edit distance (summing nodes and edges across graphs) to produce a 0–100 metric, and define similarity accordingly. Using this metric, Masala-CHAI-generated netlists matched AMSNet outputs with a 100% similarity score, validating the flow and motivating broader evaluation. A key distinction is that AMSNet requires manual effort and lacks human-readable netlist descriptions, which limits its usefulness for supervised fine-tuning of LLMs. Masala-CHAI provides an end-to-end automated flow that also generates descriptions alongside SPICE netlists, improving suitability for LLM SFT (they fine-tuned GPT-4o and show example outputs). The evaluation emphasizes label-invariant graph construction (component types) and quantitative GED-based verification for large-scale netlist generation from images.

snippets: We use Graph Edit Distance (GED) to compare representations of two SPICE netlists.
This yields a graph “G”, where nodes are circuit components and edges are interconnections, as illustrated Figure 11.
GED (G1,G2) calculates the sum of the costs associated with transforming G1 into G2 through a series of edit operations on nodes and edges, such as insert, delete, and substitute.
We normalize the GED to yield a final metric in the range 0 to 100.
This normalization is achieved by calculating the maximum GED, obtained by summing the number of nodes and edges in both graphs and scaling accordingly.
When comparing the SPICE netlists generated by our method to those produced by AMSNet, our approach achieved a 100% similarity score, as defined earlier.
This validates the robustness of our flow and motivates us to extend the evaluation to additional circuit diagrams.

AMSNet lacks a description of the SPICE netlist present in the dataset, which hinders its performance for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), making it not ready to use for LLM-based tasks that require high-quality description alongside the code for unlocking its full potential.
MASALA-CHAI also offers such a pipeline to generate description alongside the SPICE netlist from a document, as shown Figure 5 (➑).

contexts.context: The Masala-CHAI work provides a large-scale SPICE netlist dataset used to fine-tune LLMs for automated SPICE netlist generation and benchmarking. Baseline models were fine-tuned on the MASALA-CHAI corpus: open-source models were fine-tuned using LoRA on an Nvidia A100 80GB, while proprietary GPT models were fine-tuned via their fine-tuning playgrounds. Evaluation uses Pass@k (defined in the paper) with n = 15 trials. The authors selected 20 benchmark design problems (Table II) — common amplifiers, filters, current mirrors, cascode, differential stages, diode-connected designs, logic gate, etc. Dataset characterization includes distributions of #components, #nodes, #MOSFETs, and #lines of SPICE code (Fig. 9). Results show open-source models (DeepSeek, CodeLlama) underperform proprietary GPT models, and the fine-tuned GPT-4o achieved the best result, successfully generating SPICE netlists for all evaluated design IDs. Figure 10 highlights representative success and failure cases (e.g., NMOS used improperly as a current source, diode-connected transistor gate miswired); fine-tuning resolved issues like floating nets. Further analyses are in the paper appendices.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, a multi-agent, modular pipeline for end-to-end analog circuit design that is relevant to netlist generation and parameter optimization. Key components include a Candidates Module backed by a Topology Vector Database that stores netlists paired with concise two-sentence textual summaries embedded for semantic retrieval; the embedded description serves as keys while the netlist is stored as the value. A Knowledge Module runs a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to inject domain literature into agent reasoning. Stage agents (Topology Selection Agent, Topology Refinement Agent, Parameter Optimization Agent) orchestrate topology selection, refinement, and parameter estimation. The Numerical Module applies Bayesian Optimization (BO), calls a circuit simulator, evaluates metrics, and updates a surrogate model—providing simulation-grounded feedback (noted as ground-truth numerical feedback) to guide decisions. The workflow includes candidate re-ranking based on inferred constraints and a failure-analysis step (Compression Module) to analyze shortfalls. An op-amp design walk-through and specification table (GBW, Phase Margin, CMRR, PSRR, power targets) illustrate iterative search and optimization. The description focuses on analog design broadly rather than RF-specific techniques.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes MenTeR, a fully automated multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design. MenTeR composes multiple specialized AI agents that collaboratively handle specification understanding, document retrieval, testbench generation, schematic/netlist synthesis, and device sizing. The framework includes interactive checkpoints for optional human intervention and targets generation of single-block, multi-block, and ultimately system-level designs (e.g., BGR, PLL). Key methodological components to improve LLM reasoning and reliability are Diagram-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation (DA-RAG), self-referential techniques, and a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that combines domain-specific knowledge with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. The authors also leverage prompting strategies (e.g., references to Tree of Thoughts and RAG) and integrate literature analysis, mathematical reasoning, and device sizing agents to scale from simplified tasks to more complex amplifier and system designs. The excerpt claims MenTeR accelerates design cycle time, broadens the explored design space, and reduces manual trial-and-error, but it contains no quantitative results or detailed optimization metrics in the provided pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, a self-evolving, simulation-grounded framework for translating analog circuit specifications into executable netlist designs via an iterative, memory-augmented multi-agent workflow. The pipeline is driven by three stage agents: a Topology Selection Agent that converts textual performance specifications into a netlist and initializes designs via a Candidates Module, a Topology Refinement Agent that applies constrained, localized structural edits (e.g., bias or compensation adjustments), and a Parameter Optimization Agent that finalizes numerical device values. Reasoning and feedback are mediated by four Stratified Memory Layers — including Evolution Memory and Introspective Optimization — enabling cross-task knowledge transfer, self-correction from past iterations, and compressed stage context fusion. The system emphasizes retrieval-based initialization to start from validated topologies, iterative closed-loop simulation feedback, and maintaining structural validity through local edits. The authors position this approach as addressing prior limitations (over-reliance on heuristics, idealized models, limited context retention) and claim an open-source, simulation-grounded pipeline that improves across iterations and tasks for practical analog (including RF) netlist synthesis and optimization.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports MenTeR's multi-agent workflow performance, backbone comparisons, and engineering mitigations. Table III shows backbone results (GPT-4o: 84.2/89.2 Pass@1/5; GPT-4.1: 77.5/85.8). In a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) case study, AI-generated nets sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet "contained errors upon schematic inspection," primarily due to "incorrectly defined subcircuits (Frequency Detector (FD), Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO))" and "missing wire connections (VCO to FD, FD to Phase Frequency Detector (PFD), and PFD to the reference frequency source)." The authors recommend "maintaining a 'human-in-the-loop' approach" for complex system-level designs. To improve robustness, MenTeR incorporated "a self-referential technique into both multi-agent and MenTeR, ensuring all code is executable before submission," which "substantially decreased execution error rates." They also addressed "Repetitive Error Loops" by adding "an early-detection mechanism to identify such recurring errors, automatically terminating the chat when these patterns are detected." Token limits were handled by truncating chat history per agent (keeping the initial prompt and last message). On token efficiency, "MenTeR consumes more tokens for the relatively simple Task 1," but for harder tasks the single-agent approach used far more tokens and failed, indicating MenTeR's multi-agent design improved stability and task success on challenging problems.

contexts.context: The MenTeR methodology implements a simplified multi-agent workflow for end-to-end RF/analog netlist design that decomposes tasks into stages: specification understanding, document search, circuit netlist design, and test-bench generation. It uses a Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning agent that "integrates domain-specific knowledge with the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) technique," and employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)/DA-RAG for sourcing technical documents and textbooks to inform design decisions. A Meta Agent serves as a central orchestrator to analyze incoming design tasks, determine required specialist agents, decompose problems into sub-tasks, delegate to domain specialist agents, and oversee the workflow. The PI (Primary Investigator) agent interfaces with users, manages workflow, leverages DA-RAG to process requirements from multiple knowledge sources, and converts abstract requirements into well-defined specifications. The framework is intended to systematically collect reasoning paths (CoT) to enable future domain-adapted fine-tuning. Reported results indicate the multi-agent approach "already outperformed single-agent work" in their comparisons, and the authors note the framework can be further optimized for integration into existing design flows.

contexts.context: This excerpt describes an LLM-driven multi-agent design case study (AnalogSeeker) applied to a three-stage op-amp within the Atelier framework. The LLM agents are split into explicit roles: topology design, topology modification, and circuit analysis. Agents produce SPICE netlists and enumerate designable parameters and ranges. The task objective is to maximize FOM = GBW·CL / Power while meeting constraints (Gain > 85dB; GBW requirement noted as challenging; PM > 55 degrees; Power < 250 μW) with a specified load CL = 10 pF. The workflow is iterative with a back-tracking mechanism and a maximum of four iterations; Fig. 7 shows the design trajectory across iterations. In the example the topology agent initially selects an NMCF (nested Miller compensation with feedforward) topology, justified because it combines high gain and power efficiency and handles GBW/PM trade-offs for small load capacitances. The agents perform topology selection, modification, and convert designs into netlists for analysis and optimization. This provides a concrete example of an end-to-end multi-agent analog-circuit netlist-design loop, though the example focuses on op-amp (analog) rather than RF-specific circuits.

contexts.context: MenTeR is a multi-agent, end-to-end workflow for RF/analog netlist design that decomposes user requirements into assigned tasks (PI agent) and uses specialized agents for design, validation, and knowledge reuse. The Circuit Agent (CA) acts as a senior analog designer producing PySpice netlists via an internal Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning component and an Executor for syntax and basic error checks. CoS breaks design into sequential stages (requirement extraction, parameter synthesis, etc.), leverages a Circuit Think Tank (CTT) repository of validated designs and reasoning traces, and iteratively refines designs. Validated netlists are passed to the Testbench Agent (TBA), which—using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG)—defines simulations (DC sweeps, MOSFET/connection checks), generates Python code to run PySpice, and returns detailed errors for targeted refinement. All generated solutions are inspected with electrical rule checks and circuit simulations. Sub-agents (e.g., CoS Reasoning, DA-RAG) enable stagewise optimization and diagram-to-text retrieval. Evaluation uses pass@k metrics (Fig. 6) with limited attempts and GPT-4o for experiments, indicating a comparative performance analysis versus a single-agent baseline.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes EEsizer, an LLM-based agent for automated SPICE netlist sizing. The workflow requires two inputs: a SPICE netlist and target performance metrics (e.g., gain, bandwidth, phase margin). The system decomposes the sizing task into four predefined subtasks to structure LLM interaction: Task 1 (circuit identification), Task 2 (extract I/O nodes and generate simulation configurations including sources/measurement points), Task 3 (retrieve target performance values), and additional steps for simulation/analysis. Optimization runs in an action–observation–comparison ReAct loop, iterating modifications and simulations; the agent returns a modified netlist plus explicit reasoning for each parameter adjustment to improve interpretability. Example user input and targets are illustrated (SPICE netlist plus target metrics). The paper also discusses tradeoffs among LLMs (thinking models vs. large models vs. mid-sized models like Gemini 2.0 Flash) affecting reasoning depth and latency. Overall, EEsizer emphasizes structured task prompts, iterative simulation-driven optimization (e.g., invoking simulators), and transparent parameter-change explanations as outputs.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogSAGE, an open-source, self-evolving multi-agent framework for analog circuit design that coordinates three sequential agent stages—topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization—within a closed-loop, simulation-grounded workflow. It introduces a four-layer stratified memory to preserve stage-relevant context and support long-horizon reasoning while discarding irrelevant content. AnalogSAGE combines LLM-based reasoning with numerical optimization for accurate device sizing and biasing, and it explicitly learns from failed iterations by generating new topologies and incorporating simulation feedback. The authors evaluated the framework on specification-driven amplifier design tasks using the SKY130 PDK with ngspice and report substantial improvements (10× overall pass rate, 48× Pass@1, and 4× reduction in parameter search space). The implementation is released to support reproducibility. The paper contrasts this approach with prior prompt/template-based or library methods that either generate structurally invalid circuits or fail to generalize, highlighting the importance of persistently grounded experience, stratified memory, and closed-loop simulator feedback for robust end-to-end analog/netlist design automation.

snippets: AnalogSAGE, an open-source self-evolving multi-agent framework that coordinates three-stage agent explorations through four stratified memory layers, enabling iterative refinement with simulation-grounded feedback.
Figure 1: Overview of the proposed framework with three main stages: topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization.
The dashed line indicates closed-loop feedback.
which enables LLM to evolve its design capability by generating new circuit topologies and learning from failed design tasks through simulation feedback [24], similar to human designers.
We design a stratified context mechanism to selectively preserve stage-relevant information while discarding irrelevant content
Numerical optimization methods are integrated with LLM-based reasoning to provide accurate sizing and bias parameters under grounded simulation.
We release the implementation of the framework to facilitate fair comparison and accelerate the research on LLM-driven analog design.
The framework is evaluated in a set of specification-driven design tasks using the SKY130 PDK, achieving a 10× pass rate, a 48× Pass@1, and a 4× reduction in the parameter search space.

Self-evolving analog design multi-agent framework that continuously enhances reasoning and design capability through accumulated knowledge, enabling transferable domain expertise and strategy refinement from both successful and failed design iterations.

A stratified memory architecture that organizes and condenses essential design knowledge through four structured memory layers to support reasoning across different design stages within limited context windows.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, a training-free analog design agent that reframes analog topology synthesis as a conditional, real-world SPICE code generation task. Key components that could inform an end-to-end RF analog netlist workflow include: (1) a subcircuit library and a compact analog circuit representation to constrain and simplify generation; (2) a domain-specific prompting flow using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and in-context learning to guide LLMs through design steps; (3) iterative, human experience–based proofreading to enable multi-round correction when single-round generation fails on complex tasks. The method emphasizes generating SPICE-level netlists directly (rather than idealized or Python representations) and integrating human design expertise to improve correctness. Together, these design choices (task formulation, representation, guided prompting, and iterative human-in-the-loop correction) are presented as a practical pipeline that yields decent performance on conditional topology synthesis and could be adapted or extended for multi-agent workflows, agent specification, and optimization in RF netlist design.

contexts.context: Poddar et al. (DATE 2024) present a data-driven analog circuit synthesizer that performs automatic topology selection and device sizing. The method uses a modular approach to build a comprehensive, parameterized circuit topology library and constructs an adaptive topology dataset rather than relying on an exhaustive precomputed set. A one-time data collection effort gathers simulation-based performance data for each topology; this dataset is then enriched with synthetic performance data generated using variational autoencoders (VAE) to improve coverage and density. For device sizing and performance data generation the authors employ automatic analog sizers and leverage DNN-Opt. The workflow ranks candidate topologies from the repository for a target specification, verifies the chosen topology, and performs automatic sizing and re-optimization. The approach is validated on operational amplifiers (OPAMPs), with experiments involving over 360 OPAMP topologies and over 540K data points, guided by a Figure of Merit (FOM). The paper reports that the system "can generate optimal-sized topology netlists in approximately 10 minutes," emphasizing scalability and reduced runtime compared to simulation-in-the-loop methods.

contexts.context: The paper describes a data-driven flow for analog (OTA/OPAMP) synthesis that combines simulation-based sizing with VAE-generated synthetic performance data. They use DNN-Opt, a multi-threaded, online, learning-based optimizer (actor-critic + population methods) to size devices and build a simulation dataset. Designs are evaluated with a Figure-of-Merit (FoM) that combines an objective f0(x) and clipped constraint terms (FoM formula provided). The overall pipeline is two-stage: (1) performance dataset generation — a one-time effort that creates a parameterized topology library and a data repository per topology using DNN-Opt simulations augmented with VAE synthetic samples; and (2) topology selection, verification and automatic sizing leveraging that repository. The work focuses on OPAMPs/OTAs and enumerates a set of performance metrics used for queries. Topologies are generated modularly: common analog circuits are decomposed into parameterized modules (e.g., differential pairs, loads, tail current, bias, compensation) where MOSFET sizes act as parameters. VAEs are used as the generative model, trained using the ELBO, to enrich scarce simulation data with synthetic performance points.

contexts.context: Poddar et al. (2024) present a data-driven analog synthesis flow that combines automated topology selection with device sizing. They introduce a constraint-based modular approach to create parameterized analog topology libraries from scratch, then collect simulation-based performance data and VAE-generated synthetic data in a one-time effort to capture each topology’s capabilities. The VAE (variational autoencoder) is used to generate synthetic samples that augment sparse simulation data, improving ranking and sizing. The method pairs binary search with synthetic-data-driven ranking, verification, and an initial data-driven sizing strategy, enabling production of optimal-sized topology netlists in minutes (e.g., a netlist in 6.87 minutes for a test case). The authors validated the approach across 360+ diverse OPAMP topologies and compared results in Table III: in several test cases binary search fails when nearby data are scarce, while the synthetic-data approach better identifies, ranks, and sizes topologies; in at least one case (test case 4) binary search outperformed the synthetic approach. The work shows VAE-augmented data and binary search are complementary for fast topology selection and sizing.

snippets: CktGNN (Dong et al., 2023), formulates the topology design as a graph generation task,
It uses a graph variational autoencoder (VAE) to generate various circuit topologies for a specific type of analog ICs, i.e., operational-amplifiers (Op-Amps).
LaMAGIC (Chang et al., 2024), a fine-tuned masked language model (MLM), has been proposed to generate analog circuits with a fixed number of graph nodes.
AnalogCoder (Lai et al., 2024), another LLM-based work, uses domain-specific prompt engineering to generate analog circuits from well-established LLM models (e.g., GPT-4).

A major obstacle to advancing generative models for scalable analog circuit design automation is the lack of a comprehensive dataset of analog circuit topologies. We bridge this gap by building a extensive dataset that consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies with diverse functionalities

contexts.context: The paper describes an automated, modular OPAMP synthesis flow that uses SKILL automation to quickly build a parameterized library of standard topologies ("initial foundational library comprising approximately 4,000 valid standard parameterized OPAMP topologies"). Designers curated a representative subset of ~360 topologies for study. Each topology is sized using an automatic analog sizer (DNN-Opt) to obtain SPICE-based performance metrics; the authors limited simulations to ~1,000 data points per topology and used six objective/constraint configurations to increase diversity. They apply a constraint-based valid-topology filter (e.g., "merging an NMOS-input pair with a PMOS tail current would generate an invalid topology") to prune incompatible combinations. Two topology-selection paths are used: (1) Binary Search based Topology Selection over the real simulation dataset (sorted into metric lists; binary search O(log n)) to produce candidate topologies, and (2) Synthetic Data based Topology Ranking using variational autoencoders to generate synthetic performance points, compute a FoM, sort topologies, and take the top three for verification. Final verification and sizing invoke DNN-Opt on the ranked candidates and compare the final sized topology from the synthetic approach against the binary-search result by performance.

contexts.context: This excerpt from Zhao and Zhang (DATE 2022) describes a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach for analog circuit topology synthesis and contrasts it with prior methods (MOJITO, FEATS, GCTC, KMR). The authors claim their design search space is "flexible and controllable," which can be realized "by extending PBBL or changing state size." Comparisons use the same building-block library (PBBL) and identical I/O specifications. The paper defines "structure size" as the maximum block number, maximum leave number, and maximum state size allowed in each method. The DRL process is stochastic; reported results are averages over ten runs, and each learning run takes about 2–9 hours. In contrast, MOJITO required evaluating ~101,904 structures to yield 512 unique structures (taking 7 days), and KMR requires a time-consuming data-mining step. The excerpt emphasizes lower computation effort for the proposed DRL method versus some prior approaches. The provided text does not mention hash tables or symbolic-analysis techniques for topology generation or specification hashing, nor implementation details of any hash-table or symbolic analysis mechanisms.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Zhao & Zhang's DRL framework for analog circuit topology synthesis. Topologies are built from building blocks (BBs); circuits that are structurally identical but have swapped input pins are treated as distinct, and the decoded structure should reproduce swapped-pin copies. Except for differential pairs, all transistors within a BB are constrained to equal length and width; two special nets-only BBs have I/O combinations dependent on input order. Decoded structures undergo a fast symbolic evaluation: the GPDD algorithm numerically computes DC gain of unsized circuits, and the quality filter is deliberately relaxed so only very poor designs are filtered out. Passing candidates are sized with a modified NSGA-II using SPICE (CMOS 65nm) to verify feasibility rather than optimize; population and generation sizes are 20 and 50, and the routine early-terminates and stores (performance, size) in a hash table when a candidate meets the target spec. DRL agent settings reported: state size 10, action size 20; PGNN with two hidden layers (300, 200 neurons); learning rate 0.002, discount 0.95, batch 200; initial state is a PMOS differential pair (BB 26). The method produces far fewer unique structures than prior brute-force methods.
