contexts.context: Zhao & Zhang propose a DRL-based framework for automatic circuit structure (topology) synthesis that constructs circuits by selecting trustworthy building blocks (BBs) from a predefined building block library (PBBL). Design specifications are split into input-output (I/O) specifications, which form the RL environment with the PBBL, and performance specifications, which are used to compute rewards. BB terminals are categorized as V, FI, and FO, and six BB types are defined based on terminal counts and types. States are encoded as fixed-size arrays where each slot holds a BB index (or zero if empty); a single state can sometimes decode to multiple concrete circuit structures when inter-BB connections are ambiguous. Only a subset of states meeting certain conditions are decoded and fully evaluated. The agent’s policy is implemented as a policy gradient neural network (PGNN) producing action probabilities; episodes end on unmatched actions, state boundary violations, or explicit terminational actions. The reward function gives zero for non-terminal steps and assigns positive or negative rewards at terminal steps to encourage or discourage the resulting structures.

snippets: Trustworthy BBs are treated as the basic components to construct circuit structures, which are selected from a predefined building block library (PBBL) by taking actions.
The design specifications are composed of input-output (I/O) specification and performance specification.
The input and output terminals of a BB can be categorized into three types: V, FI, and FO.
Based on the counts and types of input and output terminals, we have defined six types of BBs.
In the proposed DRL-based framework, states are in the representation of an array.
However, if the detailed connections among the represented BBs are unclear, one state may be able to be decoded into several circuit structures.
It is worth noticing that only a few states that satisfy certain conditions, which will be explained in detail later, need to be decoded into circuit structures for evaluation.
the policy is modeled as a policy gradient neural network (PGNN), which takes a state as input and outputs the probability distribution over all actions.
An episode would terminate when any of the following three cases have appeared: 1) an unmatched action is taken; 2) state boundary is exceeded; 3) a terminational action is taken.

As the reward function indicated, all the non-terminal steps would receive a reward 0, while for the terminal step, a positive or negative reward  is assigned to encourage or discourage the generation of corresponding circuit structure, respectively.

contexts.context: Zhao & Zhang (2022) propose a DRL-based approach for analog circuit structure (topology) synthesis that blends structure generation and reasoning. The method begins from a starting sub-circuit and incrementally expands topology using building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process guided by reinforcement learning; necessary design rules are encoded to create a specialized RL environment. Evaluation efficiency is improved by using hash tables and symbolic-analysis techniques: these reduce the number of produced topologies that require costly sizing/simulation and enable faster verification of generated circuits. The authors claim this is the first application of DRL to automate analog-IC structure synthesis. Key reported advantages include framing synthesis as a decision process, providing a flexible and controllable design search space, enabling the automatic learning of design knowledge, and substantially lowering computation compared with conventional structure-generation methods.

snippets: This paper presents a novel deep-reinforcement-    learning (DRL)-based method, which integrates the merits of the structure generation and structure reasoning methods.
Similar to the structure reasoning method, our proposed DRL-based method starts with a starting sub-circuit and gradually expands it with building blocks (BBs) via a decision-making process;
Necessary design rules are defined    and  applied  to  set  up  the  specialized  environment  of    reinforcement learning in order to reasonably construct circuit
The produced circuit structures are then verified by symbolic analysis techniques are employed to significantly promote the evaluation efficiency.
hash table and symbolic analysis techniques are employed to significantly reduce the number of the produced structures to be sized during the synthesis process.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that employs DRL to automate the structure synthesis of analog integrated circuits.
1)  The synthesis process is a decision-making process;
2)  Design search space is flexible and controllable;
3)  Design knowledge is automatically learned;
4)  Considerably less computation effort compared with the conventional circuit structure generation methods.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes LaMAGIC2, an LLM-based analog topology generation approach that uses supervised sequence-to-sequence finetuning and a novel representation called SFCI (succinct float-input canonical formulation with identifier). SFCI encodes numeric specifications as floats and uses identifiers to improve component-type recognition, which reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|), shrinking LM input size. The training follows a two-phase finetuning/transfer workflow: large-scale finetuning on many small (3–5 component) circuits followed by targeted finetuning on fewer larger (6-component) circuits. Reported evaluation shows substantial gains: 34% higher success under a tight 0.01 tolerance and 10× lower MSE versus a prior method, plus reduced sequence lengths (LaMAGIC2-SFCI outputs ~36–41 tokens vs LaMAGIC-FM ~85–105 tokens). Code is publicly available. These points are relevant to methodologies that use two-phase generation/refinement with representation changes to improve LLM-based circuit synthesis and transfer to larger topologies.

contexts.context: The paper proposes CIRCUITSYNTH, a two-phase approach for circuit topology synthesis using pretrained LLMs. Phase 1 (Circuit Topology Generation) frames topology creation as autoregressive text generation using an "incident encoding" of netlists, sampling Y from pLLM(Y|X) and trained with a negative log-likelihood objective. Generated circuits may violate validity constraints. Phase 2 (Circuit Topology Refinement) adds: (1) a Circuit Validity Estimation module — a frozen RoBERTa-based classifier that outputs pvalid (used to compute Lvalid = (1 − pvalid)) and achieves an F1 score of 92% for validity classification; and (2) a Generation Enhancement step that combines the LLM loss and validity loss into L = λ1LLLM + λ2Lvalid to steer generation toward valid designs. To enable gradient flow through discrete samples, the method uses the Gumbel-softmax trick. They explore training strategies including In-Context Learning (ICL) with k ∈ {5,10,20} examples, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with 100–500 soft prompt tokens, and vanilla fine-tuning; models evaluated include Llama-2 (13b), Flan-Ul2 (20b), GPT-Neo and StableLM.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, an LLM-based analog topology synthesis system that is training-free and uses a subcircuit-based circuit representation combined with chain-of-thought (CoT) and in-context learning plus human experience-based proofreading to mimic human design steps. The authors evaluate on both real and synthetic structural-requirement benchmarks and report overall correct ratios of 40% on real data and 23% on synthetic data, with per-task breakdowns shown (e.g., Average 629/2000 = 31% up to 801/2000 = 40%). Figure 5 highlights three real cases with both successful and failed circuit diagrams; common failure modes include connection errors (e.g., floating current output/input terminals) and block errors (e.g., wrong cascode stage type, NMOS vs PMOS). Limitations noted include a small real dataset; future directions emphasize generating high-quality synthetic SPICE topologies and fine-tuning smaller models for improved performance and data security. These points provide concrete methodological components, evaluation metrics, failure categories, and dataset/scale considerations that could inform design, two-phase generation/refinement strategies, and evaluation for a CircuitSynth-style LLM approach.

snippets: AnalogXpert, a powerful analog topology synthesis tool based on training-free LLMs.

AnalogXpert leverages the subcircuit-based circuit representation, CoT&in-context learning, and human experience-based proofreading to imitate the human design process and improve the design accuracy.
Both real data and synthetic data benchmarks are constructed on the structural requirements tasks.
The experimental results (40% and 23% correct ratio in real and synthetic data) demonstrate the effectiveness of the AnalogXpert.
Figure 5: Three real cases with successful circuit diagrams and failed circuit diagrams.
Failed Circuit Diagram
Connection Error: Floating current output(I/O)、current input(I/I) terminal
Block Error: Wrong Cascode stage type, NMOS not PMOS
Limitation and Future Directions. Although this work has constructed both a real dataset and a synthetic dataset, the size of the real dataset is still small.
In the future, AnalogXpert can be used to generate sufficient synthetic data of the analog circuit topologies in the SPICE code format.
With high-quality synthetic data, some small models can be fine-tuned to achieve a competitive performance.
Having fine-tuned mini-models that can be run locally ensures the security of design data, which is important for commercial circuit design companies.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, a training-free LLM-based analog topology synthesis agent that embeds human design expertise. It reformulates topology synthesis as a real-world SPICE code generation problem, representing devices and their connections directly in SPICE rather than using ideal models or Python-code formulations. To make generation concise and tractable, AnalogXpert leverages a well-designed subcircuit library to create an effective analog circuit representation. Its prompting strategy is domain-specific: a chain-of-thought (CoT) based prompting flow is used and further enhanced by in-context learning examples. Because single-round LLM generation often fails on complex designs, AnalogXpert adds human experience–based proofreading: an iterative, multi-round refinement where human-like corrections guide the model to gradually fix mistakes and converge on a correct topology. The approach is presented as a staged flow (design requirement -> subcircuit/block representation -> CoT & in-context prompting -> human proofreading) and is reported to achieve decent performance on conditional analog topology synthesis tasks.
contexts.question: CircuitSynth LLM circuit topology synthesis methodology two-phase generation refinement results evaluation

contexts.context: The paper presents CIRCUITSYNTH, a two-phase LLM-based approach for automatic circuit topology synthesis. Phase 1 (Circuit Topology Generation) fine-tunes an LLM to produce netlist-style circuit topologies in an autoregressive manner. Phase 2 (Circuit Topology Refinement) applies a learned circuit validity classifier to estimate the likelihood a generated topology is valid, and uses a Generation Enhancement step that refines generation by minimizing a combined loss: negative log-likelihood plus a circuit invalidity score. The authors curated a large dataset of power-converter circuits (862,606 examples: 567,307 valid and 295,299 invalid) composed of five device types (capacitors, inductors, phase-I switches Sa and phase-II switches Sb, etc.), generated via Random Search and NGSpice. For evaluation, CIRCUITSYNTH-generated topologies are passed to a SPICE simulator to verify validity. Experimental results report that CIRCUITSYNTH outperforms various fine-tuned LLM variants. Key contributions include the method, the dataset, and the circuit validity classifier.

snippets: CIRCUITSYNTH employs a sophisticated two-phase methodology, comprising Circuit Topology Generation and Circuit Topology Refinement.
we fine-tune a LLM to produce a circuit topology in an autoregressive manner.
we employ a classifier to gauge the likelihood that a generated circuit topology adheres to the requisites of a valid circuit design.
we refine the circuit topology generation process, by minimizing the combined loss of negative log-likelihood with the circuit invalidity score.
we evaluate our model by generating new circuit topologies using the trained model and passing them to the SPICE simulator to verify their validity.

We curated a dataset of power converter circuit designs with 5 device components using Random Search (RS) [2] and NGSpice [3] simulator. Together we collected a total of 862,606 circuits (567,307 valid and 295,299 invalid ones).

Contributions: The key contributions are summarized as follows: (a) Introduction of CIRCUITSYNTH, a novel methodology leveraging LLMs for automated circuit topology synthesis; (b) Generation of a comprehensive dataset encompassing both valid and invalid circuit configurations, facilitating the development and evaluation of CIRCUITSYNTH; (c) Development of a circuit validity classifier to assess a circuit’s validity, enhancing the reliability of CIRCUITSYNTH outputs.
Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of CIRCUITSYNTH compared to various LLM variants.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HeaRT’s topology-focused methodology for AMS circuits. It first converts SPICE netlists into a device–net bipartite graph and extracts DC-connected components to reveal biasing structure; these components guide LLM-assisted decomposition with Chain-of-Thought metadata. A topology knowledge database organizes normalized circuit graphs by functional category and stores metric-wise ranks ri(T) plus Weisfeiler–Lehman (WL) fingerprints for fast lookup. HeaRT computes an aggregate rank-based Figure of Merit (FoMrank = Σ wi × ri(T)) over a feasible constraint set ϕ(U) to retrieve topologies that satisfy performance constraints. The agent does a query-conditioned traversal of a hierarchical circuit reasoning tree, using edge relevance weights and a branch-admission criterion where τstop and ϵ control expansion. Evaluation is performed on a curated repository of 40 AMS circuits (flattened SPICE), categorized by transistor-count complexity (Simple, Medium, Hard), and tested across multiple LLM backbones (e.g., LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct).

snippets: Graph-Guided Reasoning for Tree Construction: We first transform the SPICE-level circuit netlist into a device–net bipartite graph G = (VD, VN, E), where device nodes VD represents circuit components (e.g., transistors, resistors, capacitors), and net nodes VN correspond to unique net names.
From this graph, we extract DC-connected components that capture conductive paths between supply rails, exposing the circuit’s biasing structure

These components serve as structural cues for LLM-assisted circuit decomposition into the functional subcircuits defined in Section II-B, with metadata generated via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning [37]
Topology Knowledge Database Management: As illustrated in Fig. 2 (right), we maintain a topology knowledge database in which circuits are organized by functional category.
Each topology entry stores a metric-wise rank ri(T) that reflects its relative performance capability along metric i.
Each topology entry additionally stores a Weisfeiler–Lehman (WL) graph fingerprint [58] as an auxiliary hash index.
We compute an aggregate rank-based Figure of Merit (FoMrank) as a fast ranking proxy, defined as: min FoMrank(T) = Σ wi × ri(T), T ∈ ϕ(U)
ϕ(U) = {T | |rj(T) − ¯rj| ≤ ϵ, ∀j ∈ U}
In this stage, the agent performs a query-conditioned traversal of the hierarchical circuit reasoning tree.

where w(v,u) is the query-conditioned relevance of child u, τstop halts expansion when all children are weak, and ϵ stops expansion when all children are similarly strong (i.e., no dominant direction).

We evaluate HeaRT’s structural circuit decomposition and feedback loop identification on a curated repository of 40 AMS circuits spanning diverse types and complexity levels (Fig. 4), provided as flattened SPICE netlists.

Circuits are categorized into three tiers based on transistor count (excluding digital logic components) as a rough proxy for structural complexity: Simple (< 20 transistors), Medium (20–40 transistors), and Hard (> 40 transistors).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Circuitsynth's dataset, training, and evaluation setup for LLM-driven power-converter topology synthesis. A dataset of 862,606 circuits (567,307 valid, 295,299 invalid) was created by random search and NGSpice simulation; valid circuits include collected efficiency values. Designs use 5 device components (capacitors C, inductors L, phase-I switches Sa, phase-II switches Sb) plus three external ports (’IN’, ’OUT’, ’0’) yielding 13 ports total; device parameters were fixed (C=10µF, L=100µH). Training used shuffled data for 5–7 epochs, gradient checkpointing, AdamW (betas 0.9, 0.95; eps 1.0E-8), learning rate 0.95E-5, and seed 42; checkpoints saved based on validation performance. During training they evaluate 100 sample generations per check. For inference/evaluation, each model generates 1000 unique samples using nucleus + top-k sampling; generated topologies are fed to a validity classifier to compute percentage valid generations. Experiments ran on PyTorch v2.2.0 with CUDA 11.8 on NVIDIA V100 (32 GB). The excerpt does not explicitly describe a "two-phase generation/refinement" procedure or detailed refinement results.

In this study we only consider devices including capacitors C, inductors L, phase-I switches Sa and phase-II switches Sb.

During training, we evaluate a subset of 100 sample generations and employ identical evaluation settings to monitor performance, saving the checkpoint if the current performance surpasses that of the previous epoch.
We utilize shuffled data from the training split for 5-7 epochs, saving the model checkpoint with the best performance on the validation split.
we implement gradient checkpointing.
we employ the AdamW optimizer [14] with beta parameters set to 0.9 and 0.95 respectively, and an epsilon value of 1.0E-8.
we set the learning rate to 0.95E-5 and fix the seed to 42 for reproducibility purposes.
We assess all models by generating 1000 unique sample circuit topologies using each model.

the generated circuit topologies are inputted into the validity classifier to determine the percentage of valid generations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogXpert, an LLM-based agent for analog topology synthesis formulated as SPICE code generation. Key methodological elements include (1) a customized, extensible subcircuit library to reduce the design space and produce subcircuit-level outputs rather than device-level outputs, aligning with human design practice and shortening model output; (2) task decomposition emulating human design by splitting synthesis into block (subcircuit) selection and block connection/connection-graph construction, and using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting to generate topologies step-by-step; (3) a proofreading strategy that checks generated topologies and returns revision messages to the LLM for iterative self-refinement. The authors built a benchmark with 30 real and 2k synthetic tasks to evaluate the approach. Reported results show AnalogXpert attains 40% success on synthetic and 23% on real data, significantly outperforming GPT-4o (3%/3%), demonstrating improved generation success and robustness through design-space reduction, decomposition, and iterative refinement.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a two-phase, LDE-aware circuit synthesis flow comprised of four modules. Phase 1 (symbolic sizing) contains Module-I and Module-II, both formulated as MINLP problems: Module-I performs LDE-free gm/ID-based sizing (initialization, L-determination, device characterization and curve fitting) to produce baseline (W, L, nf) solutions; Module-II performs LDE-aware optimization by replacing design/intermediate variables with symbolic expressions (X1, X2 and ξi), optimizing W and nf first, then geometrical LDE-related parameters (LRext, SCt) in a second round so SA/SB and SCA are reflected in simulation netlists. Phase 2 (many-objective EA-based sizing) uses Module-III (a many-objective evolutionary algorithm) to refine LDE-aware solutions and optimize device and layout factors further. Module-IV performs layout synthesis (placement and routing) using Module-III outputs. The flow iterates on verification failures. The text also defines LDE-relevant distances (SCt, SA/SB, SABeff/SABedge) and finger-related parameters (nf, sd, bs) used in modeling layout-dependent effects.

snippets: Our proposed two-phase synthesis flow shown in Fig. 14 is comprised of four main modules.

In the symbolic sizing phase as depicted in Fig. 14(a), Module-I and Module-II, which model the circuit sizing problem by using mixed integer nonlinear programming (MINLP), generate the LDE-free and LDE-aware solutions, respectively.
the heuristic many-OEA-based sizing Module-III takes the initial solutions from the previous module and seeks refined LDE-aware solutions.

The device parameters and other layout factors optimized with LDE-awareness, which are included in Module-III’s output solutions, will be used to perform the layout synthesis through placement and routing inside Module-IV.
The flow iterates if failures emerge
The first module, LDE-free gm/ID-based sizing, starts with the initialization and L-determination

These fitted characterization terms will be used in the objective function and constraints built for any MINLP problems, whose solving is called LDE-free sizing that employs the gm/ID-based circuit sizing approach discussed in Section 4.3.
Module-II is to perform an LDE-aware optimization process based on the LDE-free sizing solution obtained from Module-I.

Since the MOSFET device parameters of W (i.e., X1) and nf (i.e., X2) play an important role not only in affecting LDEs but also in device characterization, they are optimized in the first round of Module-II.

It is followed by the second round (indicated by the dash arrows in Fig. 14) to further optimize the other LDE-related geometrical parameters of LRext and SCt by symbolically involving them into SA/SB (i.e., X1) and SCA (i.e., X2), which are present in the simulation netlist for reflecting LDEs.

contexts.context: The paper describes CIRCUITSYNTH (CS), a two-phase methodology for circuit-topology generation using LLMs with a refinement step driven by a circuit validity classifier. During training they address non-differentiable sampling from the LLM categorical distribution by using the Gumbel-Max trick and the Gumbel-softmax relaxation, combined with a straight-through estimator to enable gradient flow. They experiment with prompt tuning (100–500 soft prompt tokens) and vanilla fine-tuning of GPT-Neo-2.7 and StableLM-3B-4E1T. CIRCUITSYNTH augments fine-tuned generation with an independent validity classifier and uses SPICE simulations for ground-truth evaluation. Metrics reported include E(fvalid(ŷ)) from the classifier, E(fSvalid(ŷ)) and E(fSeff(ŷ)) from SPICE, computed over 1000 unique samples; a topology is labeled valid if the classifier score > 0.6. They also measure Duplicate Generation Rate (DGR). Results in Table I show CIRCUITSYNTH models outperform baselines in validity and efficiency (examples: CSGPT-Neo E(fvalid)=0.648, E(fSvalid)=0.713; CSStableLM E(fSvalid)=0.728). A two-sample t-test shows classifier predictions correlate strongly with simulator validity (p < 0.05). An ablation studies natural-language incident encoding’s impact.

contexts.context: The paper evaluates multiple GAN variants (GAN, WGAN, improved WGAN, DCGAN) for circuit-topology synthesis, including a low-pass filter case. Architectures: fully connected WGAN/improved WGANs use four layers with hidden sizes 128, 256, 512; the improved WGAN uses weight clipping and a regularization term λ = 100 and is trained with RMSProp. DCGAN generator uses deconvolutional layers (BS × 5×5×128, 9×9×64, 18×18×32) producing a circuit topology tensor BS × 36×36×1 and uses batch-norm + LReLU in early layers and deconvolutional-tanh output; DCGANs are trained with Adam. The generator last-layer activation uses tanh (inputs transformed to [−1,1]). Evaluation: they draw 20,000 training samples from the complete set Ω; latent vector size 64. Generated sets M are filtered and compared to Ω; metric P = |T|/|M| measures fraction of feasible topologies (T ⊂ M ∩ Ω). Generated circuits are post-filtered with NSCs and can be passed to predictive models (active learning) for performance estimation. Dataset sizes for the two problems are 43,249 and 123,156 (low-pass likely the larger). For the frequency-response case the improved WGAN achieved 1,990/10,000 feasible samples (≈19.9%), WGAN ≈3.44%, while GAN and DCGAN performed worst; similar evaluation procedures apply to the low-pass filter experiments.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes applying GAN variants to analog circuit topology synthesis where circuits are represented as labeled graphs and encoded via adjacency matrices converted to fixed-length vectors for ML. Two target problems are studied: frequency response matching and low-pass filter realizability. Large enumerated datasets are used as training data: 43,249 unique circuit topologies for the frequency task and 1,804,496 unique graphs (123,156 with up to 7 components) for the LPF problem. Several GAN variants are compared (GAN, WGAN, improved WGAN, DGGAN) with generator/discriminator implemented as either fully-connected MLPs or convolutional models. Input latent vector nz is drawn from a standard multivariate normal. GAN training details include flattened adjacency inputs (465×1 and 630×1), a 5:1 discriminator-to-generator update ratio, LReLU and tanh activations in the generator, and for WGANs four fully-connected layers with weight truncation to [−0.01, 0.01] and RMSProp optimization. Improved WGANs add a gradient-penalty term (regularization Î»=100) to enforce the K-Lipschitz constraint and mitigate issues from weight clipping (e.g., unstable training, slow convergence, vanishing gradients). Hidden layer sizes of 128, 256, 512 are used in some architectures.

contexts.context: The paper evaluates several GAN variants for analog circuit topology synthesis (frequency response and low-pass filter problems). The improved WGAN produced the highest fraction of feasible topologies (e.g., 1,990/10,000 for one case) and its feasibility rate rises markedly with longer training (up to 42.66% at 50,000 iterations). Standard WGAN, GAN, and DCGAN performed worse (3.44%, 1.51%, and 0.36% feasible, respectively). A parametric study showed the improved WGAN performed best with 64 latent variables and a penalty λ = 10 (19.90% feasible for the frequency problem); similar trends appeared for the low-pass filter (64 latent variables and λ = 10 giving 14.55% feasible). An active learning loop combined with the GAN generator yielded 2,973 feasible circuits out of 10,000 and produced predictive models evaluated by RMSE and normalized Kendall tau (Set 1 RMSE=2.4942, ˜K=0.2631; Set 2 RMSE=2.9266, ˜K=0.2557). The authors note DCGAN’s poor results may stem from insufficient training time on CPU TensorFlow implementations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes using generative adversarial networks (GANs) as an indirect design representation to generate circuit topologies for Case 2 synthesis problems where enumeration is impractical. GANs are chosen over VAEs because they generally produce sharper samples and do not require variational bounds or Markov chains. The generator maps low-dimensional latent vectors to candidate circuit topologies while a discriminator penalizes unrealistic designs. The authors train GANs on a statistical sample X of feasible topologies and produce a generated set M; effectiveness is measured by the feasibility ratio |T|/|M|, where T is the subset of generated designs that are actually feasible. The methodology is evaluated with several GAN variants and two electric circuit case studies: a frequency response optimization task and a low-pass filter feasibility problem. The approach trades some solution accuracy for greater representational scope: introducing a GAN reduces design representation dimensionality to make richer topology spaces practically solvable, at the cost of potentially incomplete coverage of the true feasible set. Quantitative results are not present in the provided excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies that targets the challenging topology-generation stage of analog IC design. AnalogGenie addresses two gaps: assembling a comprehensive dataset ("more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies with diverse functionalities") and developing "a scalable sequence-based graph representation universal to analog circuits." The paper contrasts prior generative approaches: CktGNN framed topology generation with a graph variational autoencoder (VAE) for op-amps, LaMAGIC used a masked language model for small graphs, and AnalogCoder produced PySpice code that yields SPICE netlists. Experimental claims are that AnalogGenie broadens the variety of analog ICs, increases device counts per design, and discovers unseen topologies. The authors position topology generation as the most creative and foundational stage in analog IC design and make source code available. Notably, the excerpt does not discuss GAN (generative adversarial network) architectures, low-pass filter-specific methodologies, nor per-design simulation results for filters, so direct information about GAN-based circuit synthesis or low-pass filter generation results is not provided here.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a generative engine for analog circuit topologies that uses a sequence-based representation rather than GANs. Topologies are modeled as finite connected undirected graphs and converted to directed graphs so they can be represented as Eulerian circuits; this stores only existing edges and is more efficient than adjacency matrices for sparse analog circuits. The authors prove the directed conversion contains at least one Eulerian circuit starting from any node. To address permutation invariance and overfitting, AnalogGenie generates multiple unique Eulerian circuits per topology (stating this yields 70× more data), uses BFS node ordering (GraphRNN approach) within device types, and fixes VSS as the sequence start. A custom tokenizer maps device pins to tokens (each token represents a device pin), includes truncate/padding tokens, and the model is pre-trained to predict the next device pin in the sequence. The device pin–level graph representation enforces a unique mapping from graph to circuit, and the dataset contains 3350 topologies across 11 circuit families. The excerpt does not describe GAN-based training nor low-pass filter–specific methodology or results.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. The authors represent circuit topologies using a device pin-level graph and identify multiple Eulerian circuits via depth-first search (DFS), which appears to be part of how topologies are enumerated or serialized. The paper demonstrates synthesis results by visualizing generated schematics in Cadence Virtuoso and reports several novel generated circuits with performance numbers: an op-amp (GBW=12 MHz, CL=100 pF, Power=32.88 mW, FoM=36.5), a DC converter (Efficiency=0.95, VCR=2.35, FoM=3.3), and a bandgap reference (TC=3 ppm/°C, Line regulation=0.196 %/V, PSRR=70 dB, FoM=21.9). The model shows zero-shot capability by producing a transconductance amplifier type not present in the training dataset. The paper also includes failed examples. contexts.context: The excerpt describes AnalogGenie, a GPT-based generative engine for analog circuit topology generation. The authors built an extensive dataset of over 3,000 distinct analog topologies across many functions and applied augmentation to expand the dataset by more than 70×. They identify limitations in prior representations (high-level text/SPICE and fixed-node graph methods) and propose a scalable, sequence-style, pin-level graph representation: each circuit is an undirected graph with nodes as device pins which is sequentialized into an Eulerian circuit (a trail that visits every edge exactly once and starts and ends at the same node). AnalogGenie generates topologies by predicting the next device pin to connect, leveraging the sequence representation to improve scalability and reduce ambiguity versus previous approaches. The excerpt also notes the relationship between graph representations and SPICE netlists, and claims AnalogGenie can produce diverse, large, unseen topologies and surpass prior graph- and foundation-model methods. The excerpt does not mention GAN-based methods, adversarial training, or specific low-pass filter design methodology or results.

doc.formatted_citation: Jian Gao, Weidong Cao, Junyi Yang, and Xuan Zhang. Analoggenie: a generative engine for automatic discovery of analog circuit topologies. ArXiv, Feb 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.00205. This article has 51 citations.

snippets: We bridge this gap by building a extensive dataset that consists of more than 3000 distinct analog circuit topologies with diverse functionalities
we apply data augmentation techniques to expand these circuit topologies by over 70×.
We propose a scalable sequence-style data structure that captures fundamental analog circuit design details while efficiently describing large circuit graphs.
we represent each circuit topology as an undirected graph where each node is a device pin
sequentialize it into an Eulerian circuit–a trail that visits every edge exactly once and starts and ends at the same node.
(1) We propose a generative engine, AnalogGenie, built on a GPT model to generate diverse analog circuits by predicting the next device pin to connect in the circuit;
(2) We introduce a sequence-based, pin-level graph representation that efficiently and expressively captures large analog circuit topologies;
A SPICE netlist is a text-based high-level description of the connection between devices (i.e., nets) in a circuit topology

contexts.context: Guo et al. propose a GAN-based framework for circuit synthesis that maps a lower-dimensional latent space to circuit topologies, using a generator to produce topologies and a discriminator to improve generator quality. The paper compares multiple GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) and conducts numerical experiments to evaluate their ability to generate novel circuit topologies and support design exploration. Two case studies are used to demonstrate the approach: frequency response matching and low-pass filter realizability. The motivation is to address synthesis problems too large for enumeration methods: while recent efficient enumeration algorithms can handle moderately sized catalogs and have been used to generate data, enumeration becomes impractical for large component sets. The authors position GANs as a way to solve larger synthesis problems and to gain deeper design insights. The paper also situates the GAN approach relative to prior methods (domain-knowledge, metaheuristics, indirect encodings) and notes issues with metaheuristics such as high-dimensional direct encodings and tuning needs. Sizing optimization (continuous parameter tuning) remains a separate step after topology generation.

snippets: In this article, we present a generative adversarial network (GAN) design framework for circuit synthesis design.
A GAN is a generative unsupervised learning model where a lower-dimensional latent space is predeﬁned as an abstract representation of the circuit topology space.
The GAN is trained to learn the mapping from the latent space to circuit topology space.
Circuit topologies can be produced using the generator, and a discriminator helps to improve generator quality.
A comparative study is conducted using several recent GAN models, including GANs, DCGANs, WGANs, and improved WGANs.
We designed and carried out a series of numerical experiments to evaluate the ability of these GANs to generate new circuit topologies and support design exploration.
Two circuit synthesis case studies are presented here: frequency response matching and low-pass ﬁlter realizability.

Recent advances in eﬃcient circuit topology enumeration methods have proven to be useful for circuit synthesis problem with moderately-sized component catalogs; enumeration methods, however, cannot be used as a solution method for problems with large catalogs.

As a strategy to generate data to support the creation of more eﬀective generative methods, Herber and Allison developed an eﬃcient enumeration strategy for general synthesis problems based on perfect matching graph theory and a brute-force search algorithm.

The work presented here is an eﬀort to leverage recent advancements in artiﬁcial intelligence to 1) create the ability to solve larger synthesis problems than previously possible, and 2) gain deeper design insights into engineering synthesis problems.

contexts.context: The paper evaluates multiple GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) for generating analog circuit topologies (case studies: frequency response and low-pass filter problems). The improved WGAN produced the most feasible topologies, with a reported 42.66% feasibility for the frequency-response case. Using the Wasserstein distance in the loss helped overcome instability and mode collapse. DCGAN underperformed, possibly due to lengthy CPU-only TensorFlow training; the authors note that GPU training or a deep convolutional WGAN (DC-WGAN) might help. Network architecture matters: four hidden layers caused instability and mode collapse, while reducing layers improved results; simpler fully-connected improved WGANs outperformed more complex DCGANs in their experiments. Representation issues were noted: fixed-size adjacency matrices can cause graph isomorphism problems that may mislabel feasible/infeasible designs. The workflow pairs GAN-generated topologies with sizing optimization (Figure 13) and active learning; ranking quality of generated candidates was similar according to normalized Kendall tau. The authors suggest future work including different generative models (VAEs, PixelCNN/RNN), DC-WGANs, and transfer to other topology synthesis problems.

snippets: The numerical results show that the improved WGAN can eﬃciently produce feasible circuit topologies in circuit synthesis.
the Wasserstein distance in the loss function overcame the instability and mode collapse issues.
Because all experiments were carried out using TensorFlow CPU implementations, DCGAN required a significant amount of time to complete the training.
even the improved WGANs with fully-connected layers (simpler network architecture) outperforms the DCGAN with convolutional layers (complicated network architecture).
it turned out that the GAN experienced instability and mode collapse with this architecture. Replacing two hidden layers improved performance
could be issues with graph isomorphisms11 because each generated circuit topologies used a ﬁxed-size adjacency matrix.
the improved WGAN achieved as high as 42.66% feasibility percentage for the frequency response problem; the isomorphism check would potentially increase metric P further.
We studied the eﬃciency of generating feasible samples using diﬀerent GAN models, including GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, and improved WGAN.
The numerical results indicate that the generator of the improved WGAN has a higher likelihood to generate feasible circuit topologies.

the GAN framework combined with active learning made predictions for generated circuit performance, and the resulting normalized Kendall tau distance indicates that the performance ranking is similar.
Once the circuit topology x is available, the circuit performance must be assessed via sizing optimization.
Figure 13 illustrates a possible workﬂow where the GAN framework can incorporated with sizing optimization.
The frequency response and low-pass ﬁlter problems, containing complete topological design data up to a certain number of components, were used as the case studies.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Find recent (2019-2025) peer-reviewed and arXiv academic publications on automated and generative schematic / circuit-topology synthesis and netlist generation for electronic design. Cover: (a) machine-learning and generative models for analog and mixed-signal circuit topology generation, (b) LLM-based and agentic generation of schematics, netlists, and circuit descriptions (e.g., LaMAGIC, AnalogCoder, Atelier/Artisan-style analog agents, schematic-from-spec systems), (c) graph-neural-network and reinforcement-learning approaches to circuit synthesis and device sizing, and (d) constraint-/specification-driven topology selection. For each paper give: full citation (authors, year, venue, volume, pages, DOI/arXiv id), a 3-5 sentence summary, and why it is relevant to building a code-based generative pipeline for the control/driver electronics of an open-source modular multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end). Note capabilities and limitations (validity of generated circuits, manufacturability, hallucination, evaluation). Aim for ~10-15 strong references.

# Automated and Generative Schematic/Circuit-Topology Synthesis and Netlist Generation for Electronic Design: A Literature Survey (2019–2025)

## Overview

This survey covers 15 key references spanning four methodological categories relevant to building a code-based generative pipeline for the control/driver electronics of an open-source modular multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller interfaces, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end amplifiers). The references are organized into: (a) machine-learning and generative models for analog/mixed-signal circuit topology generation, (b) LLM-based and agentic generation of schematics and netlists, (c) graph-neural-network (GNN) and reinforcement-learning (RL) approaches to circuit synthesis and device sizing, and (d) constraint-/specification-driven topology selection.

The following table provides a compact overview of all 15 references:

| Reference # | Authors (Year) | Title | Venue | Category (a/b/c/d) | Key Method | Key Results/Metrics | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chang et al. (2024) | LaMAGIC | arXiv:2407.18269 | a,b | SFT on Flan-T5 for analog topology generation using adjacency-matrix and float-input formulations | Up to 96% success at tolerance 0.01; transfer from 3-5 component pretraining to 6-component circuits; power-converter focus (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 5-6) | Small circuits only; synthetic data and simulated evaluation; limited component diversity (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5) |
| 2 | Chang et al. (2025) | LaMAGIC2 | arXiv:2506.10235 | a,b | SFCI representation reduces sequence length from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|) and improves numeric precision handling | 34% higher success than LaMAGIC under tight tolerance and 10x lower MSE; shorter token sequences and better transferability (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5) | Still focused on power-converter topology generation; not broadly shown on mixed-signal control electronics (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2) |
| 3 | Gao et al. (2025) | AnalogGenie | arXiv:2503.00205 | a | GPT-based pin-level graph generation with Eulerian-circuit encoding over 3,350 topologies across 11 circuit types | Discovers unseen and larger-scale topologies; reports zero-shot generation beyond training distribution; ~70x data augmentation via multiple serializations (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 15-19) | Requires a large curated dataset and custom representation pipeline; weak evidence for direct spec conditioning (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 1-2) |
| 4 | Dong et al. (2023) | CktGNN | arXiv:2308.16406 | a,c | Two-level nested GNN with subgraph basis plus VAE latent generation; Open Circuit Benchmark with 10K op-amps | ~91.9% valid-circuit rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson r≈0.806; outperforms strong GNN baselines and reported human designs (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 9-11) | Benchmark is op-amp-centric; uses idealized graph abstractions and component models, limiting manufacturable transistor-level realism (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 2-4) |
| 5 | Lai et al. (2025) | AnalogCoder | arXiv:2405.14918 | b | Training-free LLM agent generating PySpice code with domain prompts, feedback loop, and circuit tool library | Solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, beating GPT-4o; checks requirements, simulation, operating point, DC sweep, and function (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28) | Struggles on complex circuits; prioritizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; still needs external simulation to catch invalid outputs (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 9-11, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13) |
| 6 | Zhang et al. (2025) | AnalogXpert | ISEDA 2025, pp. 772-777; arXiv:2412.19824 | b,d | Training-free LLM agent with subcircuit library, CoT prompting, in-context examples, iterative proofreading, and SPICE output | 40% synthetic success and 23% real-data success versus GPT-4o at 3% and 3%; more practical SPICE-level output than idealized graph methods (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 1-2, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10) | Small real dataset; connection mistakes and wrong block choices still common; needs richer real-data corpora or fine-tuned local models (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10) |
| 7 | Vijayaraghavan et al. (2024) | CircuitSynth | IEEE LAD 2024, pp. 1-6 | b | Two-phase pipeline: LLM netlist generation plus RoBERTa validity classifier; Gumbel-softmax refinement toward valid circuits | Uses 862,606-example power-converter dataset; validity classifier F1≈92%; SPICE-validated validity about 0.713-0.728 for best variants, i.e. ~72% (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 2-3, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 3-4) | Restricted to power converters and validity-centric evaluation; no explicit broad spec-conditioned AMS block generation (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6) |
| 8 | Chen et al. (2025) | MenTeR | IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124-132 | b,d | Multi-agent workflow with PI, Circuit, and Testbench agents; DA-RAG, Chain-of-Stage reasoning, execution checks, iterative refinement | GPT-4o backbone reached 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5; supports end-to-end netlist and testbench generation (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5) | Human-in-the-loop still needed for complex systems; subcircuit-definition and missing-wire errors remain at system level (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8) |
| 9 | Wang et al. (2025) | AnalogSAGE | arXiv:2512.22435 | b,c,d | Self-evolving multi-agent workflow with topology selection, refinement, parameter optimization, stratified memory, RAG, and Bayesian optimization grounded by simulation | Reported 10x overall pass-rate improvement, 48x Pass@1 improvement, and 4x smaller parameter search space on SKY130 op-amp tasks (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 4-5) | Limited to op-amp designs and SKY130 environment; late 2025 arXiv work with little independent validation so far (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2) |
| 10 | Bhandari et al. (2024) | Masala-CHAI | arXiv:2411.14299 | b | Automated schematic-to-SPICE dataset creation from textbook diagrams using YOLOv8, net extraction, prompt tuning, and LLM verification | Fine-tuning on Masala-CHAI improves AnalogCoder Pass@1 by 46%; ~7,500 netlists with GED-based structural verification (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 7-9) | Component confusion such as NMOS versus PMOS and weak transistor-level net detection remain major issues; net detection can be ~45% accurate without stronger CV support (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, tao2024amsnetnetlistdataset pages 1-2) |
| 11 | Settaluri et al. (2020) | AutoCkt | arXiv:2001.01808 | c | PPO-based deep RL for analog sizing across multiple target specs with transfer from schematic to post-layout environments | 96.3% target convergence on unseen specs, ~40x faster than genetic algorithms, and 40 LVS-passed op-amps in layout-aware experiments (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6) | Fixed-topology sizing only; requires simulator access and substantial environment engineering; not a topology generator (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6) |
| 12 | Zhao and Zhang (2022) | DRL Circuit Structure Synthesis | DATE 2022, pp. 1157-1160 | c,d | DRL topology synthesis from predefined building blocks with policy-gradient network, hash-table caching, and symbolic analysis filtering | Early RL topology-synthesis framework; uses GPDD symbolic filtering and SPICE-based NSGA-II verification; explores novel structures with lower computation than older methods (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4) | Constrained by building-block library and state encoding; stochastic results; runs can take 2-9 hours and still need downstream sizing (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-2) |
| 13 | Choi et al. (2024) | MA-Opt | IEEE TCAS-I 71(5):2045-2056 | c | Multi-actor RL for analog sizing with shared elite set, critic surrogate, and cooperative near-sampling | Up to 34% better FoM and target metrics than DNN-Opt at equal simulation budget; improves exploration and sample efficiency (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2) | Sizing only, not topology synthesis; depends on an already valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2) |
| 14 | Poddar et al. (2024) | Data-Driven Synthesizer | DATE 2024, pp. 1-6 | a,d | Modular parameterized topology library, VAE synthetic data augmentation, binary-search and synthetic ranking, plus DNN-Opt verification and sizing | Evaluated on 360+ OPAMP topologies and 540K+ data points; generates optimal-sized topology netlists in about 10 minutes; synthetic data helps when real samples are sparse (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 5-6, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4) | Requires significant one-time simulation and data collection; limited to OPAMP and OTA families in curated libraries (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4) |
| 15 | Guo et al. (2019) | GAN Circuit Synthesis | AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum | a | GAN, WGAN, and improved WGAN generation of circuit-topology adjacency encodings for frequency-response and low-pass-filter synthesis | Improved WGAN reached up to 42.66% feasibility in the frequency-response case and beat GAN, DCGAN, and WGAN baselines (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17) | Feasibility remains relatively low; graph-isomorphism and representation issues persist; no spec-conditioned or manufacturability-aware generation loop (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 2-4, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17) |


*Table: This table summarizes 15 influential 2019-2025 references on automated and generative circuit-topology synthesis, netlist generation, and analog design agents. It is useful for comparing methods, results, and practical limitations when building a code-based electronics design pipeline.*

---

## Detailed Reference Summaries

### Category (a): ML and Generative Models for Analog Circuit Topology Generation

**1. Chang, C.-C., Shen, Y., Fan, S., Li, J., Zhang, S., Cao, N., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2024). "LaMAGIC: Language-Model-based Topology Generation for Analog Integrated Circuits." arXiv:2407.18269.**

LaMAGIC pioneers the use of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a Flan-T5 (248M parameters) encoder-decoder model to generate analog circuit topologies from user-specified performance targets (e.g., voltage conversion ratio, efficiency) in a single forward pass (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 2-3). The system represents circuits as graphs encoded via adjacency matrices and develops multiple input/output formulations — including a float-input adjacency-matrix formulation (FM) — that align with the autoregressive nature of language models. Experimental results on power converter circuits demonstrate a success rate of up to 96% under a strict tolerance of 0.01 (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2). The model is pretrained on 3–5 component circuits and transferred to 6-component designs using a dataset of 76K sampled circuits (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5). **Relevance to dosing device:** Demonstrates that specification-to-topology generation is feasible via LMs, a paradigm applicable to generating power converter sub-circuits (e.g., stepper driver power stages). **Limitations:** Restricted to small power converter topologies (3–6 passive/switching components); synthetic evaluation only; no direct applicability to mixed-signal blocks like load-cell amplifiers (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 9-10, chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 4-5).

**2. Chang, C.-C., Lin, W.-H., Shen, Y., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2025). "LaMAGIC2: Advanced Circuit Formulations for Language Model-Based Analog Topology Generation." arXiv:2506.10235.**

LaMAGIC2 introduces the Succinct Float-Input Canonical Formulation with Identifier (SFCI), which reduces token-length complexity from O(|V|²) to O(|V|) and adds component-type identifiers for better differentiation (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2). This yields 34% higher success rates under tight tolerance (0.01) and 10× lower MSE compared to the original LaMAGIC, with substantially shorter output sequences (~36–41 tokens vs. ~85–105) (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5). **Relevance:** Improved scalability and precision make this formulation more practical for generating larger circuit blocks. **Limitations:** Still confined to power converter topologies; O(|V|) scaling, while improved, has not been demonstrated on complex mixed-signal circuits (chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 1-2, chang2025lamagic2advancedcircuit pages 3-5).

**3. Gao, J., Cao, W., Yang, J., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogGenie: A Generative Engine for Automatic Discovery of Analog Circuit Topologies." arXiv:2503.00205.**

AnalogGenie is a GPT-based generative engine that represents circuits at the device-pin level using an Eulerian-circuit encoding of directed graphs, trained on a dataset of 3,350 distinct topologies across 11 circuit families (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4). The pin-level representation captures low-level design details and scales to larger graphs better than adjacency-matrix methods. Data augmentation via multiple Eulerian serializations per topology yields ~70× more training data (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 5-7). AnalogGenie demonstrates zero-shot generation of circuit types not present in training (e.g., transconductance amplifiers) and produces novel topologies validated in Cadence Virtuoso, including op-amps (GBW=12 MHz), DC converters (efficiency=0.95), and bandgap references (TC=3 ppm/°C) (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 15-19). **Relevance:** The ability to discover novel topologies across diverse circuit families (including amplifiers and references) is directly applicable to generating load-cell front-end and voltage reference sub-circuits. **Limitations:** Requires a large curated dataset; specification conditioning is indirect (gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 2-4, gao2503analoggenieagenerative pages 1-2).

**4. Guo, T., Herber, D. R., and Allison, J. T. (2019). "Circuit Synthesis Using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)." AIAA Scitech 2019 Forum. DOI: 10.2514/6.2019-2350.**

This early work applies GAN variants (GAN, DCGAN, WGAN, improved WGAN) to generate circuit topologies encoded as adjacency matrices. Using datasets of 43K–123K enumerated topologies for frequency-response matching and low-pass filter realizability, the improved WGAN achieved the highest feasibility rate of up to 42.66% after 50,000 training iterations (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 11-14, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17). **Relevance:** Establishes the foundational concept of using generative models for circuit topology search, relevant to exploring filter and signal-conditioning topologies. **Limitations:** Relatively low feasibility rates; graph isomorphism problems in fixed-size adjacency representations; no specification conditioning or manufacturability awareness (guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 2-4, guo2019circuitsynthesisusing pages 14-17).

**5. Poddar, S., Budak, A., Zhao, L., Hsu, C.-H., Maji, S., Zhu, K., Jia, Y., and Pan, D. Z. (2024). "A Data-Driven Analog Circuit Synthesizer with Automatic Topology Selection and Sizing." DATE 2024, pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.23919/DATE58400.2024.10546840.**

This work presents a modular, data-driven framework that combines a parameterized OPAMP topology library (~4,000 valid topologies, ~360 curated), VAE-based synthetic data augmentation, and DNN-Opt sizing to automatically select and size topologies from performance specifications (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4). The system generates optimal-sized netlists in approximately 10 minutes using over 540K data points (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 5-6). **Relevance:** The modular library approach and specification-driven selection are directly applicable to choosing amplifier topologies for load-cell signal conditioning. **Limitations:** Requires a costly one-time data-collection effort; limited to OPAMP/OTA families (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2, poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 3-4).

---

### Category (b): LLM-Based and Agentic Generation of Schematics and Netlists

**6. Lai, Y., Lee, S., Chen, G., Poddar, S., Hu, M., Pan, D. Z., and Luo, P. (2025). "AnalogCoder: Analog Circuit Design via Training-Free Code Generation." arXiv:2405.14918.**

AnalogCoder is the first training-free LLM agent that reframes analog circuit design as Python (PySpice) code generation (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). It uses domain-specific prompts, a four-stage feedback loop (requirement checks, simulation/operating-point checks, DC sweep checks, functional checks), and a circuit tool library that archives successful designs as reusable modular sub-circuits (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). AnalogCoder solved 20 of 24 benchmark tasks, outperforming standard GPT-4o (15 solved) and Llama-3 (11 solved) (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 2-4). **Relevance:** The PySpice code-generation paradigm and modular sub-circuit library are directly transferable to generating driver electronics: one could encode stepper-driver half-bridges, motor H-bridges, or instrumentation amplifier blocks as library components and compose them via LLM-generated code. **Limitations:** Struggles with highly complex circuits; emphasizes functional correctness over parameter optimization; requires external SPICE simulation for validation; a later evaluation (AnalogCoder-Pro) showed only 10% pass rate on harder specification-driven benchmarks (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 9-11, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 6-7).

**7. Zhang, H., Sun, S., Lin, Y., Wang, R., and Bian, J. (2025). "AnalogXpert: Automating Analog Topology Synthesis by Incorporating Circuit Design Expertise into Large Language Models." ISEDA 2025, pp. 772–777. arXiv:2412.19824.**

AnalogXpert formulates topology synthesis as subcircuit-level SPICE code generation and incorporates circuit design expertise through a subcircuit library, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, in-context learning examples, and iterative human-experience-based proofreading (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 1-2). On a benchmark of 30 real and 2,000 synthetic tasks, AnalogXpert achieved 40% and 23% success rates respectively, markedly outperforming GPT-4o at 3%/3% (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 2-4, zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10). **Relevance:** The SPICE-level output and subcircuit decomposition align well with practical PCB-level design (e.g., decomposing a motor driver into gate driver, half-bridge, current sense subcircuits). **Limitations:** Connection errors and wrong block selections remain common failure modes; the real dataset is small (30 tasks) (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10).

**8. Vijayaraghavan, P., Shi, L., Degan, E., and Zhang, X. (2024). "CircuitSynth: Leveraging Large Language Models for Circuit Topology Synthesis." IEEE LLM Aided Design Workshop (LAD), pp. 1–6. DOI: 10.1109/LAD62341.2024.10691716.**

CircuitSynth introduces a two-phase pipeline: Phase 1 fine-tunes an LLM to generate netlist-encoded topologies autoregressively, and Phase 2 applies a RoBERTa-based validity classifier (F1 ≈ 92%) with Gumbel-softmax refinement to steer generation toward valid circuits (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 2-3). Trained on 862,606 power-converter examples, the best models achieved ~71–73% SPICE-validated topology validity (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 3-4, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6). **Relevance:** The validity-classifier refinement concept could be adapted to filter LLM-generated netlists for driver electronics against electrical rule checks. **Limitations:** Restricted to power converters; no specification-conditioned generation (vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 1-2, vijayaraghavan2024circuitsynthleveraginglarge pages 5-6).

**9. Chen, P.-H., Lin, Y.-S., Lee, W., Leu, T.-Y., Hsu, P.-H., Dissanayake, A., Oh, S., and Chiu, C.-S. (2025). "MenTeR: A Fully-Automated Multi-Agent Workflow for End-to-End RF/Analog Circuits Netlist Design." IEEE ICLAD 2025, pp. 124–132. arXiv:2505.22990.**

MenTeR deploys a multi-agent architecture comprising a Primary Investigator (PI) agent for specification understanding, a Circuit Agent for netlist synthesis via Chain-of-Stage (CoS) reasoning, and a Testbench Agent for simulation validation using Diagram-Aware RAG (DA-RAG) (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 4-5). With GPT-4o as backbone, MenTeR achieved 84.2% Pass@1 and 89.2% Pass@5, supporting end-to-end design from specification to validated PySpice netlist and testbench (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8). **Relevance:** The multi-agent spec-to-netlist-to-testbench pipeline is the closest paradigm to what would be needed for an automated dosing-device electronics generator — one agent interprets requirements (e.g., "4 stepper channels, 24V, load-cell excitation"), another generates netlists, and a third validates via simulation. **Limitations:** AI-generated nets can contain subcircuit definition errors and missing wires at system level, necessitating human-in-the-loop review for complex designs (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8).

**10. Wang, Z., Gao, J., Fu, W., Guo, X., and Zhang, X. (2025). "AnalogSAGE: Self-Evolving Analog Design Multi-Agents with Stratified Memory and Grounded Experience." arXiv:2512.22435.**

AnalogSAGE coordinates three sequential agent stages — topology selection, topology refinement, and parameter optimization — within a closed-loop, simulation-grounded workflow augmented by four-layer stratified memory for long-horizon reasoning and cross-task knowledge transfer (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 2-3, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2). Using SKY130 PDK and ngspice, AnalogSAGE reports a 10× overall pass-rate improvement and 48× Pass@1 improvement over baselines, with Bayesian optimization for parameter tuning (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 4-5). **Relevance:** The self-evolving, simulation-grounded approach with memory-based learning could enable iterative improvement of driver circuit designs across multiple dosing channels. **Limitations:** Currently demonstrated only on op-amp designs in SKY130; recent preprint with limited independent validation (wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2).

**11. Bhandari, J., Bhat, V., He, Y., Garg, S., Rahmani, H., and Karri, R. (2024). "Masala-CHAI: A Large-Scale SPICE Netlist Dataset for Analog Circuits by Harnessing AI." arXiv:2411.14299.**

Masala-CHAI provides an automated pipeline for extracting SPICE netlists from circuit schematic images in textbooks, using YOLOv8 for component detection and deep Hough-transform priors for net recognition, followed by LLM-based netlist generation and verification (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 3-4). The open-sourced dataset of ~7,500 netlists from 10 textbooks enables LLM fine-tuning; models fine-tuned on Masala-CHAI improved AnalogCoder's Pass@1 by 46% (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 1-2, bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3). Structural correctness is verified via Graph Edit Distance (GED), achieving 100% similarity with AMSNet reference netlists (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 7-9). **Relevance:** The dataset and pipeline could be extended to extract and catalog common control electronics circuits (H-bridges, stepper drivers, instrumentation amplifiers) from open-source hardware documentation, creating training data for a dosing-device-specific generator. **Limitations:** Component confusion (NMOS vs. PMOS) and weak transistor-level net detection (~45% accuracy without CV support) remain significant challenges (bhandari2024masalachaialargescale pages 2-3, tao2024amsnetnetlistdataset pages 1-2).

---

### Category (c): GNN and RL Approaches to Circuit Synthesis and Device Sizing

**12. Dong, Z., Cao, W., Zhang, M., Tao, D., Chen, Y., and Zhang, X. (2023). "CktGNN: Circuit Graph Neural Network for Electronic Design Automation." arXiv:2308.16406.**

CktGNN uses a two-level (nested) GNN architecture where inner GNNs encode subgraph-basis components and an outer directed GNN performs message passing over the transformed circuit DAG (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 1-2, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 4-6). Integrated into a VAE framework, CktGNN jointly addresses topology generation and device sizing. The authors release the Open Circuit Benchmark (OCB) with 10K distinct operational amplifiers. In experiments, CktGNN achieves ~91.9% valid-circuit generation rate, ~96.3% novelty, FoM Pearson correlation r ≈ 0.806, and outperforms human expert designs in Bayesian-optimization-guided search (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 9-11). **Relevance:** The GNN+VAE approach to joint topology-and-sizing optimization is applicable to amplifier blocks in a dosing device (e.g., load-cell instrumentation amplifiers, analog front-ends). The OCB benchmark and open-source code support reproducible research. **Limitations:** Benchmark is op-amp-centric with idealized component models; not directly demonstrated on mixed-signal driver circuits (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 6-8, dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 2-4).

**13. Settaluri, K., Haj-Ali, A., Huang, Q., Hakhamaneshi, K., and Nikolic, B. (2020). "AutoCkt: Deep Reinforcement Learning of Analog Circuit Designs." arXiv:2001.01808.**

AutoCkt formulates analog circuit sizing as a multi-objective RL problem using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), where the agent observes simulated performance and target specifications and iteratively adjusts device parameters (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 2-3). Trained on only 50 target specifications, AutoCkt generalizes to 96.3% of 1,000 unseen targets with an average of 27 simulations to converge — approximately 40× faster than genetic algorithms (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5). Transfer learning from schematic to post-layout (PEX) environments produced 40 LVS-passed operational amplifier layouts in TSMC 16nm (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 6-6, settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6). **Relevance:** Once a topology is selected for a dosing-device sub-circuit (e.g., a transimpedance amplifier for load-cell readout), AutoCkt-style RL sizing can rapidly optimize component values to meet gain, bandwidth, and noise specifications. **Limitations:** Fixed-topology sizing only; requires access to a SPICE simulator and environment engineering; not a topology generator (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2).

**14. Zhao, Z. and Zhang, L. (2022). "Deep Reinforcement Learning for Analog Circuit Structure Synthesis." DATE 2022, pp. 1157–1160. DOI: 10.23919/DATE54114.2022.9774699.**

This work is among the first to apply deep RL directly to analog circuit topology synthesis (rather than just sizing). A policy-gradient neural network (PGNN) incrementally constructs topologies by selecting building blocks from a predefined library, with hash tables and GPDD symbolic analysis used to efficiently filter and evaluate candidates before SPICE-based NSGA-II verification (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-1, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4). The method requires substantially less computation than brute-force enumeration approaches like MOJITO (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4). **Relevance:** The building-block-based topology construction paradigm could be adapted for driver electronics by defining blocks such as half-bridges, gate drivers, current mirrors, and feedback networks. **Limitations:** Constrained by the predefined building-block library; stochastic results requiring multiple runs (2–9 hours each); downstream sizing still needed (zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 3-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 4-4, zhao2022deepreinforcementlearning pages 1-2).

**15. Choi, Y., Park, S., Choi, M., Lee, K., and Kang, S. (2024). "MA-Opt: Reinforcement Learning-Based Analog Circuit Optimization Using Multi-Actors." IEEE TCAS-I, 71(5):2045–2056. DOI: 10.1109/TCSI.2024.3356582.**

MA-Opt uses multiple parallel RL actors with a shared elite-solution memory and a cooperative near-sampling strategy to optimize analog circuit device parameters. A critic network serves as a surrogate simulator to reduce expensive SPICE calls (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). MA-Opt achieves up to 34% better Figure-of-Merit than DNN-Opt at the same simulation budget and consistently satisfies all given performance constraints (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). **Relevance:** Multi-actor RL sizing is applicable to optimizing component values in motor driver and signal-conditioning circuits where multiple performance targets (efficiency, thermal, bandwidth) must be simultaneously satisfied. **Limitations:** Sizing-only; requires a valid starting topology and simulation-based supervision (choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2).

---

## Cross-Cutting Analysis: Capabilities, Limitations, and Relevance to a Dosing-Device Electronics Pipeline

### Capabilities
The surveyed methods collectively demonstrate that: (1) LM-based approaches can achieve high success rates (up to 96%) for specification-conditioned topology generation of well-defined circuit families (chang2024lamagiclanguagemodelbasedtopology pages 1-2); (2) training-free LLM agents like AnalogCoder and MenTeR can generate functional PySpice/SPICE netlists with multi-stage feedback verification, solving 20+/24 benchmark tasks (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2, chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8); (3) GNN+VAE methods enable joint topology-sizing optimization with >91% validity rates (dong2023cktgnncircuitgraph pages 18-20); and (4) RL-based sizing converges to meet specifications on 96%+ of unseen targets with 40× speedup over genetic algorithms (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 3-5).

### Hallucination and Validity
A recurring challenge across LLM-based methods is the generation of structurally invalid or electrically incorrect circuits. AnalogCoder requires multi-stage simulation-based feedback to catch floating nodes and incorrect operating points (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 26-28, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13). MenTeR found that AI-generated netlists sometimes passed automated benchmarks yet contained errors upon schematic inspection, including incorrectly defined subcircuits and missing wire connections (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 7-8). AnalogXpert reports persistent connection errors and wrong block selections even with iterative proofreading (zhang2025analogxpertautomatinganalog pages 9-10). These findings underscore the necessity of SPICE-level simulation verification in any generative pipeline.

### Manufacturability
Most methods operate at the schematic level and do not address layout parasitics, PCB-level constraints, or component availability (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 11-12, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13). AutoCkt is a notable exception, demonstrating transfer to post-layout (PEX) environments with LVS-passed designs (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 5-6). For a dosing device using discrete, off-the-shelf components (DRV8825 stepper drivers, HX711 load-cell ADCs, etc.), the gap between schematic-level generation and a manufacturable BOM/PCB is substantial — generated topologies would need to be mapped to specific component footprints, validated against datasheets, and checked for thermal and EMC compliance.

### Relevance to Dosing-Device Control Electronics
The control electronics for a multi-powder dosing device (microcontroller, stepper drivers, motor drivers, load-cell front-end) represent a system-level design challenge that spans digital control logic and multiple analog/mixed-signal sub-circuits. The most directly applicable paradigms are: (1) **Multi-agent agentic workflows** (MenTeR, AnalogSAGE) that decompose a system specification into sub-blocks and generate, validate, and iterate on each block's netlist (chen2025menterafullyautomated pages 2-4, wang2512analogsageselfevolvinganalog pages 1-2); (2) **Code-generation agents** (AnalogCoder) that produce executable PySpice code from natural-language descriptions, enabling composition of modular sub-circuits such as H-bridges and instrumentation amplifiers (lai2025analogcoderanalogcircuit pages 1-2); (3) **Specification-driven topology selectors** (Poddar et al.) that rank candidate topologies from a parameterized library against target specifications and automatically size them (poddar2024adatadrivenanalog pages 1-2); and (4) **RL-based optimizers** (AutoCkt, MA-Opt) for downstream component-value tuning once topologies are selected (settaluri2020autocktdeepreinforcement pages 1-2, choi2024maoptreinforcementlearningbased pages 1-2). A practical pipeline would likely combine these approaches: an LLM agent interprets system requirements and selects/generates topology candidates, a GNN or RL module optimizes device parameters, and simulation-in-the-loop verification catches hallucinated or invalid designs before output.

### Key Gaps
No surveyed work has demonstrated end-to-end generation of complete, manufacturable PCB-level control electronics from specifications. Current methods are largely validated on IC-level analog circuits (op-amps, power converters, bandgap references) rather than board-level mixed-signal systems with discrete components. Bridging this gap — from transistor-level IC topology to board-level system design with real component libraries — remains an open challenge for the field (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 11-12, fang2025asurveyof pages 48-50).
